We first recall the problem with directly employing the definitions of such functions on the lattice, using the example of unpolarized PDFs. The unpolarized PDF, denoted here q‚Äč(x)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•q(x), is defined on the light cone: q‚Äč(x)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě+‚ąěd‚Äčőĺ‚ąí4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčx‚ÄčP+‚Äčőĺ‚ąí‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí)‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčW‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚúČ4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúČquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚúČsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎäsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉq(x)=int-infty+inftyfracdxi-4pie-ixP+xi- P|overlinepsi(xi-)gamma+W(xi-,0)psi|P, where |P‚ü©ketūĚĎÉ|P is the hadron state with momentum PőľsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚúáPmu, in the standard relativistic normalization2In the remainder of the paper, the standard relativistic normalization is always assumed and the states are labeled with the hadron momentum and other labels, if necessary. , the light-cone vectors are taken as v¬Ī=(v0¬Īv3)/2superscriptūĚĎ£plus-or-minusplus-or-minussuperscriptūĚĎ£0superscriptūĚĎ£32vpm=(v0pm v3)/sqrt2, W‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)=e‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0őĺ‚ąíūĚĎĎő∑‚ąí‚ÄčA+‚Äč(ő∑‚ąí)ūĚĎäsuperscriptūĚúČ0superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0superscriptūĚúČdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚúāsuperscriptūĚźīsuperscriptūĚúāW(xi-,0)=e-igint0xi-deta-A+(eta-) is the Wilson line connecting the light-cone points 00 and őĺ‚ąísuperscriptūĚúČxi-, while the factorization scale is kept implicit. Such light-cone correlations are not accessible on a Euclidean spacetime, because the light cone directions shrink to one point at the origin. As discussed in the Introduction, this fact prevented lattice extraction of PDFs for many years, apart from their low moments, reachable via local matrix elements and the operator product expansion (OPE). However, since the number of moments that can be reliably calculated is strongly limited, alternative approaches were sought for to yield the full Bjorken-xūĚĎ•x dependence. The common feature of all the approaches is that they rely to some extent on the factorization framework. For a lattice observable Q‚Äč(x,őľR)ūĚĎĄūĚĎ•subscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖQ(x,muR) that is to be used to extract PDFs, one can generically write: 3For simplicity, we neglect possible mixings under factorization. Q‚Äč(x,őľR)=‚ąę‚ąí11d‚Äčyy‚ÄčC‚Äč(xy,őľF,őľR)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľF),ūĚĎĄūĚĎ•subscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖsuperscriptsubscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚúáūĚźĻsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖūĚĎěūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚúáūĚźĻQ(x,muR)=int-11fracdyy,Cleft(fracxy,muF,muRright)q(y,muF), where C‚Äč(x/y,őľF,őľR)ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚúáūĚźĻsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖC(x/y,muF,muR) is a perturbatively computable function and q‚Äč(y,őľF2)ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚźĻ2q(y,muF2) is the desired PDF. In the above expression we distinguish between the factorization scale, őľFsubscriptūĚúáūĚźĻmuF, and the renormalization scale, őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR. These scales are usually taken to be the same and, hence, from now on we will adopt this choice and take őľF=őľR‚Č°őľsubscriptūĚúáūĚźĻsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖūĚúámuF=muRequivmu. Lattice approaches use different observables QūĚĎĄQ that fall into two classes: 1. observables that are generalizations of light-cone functions such that they can be accessed on the lattice; such generalized functions have direct xūĚĎ•x-dependence, but xūĚĎ•x does not have the same partonic interpretation as the Bjorken-xūĚĎ•x, 2. observables in terms of which hadronic tensor can be written; the hadronic tensor is then decomposed into structure functions like F1subscriptūĚźĻ1F1 and g1subscriptūĚĎĒ1g1, which are factorizable into PDFs. Below, we provide the general idea for several proposals that were introduced in recent years.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Hadronic tenso: All the information about a DIS cross section is contained in the hadronic tensor citebib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, defined by Wőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(p,q,őĽ,őĽ‚Ä≤)=14‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč‚ąęd4‚Äčx‚Äčei‚Äčq‚Äčx‚Äč‚ü®p,őĽ‚Ä≤|[Jőľ‚Äč(x),JőĹ‚Äč]|p,őĽ‚ü©,subscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąūĚĎĚūĚĎěūĚúÜsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤14ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎĎ4ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎ•quantum-operator-productūĚĎĚsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤subscriptūĚźĹūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚźĹūĚúą0ūĚĎĚūĚúÜWmunu(p,q,lambda,lambdaprime)=frac14piint,d4xeiqx p,lambdaprime|[Jmu(x),Jnu]|p,lambda, where |p,őĽ‚ü©ketūĚĎĚūĚúÜ|p,lambda is the hadron state labeled by its momentum pūĚĎĚp and polarization őĽūĚúÜlambda, qūĚĎěq ‚Äď virtual photon momentum, Jőľ‚Äč(x)subscriptūĚźĹūĚúáūĚĎ•Jmu(x) ‚Äď electromagnetic current at point xūĚĎ•x. The hadronic tensor can be related to DIS structure functions and hence, in principle, PDFs can be extracted from it. Wőľ‚ÄčőĹsubscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąWmunu is the imaginary part of the forward Compton amplitude and can be written as the current-current correlation function, Wőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(p,q)=‚ü®p,őĽ‚Ä≤|‚ąęd4‚Äčx4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčq‚Äčx‚ÄčJőľ‚Äč(x)‚ÄčJőĹ‚Äč|p,őĽ‚ü©őĽ‚ÄčőĽ‚Ä≤¬Į,subscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąūĚĎĚūĚĎěsubscriptquantum-operator-productūĚĎĚsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎĎ4ūĚĎ•4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscriptūĚźĹūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚźĹūĚúą0ūĚĎĚūĚúÜ¬ĮūĚúÜsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤Wmunu(p,q)= p,lambdaprime|intfracd4x4pieiqxJmu(x)Jnu|p,lambdaoverlinelambdalambdaprime, where the subscript őĽ‚ÄčőĽ‚Ä≤¬Į¬ĮūĚúÜsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤overlinelambdalambdaprime denotes averaging over polarizations. The approach has been introduced as a possible way of investigating hadronic structure on the lattice by K. -F. Liu and S. -J. Dong already in 1993. They also proposed a decomposition of the contributions to the hadronic tensor according to different topologies of the quark paths, into valence and connected or disconnected sea ones. In this way, they addressed the origin of Gottfried sum rule violation. A crucial aspect for the implementation in Lattice QCD is the fact that the hadronic tensor Wőľ‚ÄčőĹsubscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąWmunu, defined in Minkowski spacetime, can be obtained from the Euclidean path-integral formalism citebib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, by considering ratios of suitable four-point and two-point functions. In the limit of the points being sufficiently away from both the source and the sink, where the hadron is created or annihilated, the matrix element receives contributions from only the ground state of the hadron. Reconstructing the Minkowski tensor from its Euclidean counterpart is formally defined by an inverse Laplace transform of the latter and can, in practice, be carried out using e. g. the maximum entropy method or the Backus-Gilbert method. Nevertheless, this aspect is highly non-trivial and improvements thereof are looked for. As pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib59, a significant role may be played by power-law finite volume effects related to the matrix elements being defined in Euclidean spacetime. A similar phenomenon was recently observed also in the context of K‚ąíK¬ĮūĚźĺ¬ĮūĚźĺK-barK mixing citebib.bib60. Another difficulty of the hadronic tensor approach on the lattice is the necessity to calculate four-point correlation functions, which is computationally more intensive than for three-point functions, the standard tools of hadron structure investigations on the lattice. However, the theoretical appeal of the hadronic tensor approach recently sparked renewed interest in it citebib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63. We describe some exploratory results in Sec. .

W‚Äč(0,x)ūĚĎä0ūĚĎ•W(0,x) is the Wilson line that ensures gauge invariance of the matrix element. The essence of the idea is to ‚Äúobserve‚ÄĚ and study on the lattice the partonic constituents of hadrons instead of the hadrons themselves citebib.bib65, bib.bib66. As shown in Ref. citebib.bib64, the pion LCWF can be extracted by considering a vacuum-to-pion expectation value of the axial vector current with quark fields separated in spacetime. Gauge invariance is ensured by a scalar quark propagator with color quantum numbers of a quark, and at a large momentum transfer. The relation between the Fourier transform of this matrix element, computed on the lattice, and the pion LCWF, ő¶ŌÄsubscriptő¶ūĚúčPhipi, is given by the following formula: Főľ‚Äč(pŌÄ‚Üí,q‚Üí;t,tŌÄ)superscriptūĚźĻūĚúá‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúč‚ÜíūĚĎěūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúčdisplaystyle Fmu(vecppi,vecq;t,tpi) ‚Č°displaystyleequiv ‚ąęd3‚ÄčxŌÄ‚Äčd3‚Äčxt‚Äče‚ąíi‚ÄčpŌÄ‚Üí‚čÖxŌÄ‚Üí‚ąíi‚Äčq‚Üí‚čÖxt‚Üí‚ÄčeEŌÄ‚Äč(tŌÄ‚ąít)‚Äč‚ü®ŌÄ‚Äč(pŌÄ)|u‚Äč(xt‚Üí,t)‚ÄčS‚Äč(xt‚Üí,t;0)‚Äčő≥őľ‚Äčő≥5‚Äčd¬Į‚Äč|0‚ü©superscriptūĚĎĎ3subscriptūĚĎ•ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎĎ3subscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúč‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚúč‚čÖūĚĎĖ‚ÜíūĚĎě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚźłūĚúčsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúčūĚĎ°quantum-operator-productūĚúčsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúčūĚĎĘ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°ūĚĎ°ūĚĎÜ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°ūĚĎ°0subscriptūĚõĺūĚúásubscriptūĚõĺ5¬ĮūĚĎĎ00displaystyleint d3xpid3xte-ivecppicdotvecxpi-ivecqcdotvecxteEpi(tpi-t)pi(ppi)|u(vecxt,t)S(vecxt,t;0)gammamugamma5bard|0 where q‚Üí‚ÜíūĚĎěvecq ‚Äď momentum transfer, S‚Äč(xt‚Üí,t;0)ūĚĎÜ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°ūĚĎ°0S(vecxt,t;0) ‚Äď scalar colored propagator, uisubscriptūĚĎĘūĚĎĖui ‚Äď discrete set of partonic momentum fractions (allowed by the discretized momenta in a finite volume). The spacetime points are explained in Fig. , which shows the three-point function that needs to be computed. The interval tŌÄ‚ąítsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúčūĚĎ°tpi-t needs to be large to have an on-shell pion. To extract the LCWF, several conditions need to be satisfied: injected pion momentum needs to be large (to have a ‚Äúfrozen‚ÄĚ pion and see its partonic constituents), the scalar quark needs to carry large energy, the time tūĚĎ°t (time of momentum transfer and ‚Äútransformation‚ÄĚ of a quark to a scalar quark) has to be small (to prevent quantum decoherence and hadronization) and the lattice volume large enough (to minimize effects of discretizing parton momenta). We refer to the original papers for an extensive discussion of these conditions. An exploratory study of the approach was presented in Refs. citebib.bib65, bib.bib66 and later in Ref. citebib.bib67, both in the quenched approximation. Naturally, the conditions outlined above are very difficult to satisfy simultaneously on the lattice, due to restrictions from the finite lattice spacing and the finite volume. However, the knowledge of the full hadronic wave function from first principles would be very much desired and further exploration of this approach may be interesting. In particular, integrals of hadronic wave functions over transverse momenta yield distribution amplitudes and PDFs.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Auxiliary heav: In 2005, another method was proposed citebib.bib58 to access hadron structure on the lattice, including PDFs. The idea relies on simulating on the lattice the Compton scattering tensor, using currents that couple physical light quarks of a hadron with a purely valence fictitious heavy quark. In the continuum limit, one can extract matrix elements of local operators in the OPE in the same renormalization scheme in which the Wilson coefficients are calculated. In this way, one gets the moments of PDFs. The crucial difference with respect to standard lattice calculations of moments is that the approach removes power divergent mixings with lower-dimensional operators, unavoidable in the lattice formulation for fourth and higher moments due to the breaking of the rotational invariance into the discrete hypercubic subgroup H‚ÄčūĚźĽ4H. This is derived and discussed in detail in Ref. citebib.bib58. Thus, in principle, any PDF moment can be extracted and the whole PDF can be reconstructed. Moreover, the heavy fictitious quark suppresses long-range correlations between the currents and also removes many higher-twist contaminations (twist=dimension‚ąí-spin). The results are independent of the mass of the heavy quark, mő®subscriptūĚĎöő®mPsi, as long as it satisfies a lattice window restriction, i. e. it should be much larger than őõQCDsubscriptőõQCDLambdarm QCD, but much smaller than the ultraviolet cutoff a‚ąí1superscriptūĚĎé1a-1. In practice, this means a requirement of rather small lattice spacings. The considered heavy-light current is defined as: Jő®,Ōąőľ‚Äč(x)=ő®¬Į‚Äč(x)‚Äčőďőľ‚ÄčŌą‚Äč(x)+Ōą¬Į‚Äč(x)‚Äčőďőľ‚Äčő®‚Äč(x),superscriptsubscriptūĚźĹő®ūĚúďūĚúáūĚĎ•¬Įő®ūĚĎ•superscriptőďūĚúáūĚúďūĚĎ•¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎ•superscriptőďūĚúáő®ūĚĎ•JPsi,psimu(x)=overlinePsi(x)Gammamupsi(x)+overlinepsi(x)GammamuPsi(x),, with Ōą‚Äč(x)ūĚúďūĚĎ•psi(x) denoting the light quark field and ő®‚Äč(x)ő®ūĚĎ•Psi(x) the fictitious heavy quark field. The Dirac structure, őďőľsuperscriptőďūĚúáGammamu can be general and is typically chosen according to the desired final observable. The Euclidean Compton scattering tensor is then constructed: Tő®,Ōąőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(p,q)‚Č°‚ąĎS‚ü®p,S|tő®,Ōąőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(q)|p,S‚ü©=‚ąĎS‚ąęd4‚Äčx‚Äčei‚Äčq‚čÖx‚Äč‚ü®p,S|T‚Äč[Jő®,Ōąőľ‚Äč(x)‚ÄčJő®,ŌąőĹ‚Äč]|p,S‚ü©,subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúąő®ūĚúďūĚĎĚūĚĎěsubscriptūĚĎÜquantum-operator-productūĚĎĚūĚĎÜsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúáūĚúąő®ūĚúďūĚĎěūĚĎĚūĚĎÜsubscriptūĚĎÜsuperscriptūĚĎĎ4ūĚĎ•superscripte‚čÖūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎ•quantum-operator-productūĚĎĚūĚĎÜūĚĎádelimited-superscriptsubscriptūĚźĹő®ūĚúďūĚúáūĚĎ•superscriptsubscriptūĚźĹő®ūĚúďūĚúą0ūĚĎĚūĚĎÜTmunuPsi,psi(p,q)equivsumS p,S|hskip 0.85355pttmunuPsi,psi(q)|p,S=sumSint d4x eiqcdot x p,S|Tleft[JPsi,psimu(x)JPsi,psinuright]|p,S,, where |p,S‚ü©ketūĚĎĚūĚĎÜ|p,S are hadron states with momentum pūĚĎĚp and spin SūĚĎÜS and the spins are summed over. The momentum transfer qūĚĎěq between the hadron and the scattered lepton in a DIS process should be smaller or at most of the order of the heavy quark mass. Furthermore, the momenta should satisfy the constraint (pM+qM)2<(mő®+őõQCD)2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÄsubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎÄ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎöő®subscriptőõQCD2(pM+qM)2<(mPsi+Lambdarm QCD)2, where the momenta with subscript MūĚĎÄM are the Minkowski counterparts of the Euclidean ones. If this condition is satisfied, analytic continuation of the hadronic tensor to Euclidean spacetime is straightforward and can be achieved by relating q4subscriptūĚĎě4q4 to i‚Äčq0ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎě0iq0. Expanding this tensor, in the continuum, using OPE, one can relate it to moments of PDFs. On the lattice, one needs to compute four-point functions to access the Compton tensor for PDFs. Analogous procedure may be applied to compute distribution amplitudes, for which the hadronic interpolating operator needs to be applied only once to the vacuum state and hence a computation of only three-point functions is required. Numerical exploration, in the quenched approximation, is in progress citebib.bib68, aimed at extracting the moments of the pion DA. Since the matching to OPE has to be performed in the continuum, at least three values of the lattice spacing need to be employed for a reliable extrapolation. Preliminary results are presented in Sec. demonstrating the feasibility of this method.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Auxiliary ligh: Another possibility for extraction of light-cone distribution functions appeared in 2007 by V. Braun and D. M√ľller citebib.bib69 and is based on the lattice calculation of exclusive amplitudes in coordinate space. It is similar to the fictitious heavy quark approach, but the heavy quark is replaced by an auxiliary light quark. One considers a current-current correlator: Tőľ‚ÄčőĹ=‚ü®0|T‚Äčjőľ‚Äč(z)‚ÄčjőĹ‚Äč(‚ąíz)|ŌÄ‚Äč(p)‚ü©,subscriptūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúąquantum-operator-product0ūĚĎásubscriptūĚĎóūĚúáūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎóūĚúąūĚĎßūĚúčūĚĎĚTmunu= 0|Tjmu(z)jnu(-z)|pi(p),, with jőľ‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎóūĚúáūĚĎßjmu(z) being the electromagnetic current, but other choices of currents are also possible. For a discussion on extracting partonic distributions at light-like separations from such Euclidean correlators, we refer to Sec. II of Ref. citebib.bib70. On the lattice, Tőľ‚ÄčőĹsubscriptūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúąTmunu can be computed as an appropriate ratio of a three-point and a two-point function. If the separation between currents is small, the correlator can be computed perturbatively (using OPE) and in such a case Eq. yields: Tőľ‚ÄčőĹ=‚ąí5‚Äči9‚ÄčfŌÄ‚ÄčŌĶőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚ÄčŌĀ‚ÄčŌÉ‚ÄčzŌĀ‚ÄčpŌÉ8‚ÄčŌÄ2‚Äčz4‚Äč‚ąę01ūĚĎĎu‚Äčei‚Äč(2‚Äču‚ąí1)‚Äčp‚čÖz‚ÄčŌēŌÄ‚Äč(u,őľ),subscriptūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúą5ūĚĎĖ9subscriptūĚĎďūĚúčsubscriptitalic-ŌĶūĚúáūĚúąūĚúĆūĚúésuperscriptūĚĎßūĚúĆsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚúé8superscriptūĚúč2superscriptūĚĎß4superscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎĘsuperscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖ2ūĚĎĘ1ūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptitalic-ŌēūĚúčūĚĎĘūĚúáTmunu=-frac5i9fpiepsilonmunurhosigmafraczrhopsigma8pi2z4int01du,ei(2u-1)pcdot zphipi(u,mu), at leading order and leading twist. Eq. is proportional to the Fourier transform, ő¶ŌÄ‚Äč(p‚čÖz)=‚ąę01ūĚĎĎu‚Äčei‚Äč(2‚Äču‚ąí1)‚Äčp‚čÖx‚ÄčŌēŌÄ‚Äč(u,őľ)subscriptő¶ūĚúč‚čÖūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎĘsuperscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖ2ūĚĎĘ1ūĚĎĚūĚĎ•subscriptitalic-ŌēūĚúčūĚĎĘūĚúáPhipi(pcdot z)=int01du,ei(2u-1)pcdot xphipi(u,mu), of the pion DA, ŌēŌÄ‚Äč(u,őľ)subscriptitalic-ŌēūĚúčūĚĎĘūĚúáphipi(u,mu), where uūĚĎĘu is the quark momentum fraction. The perturbative expression for the correlator was also derived in Ref. citebib.bib69 to NNLO and including twist-4 corrections. The LO and leading twist expression for the case of scalar-pseudoscalar densities in Eq. was given in Ref. citebib.bib71. It has been emphasized that the pion boost plays a different role than in some other approaches, as it does not suppress higher-twist contributions, but rather enters the Ioffe time p‚čÖz‚čÖūĚĎĚūĚĎßpcdot z. Thus, going to large boosts is important to have the full information on the coordinate space pion DA, ő¶ŌÄ‚Äč(p‚čÖz)subscriptő¶ūĚúč‚čÖūĚĎĚūĚĎßPhipi(pcdot z), which can allow disentanglement between phenomenological models considered in the literature, that disagree in the regime of large Ioffe times. Advantages of the approach include the possibility of having arbitrary direction of zūĚĎßz with respect to the boost direction, which may make it possible to minimize discretization effects. Moreover, one avoids complications related to the renormalization in the presence of a Wilson line (see Sec. ), i. e. one only needs renormalization of standard local operators which is at most logarithmically divergent. Finally, different possible Dirac structures may give the possibility of better control of higher-twist contamination. Obviously, the approach can also be generalized to extract PDFs, which, however, would necessitate the computation of four-point functions (see also Sec. ). The first numerical investigation of this approach is under way by the Regensburg group citebib.bib71, bib.bib70 and is aimed at computing the pion DA, using multiple channels. The results fully prove the feasibility of this method and establish its status as a promising way of studying hadron structure, see also Sec. . Nevertheless, the requirement of calculation of four-point functions for extracting PDFs may prove to be a serious restriction and an exploratory study for e. g. nucleon PDFs is not yet available.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Quasi-distribu: In 2013, X. Ji proposed a new approach to extracting the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of structure functions citebib.bib45. Although historically it was not the first idea, it can be presently judged that it has been a breakthrough in the community‚Äôs thinking about xūĚĎ•x-dependence from numerical simulations on a Euclidean lattice. In particular, it clearly renewed the interest also in approaches proposed earlier and described above. Ji‚Äôs approach, obviously, bears similarities with the earlier methods and is also based on the factorization framework, in which a lattice computable function is factorized into a hard coefficient and a non-perturbative object like a PDF or a DA. The main difference is another type of object that is used to connect a quark and an antiquark separated by some distance and that ensures gauge invariance. In earlier proposals, different types of auxiliary quark propagators were used for this ‚Äď scalar, heavy or light quark propagators. In Ji‚Äôs technique, this role is played by a Wilson line, i. e. the same object that is used in definitions of PDFs and other distribution functions. Thus, in general, the quasi-distribution approach is the closest transcription of a light-cone definition to Euclidean spacetime, effectively boiling down to replacing light-cone correlations by equal-time correlators along the direction of the Wilson line. We illustrate the idea using the example of PDFs, while analogous formulations can be used to define DAs, GPDs etc. It is instructive to see the direct correspondence between the light-cone definition (Eq. ) and the definition of quasi-PDFs. As pointed out above, since light-cone correlations can not be accessed on a Euclidean lattice, Ji proposed to evaluate on the lattice the following distribution, now termed the quasi-distribution: q‚Äč(x,P3)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčz4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčz‚Äčk3‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčő≥0‚ÄčW‚Äč(z)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉ3superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎß4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎė3quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚõĺ0ūĚĎäūĚĎßūĚúď0ūĚĎÉtildeq(x,P3)=int-inftyinftyfracdz4pie-izk3 P|barpsi(z)gamma0W(z)psi|P, where P=(P0,0,0,P3)ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎÉ000subscriptūĚĎÉ3P=(P0,0,0,P3), k3=x‚ÄčP3subscriptūĚĎė3ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉ3k3=xP3 is the quark momentum in the 333-direction, and W‚Äč(z)=e‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0zūĚĎĎz‚Ä≤‚ÄčA3‚Äč(z‚Ä≤)ūĚĎäūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎßdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤subscriptūĚźī3superscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤W(z)=e-igint0zdzprimeA3(zprime) is the Wilson line in the boost direction 4The Dirac structure was, in the original papers, also in the same direction, i. e. ő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma3 was used. However, it became clear that ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 is a better choice that leads to the same PDF, as described in Sec. . . The light-cone definition corresponds to the above expression at infinite momentum boost, in line with Feynman‚Äôs original parton model citebib.bib72, bib.bib73. Since the momentum of the nucleon on the lattice is obviously finite, the partonic interpretation is formally lost and some quarks can carry more momentum than the whole nucleon (x>1ūĚĎ•1x>1) or move in the opposite direction to it (x<0ūĚĎ•0x<0). The quasi-distribution differs from the light-cone one by higher-twist corrections suppressed with őõQCD2/P32superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ32Lambdarm QCD2/P32 and MN2/P32superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ32MN2/P32, where MNsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀMN is the nucleon mass, see Sec. for more details. A vital observation of Ji was that the difference between the two types of distributions arises only in the UV region, i. e. their structure in the IR is the same. This means that the UV difference can be computed in perturbation theory and subtracted from the result, which comes under the name of matching to a light-cone distribution or Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) citebib.bib74. The possibility of correcting the higher-twist effects by LaMET is an important difference with respect to previously mentioned approaches. However, explicit computation of such effects is also possible in them, as demonstrated already in the original paper for the auxiliary light quark approach citebib.bib69. The quasi-distribution approach received a lot of interest in the community and sparked most of the numerical work among all the direct xūĚĎ•x-dependence methods. In further sections, we discuss in more detail its various aspects and the plethora of numerical results obtained so far.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Pseudo-distrib: The approach of quasi-distributions was thoroughly analyzed by A. Radyushkin citebib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77 in the framework of virtuality distribution functions introduced by the same Author citebib.bib78, bib.bib79 and straight-link primordial TMDs. In the process, he discovered another, but strongly related, type of distribution that is accessible on the lattice and can be related to light-cone distributions via factorization. It can be extracted from precisely the same matrix element that appears in Eq. , ‚Ą≥‚Äč(őĹ,‚ąíőĺ2)‚Č°‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí)‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčW‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©‚Ą≥ūĚúąsuperscriptūĚúČ2quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚúČsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎäsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉcal M(nu,-xi2)equiv P|overlinepsi(xi-)gamma+W(xi-,0)psi|P, viewed as a function of two Lorentz invariants, the ‚ÄúIoffe time‚ÄĚ citebib.bib52, őĹ‚Č°‚ąíp‚čÖőĺūĚúą‚čÖūĚĎĚūĚúČnuequiv-pcdotxi and ‚ąíőĺ2superscriptūĚúČ2-xi2. Thus, ‚Ą≥‚Äč(őĹ,‚ąíőĺ2)‚Ą≥ūĚúąsuperscriptūĚúČ2cal M(nu,-xi2) has been termed the Ioffe-time distribution (ITD). As in Ji‚Äôs approach the vector őĺūĚúČxi can be chosen to be purely spatial, őĺ=(0,0,0,z)ūĚúČ000ūĚĎßxi=(0,0,0,z) on a Euclidean lattice. Then, one defines a pseudo-distribution: ūĚíę‚Äč(x,z2)=12‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěūĚĎĎőĹ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčx‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč‚Ą≥‚Äč(őĹ,z2). ūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎß212ūĚúčsuperscriptsubscriptdifferential-dūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•ūĚúą‚Ą≥ūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎß2cal P(x,z2)=frac12piint-inftyinftydnu,e-ixnu,cal M(nu,z2). Thus, the variation with respect to a quasi-PDF is the Fourier transform that is taken over the Ioffe time (at fixed z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2), as opposed to being over the Wilson line length zūĚĎßz (at fixed momentum P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3). A consequence of this difference is that pseudo-PDFs have considerably distinct properties from quasi-PDFs. In particular, the distribution has the canonical support, x‚ąą[‚ąí1,1]ūĚĎ•11xin[-1,1]. We briefly mention here the issue of power divergences induced by the Wilson line, to be discussed more extensively in Sec. and Sec. . In the pseudo-distribution approach, a convenient way of eliminating these (multiplicative) divergences is to take the ratio ūĚĒź‚Äč(őĹ,z2)=‚Ą≥‚Äč(őĹ,z2)/‚Ą≥‚Äč(0,z2)ūĚĒźūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎß2‚Ą≥ūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎß2‚Ą≥0superscriptūĚĎß2mathfrakM(nu,z2)=cal M(nu,z2)/cal M(0,z2) citebib.bib77, bib.bib80. The reduced ITD, ūĚĒź‚Äč(őĹ,z2)ūĚĒźūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎß2mathfrakM(nu,z2), can then be perturbatively matched to a light-cone Ioffe-time PDF citebib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, as demonstrated in Sec. . The (inverse) length of the Wilson line plays the role of the renormalization scale and can be related to, e. g. , the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scale. Numerical investigation of the pseudo-distribution approach has proceeded in parallel with the theoretical developments and promising results are being reportedcitebib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib81, bib.bib88 (see also Sec. ).
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS OPE without OP: Yet another recent proposal to compute hadronic structure functions was suggested in Ref. citebib.bib89. It is closely related to known ideas introduced around 20 years ago, dubbed ‚ÄúOPE without OPE‚ÄĚ by G. Martinelli citebib.bib90 and applied, e. g. , in flavor physicscitebib.bib91. The name originates from the fact that one directly computes the chronologically ordered product of two currents rather than matrix elements of local operators. In addition, one works in the regime of small spacetime separations between currents (to use perturbation theory to determine the expected form of the OPE), but large enough to avoid large discretization effects. The idea is also an ingredient of the proposal to compute LCWFs with the aid of a fictitious scalar quark citebib.bib64. The starting point is the forward Compton amplitude of the nucleon, defined similarly as in Eq. . It can be decomposed in terms of DIS structure functions F1subscriptūĚźĻ1F1 and F2subscriptūĚźĻ2F2. With particular choice of kinematics, one can obtain the following relations between the 333333-component of the Compton amplitude and F1subscriptūĚźĻ1F1: T33‚Äč(p,q)=‚ąĎn=2,4,‚čĮ‚ąě4‚ÄčŌČn‚Äč‚ąę01ūĚĎĎx‚Äčxn‚ąí1‚ÄčF1‚Äč(x,q2)=4‚ÄčŌČ‚Äč‚ąę01ūĚĎĎx‚ÄčŌČ‚Äčx1‚ąí(ŌČ‚Äčx)2‚ÄčF1‚Äč(x,q2),subscriptūĚĎá33ūĚĎĚūĚĎěsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎõ24‚čĮ4superscriptūĚúĒūĚĎõsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ1subscriptūĚźĻ1ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎě24ūĚúĒsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎ•ūĚúĒūĚĎ•1superscriptūĚúĒūĚĎ•2subscriptūĚźĻ1ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎě2T33(p,q)=sumn=2,4,cdotsinfty4omeganint01dx,xn-1F1(x,q2)=4omegaint01dx,fracomega x1-(omega x)2F1(x,q2),, where ŌČ=2‚Äčp‚čÖq/q2ūĚúĒ‚čÖ2ūĚĎĚūĚĎěsuperscriptūĚĎě2omega=2pcdot q/q2. Being able to access T33‚Äč(p,q)subscriptūĚĎá33ūĚĎĚūĚĎěT33(p,q) for large enough number of values of ŌČūĚúĒomega, one can extract the moments of F1‚Äč(x,q2)subscriptūĚźĻ1ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎě2F1(x,q2) or even the whole function. Another important ingredient of the method proposed in Ref. citebib.bib89 is the efficient computation of T33subscriptūĚĎá33T33, i. e. one that avoids the computation of four-point functions. It relies on the Feynman-Hellmann relation citebib.bib92. One extends the QCD Lagrangian with a perturbation ‚Ąí‚Äč(x)‚Üí‚Ąí‚Äč(x)+őĽ‚ÄčūĚí•3‚Äč(x),ūĚí•3‚Äč(x)=ZV‚Äčcos‚Ā°(q‚Üí‚čÖx‚Üí)‚Äčef‚ÄčŌą¬Įf‚Äč(x)‚Äčő≥3‚ÄčŌąf‚Äč(x),formulae-sequence‚Üí‚ĄíūĚĎ•‚ĄíūĚĎ•ūĚúÜsubscriptūĚí•3ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚí•3ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎćūĚĎČ‚čÖ‚ÜíūĚĎě‚ÜíūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎíūĚĎďsubscript¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎďūĚĎ•subscriptūĚõĺ3subscriptūĚúďūĚĎďūĚĎ•L(x)rightarrowL(x)+lambdaJ3(x),,quadJ3(x)=ZVcos(vecqcdotvecx);ef,barpsif(x)gamma3psif(x),, where efsubscriptūĚĎíūĚĎďef is the electric charge of the fūĚĎďf-th flavor and őĽūĚúÜlambda is a parameter with dimension of mass. Evaluating the derivative of the nucleon energy with respect to őĽūĚúÜlambda, which requires dedicated simulations at a few őĽūĚúÜlambda values, leads to estimates of T33subscriptūĚĎá33T33: T33‚Äč(p,q)=‚ąí2‚ÄčEőĽ‚Äč(p,q)‚Äč‚ąā2‚ąāőĽ2‚ÄčEőĽ‚Äč(p,q)|őĽ=0. subscriptūĚĎá33ūĚĎĚūĚĎěevaluated-at2subscriptūĚźłūĚúÜūĚĎĚūĚĎěsuperscript2superscriptūĚúÜ2subscriptūĚźłūĚúÜūĚĎĚūĚĎěūĚúÜ0T33(p,q)=-2Elambda(p,q),fracpartial2partiallambda2Elambda(p,q),big|lambda=0,. The Authors also showed first results obtained in this framework and point to directions of possible improvements and to prospects of computing the entire structure function based on this method (see Sec. ).
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Good lattice c: A novel approach to extracting PDFs or other partonic correlation functions from ab initio lattice calculations was proposed by Y. -Q. Ma and J. -W. Qiu citebib.bib93, bib.bib94, bib.bib95. They advocate for a global fit of ‚Äúlattice cross sections‚ÄĚ (LCSs), i. e. appropriate lattice observables defined below, to which many of the ones described above belong. The logic is that standard phenomenological extractions of PDFs rely on an analogous fit to hadronic cross sections (HCSs) obtained in experiments and a global fit approach can average out some of the systematics and yield ultimately good precision. Good LCSs, i. e. ones that can be included in such a global fit, are the ones that have the following properties: 1. they are calculable in Euclidean lattice QCD, 2. have a well-defined continuum limit, 3. have the same and factorizable logarithmic collinear divergences as PDFs. All of these properties are crucial and non-trivial. The first one excludes the direct use of observables defined on the light cone. In practice, the second one requires the observables to be renormalizable. Finally, the third property implies that the analogy with global fits to HCSs is even more appropriate ‚Äď both strategies need to rely on the factorization framework: LCSs and HCSs are then written as a convolution of a perturbatively computable hard coefficient with a PDF. Ma and Qiu constructed also a class of good LCSs in coordinate space that have the potential of being used in the proposed global fits, demonstrating that the three defining properties of LCSs are satisfied citebib.bib95. The considered class is very closely related to the one proposed by Braun and M√ľller (see Sec. ), but the latter Authors concentrated on the pion DA, while the analysis of Ma and Qiu deals with the case of hadronic PDFs. In general, the relevant matrix element can be written as ŌÉn‚Äč(ŌČ,őĺ2,P2,őľ)=‚ü®P|T‚ÄčūĚí™n‚Äč(őĺ,őľ)|P‚ü©,subscriptūĚúéūĚĎõūĚúĒsuperscriptūĚúČ2superscriptūĚĎÉ2ūĚúáquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉūĚĎásubscriptūĚí™ūĚĎõūĚúČūĚúáūĚĎÉsigman(omega,xi2,P2,mu)= P|Tcal On(xi,mu)|P, where nūĚĎõn stands for different possible operators that can be shown to be factorizable into the desired PDF. PūĚĎÉP is the hadron momentum, and őĺūĚúČxi is the largest separation of fields from which the nūĚĎõn-th operator is constructed (őĺ2‚Č†0superscriptūĚúČ20xi2neq 0), ŌČ‚Č°P‚čÖőĺūĚúĒ‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚúČomegaequivPcdotxi. One suggested choice for ūĚí™nsubscriptūĚí™ūĚĎõcal On are the current-current correlators: ūĚí™J1‚ÄčJ2‚Äč(őĺ)‚Č°őĺdJ1+dJ2‚ąí2‚ÄčJ1R‚Äč(őĺ)‚ÄčJ2R‚Äč,subscriptūĚí™subscriptūĚźĹ1subscriptūĚźĹ2ūĚúČsuperscriptūĚúČsubscriptūĚĎĎsubscriptūĚźĹ1subscriptūĚĎĎsubscriptūĚźĹ22superscriptsubscriptūĚźĹ1ūĚĎÖūĚúČsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĹ2ūĚĎÖ0cal OJ1J2(xi)equivxidJ1+dJ2-2,J1R(xi)J2R,, where dJisubscriptūĚĎĎsubscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĖdJi stands for the dimension of the renormalized current JiR=ZJi‚ÄčJisuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĖūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎćsubscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĖJiR=ZJiJi, with ZJisubscriptūĚĎćsubscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĖZJi being the renormalization function of the current JisubscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĖJi. Different possible options for the currents were outlined and then, factorization was demonstrated for this whole class of LCSs. Renormalizability of these objects is straightforward, as they are constructed from local currents. Also, the feasibility of a lattice calculation is easy to establish if őĺūĚúČxi has no time component. Thus, this class of matrix elements belongs to the set of good LCSs. It was also shown in Ref. citebib.bib95 that three of the observables discussed above, quasi-PDFs, pseudo-PDFs and the Compton amplitude T33subscriptūĚĎá33T33 are also examples of good LCSs. An explicit numerical investigation of the current-current correlators is in progress by the theory group of Jefferson National Laboratory (JLab) and first promising results for pion PDFs, using around 10 different currents, have been presented. For more details see Sec. .
content_sections.QUASI-PDFS: MORE DETAILS AND EARLY NUMER: We discuss now, in more detail, the quasi-distribution approach which is the main topic of this review. The focus of this section is on the theoretical principles of this method and we closely follow the original discussion in Ji‚Äôs first papers. Since these were soon followed by numerical calculation within Lattice QCD exploring the feasibility of the approach, we also summarize the progress on this side. We also identify the missing ingredients in these early studies and aspects that need significant improvement.

Ji‚Äôs idea of quasi-PDFs citebib.bib45 relies on the intuition that if light-cone PDFs can be equivalently formulated in the infinite momentum frame (IMF), then the physics of a hadron boosted to a large but finite momentum has to have much in common with the physics of the IMF. Moreover, the difference between a large momentum frame and the IMF should vanish when the hadron momentum approaches infinity. These intuitions were formalized by Ji in his original paper and we reproduce here his arguments. Consider a local twist-2 operator Oőľ1‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äčőľn=Ōą¬Į‚Äčő≥(őľ1‚Äči‚ÄčDőľ2‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äči‚ÄčDőľn)‚ÄčŌą‚ąítraces,Omu1ldotsmun=overlinepsigamma(mu1iDmu2. . . iDmun)psi-rm traces,, where parentheses in superscript indicate symmetrization of indices and the subtracted trace terms include operators of dimension (n+2)ūĚĎõ2(n+2) with at most n‚ąí2ūĚĎõ2n-2 Lorentz indices. The matrix element of such an operator in the nucleon state reads ‚ü®P|Oőľ1‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äčőľn‚Äč(őľ2)|P‚ü©=2‚Äčan‚Äč(őľ2)‚Äčő†őľ1‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äčőľn,quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚúá1‚Ä¶subscriptūĚúáūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2ūĚĎÉ2subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptő†subscriptūĚúá1‚Ä¶subscriptūĚúáūĚĎõ P|Omu1ldotsmun(mu2)|P=2an(mu2)Pimu1ldotsmun,, where ő†őľ1‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äčőľnsuperscriptő†subscriptūĚúá1‚Ä¶subscriptūĚúáūĚĎõPimu1ldotsmun is a symmetric rank-nūĚĎõn tensor citebib.bib96 and the coefficients ansubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõan are moments of PDFs, i. e. ‚ąęūĚĎĎx‚Äčxn‚ąí1‚Äčq‚Äč(x,őľ2)=an‚Äč(őľ2)differential-dūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ1ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚúá2subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2int dx,xn-1q(x,mu2)=an(mu2) with even nūĚĎõn. Taking all indices őľ1=‚Ä¶=őľn=+subscriptūĚúá1‚Ä¶subscriptūĚúáūĚĎõmu1=ldots=mun=+, one recovers the light-cone, time-dependent correlation that defines the PDF. We now consider a different choice of indices, without any temporal component, őľ1=‚Ä¶=őľn=3subscriptūĚúá1‚Ä¶subscriptūĚúáūĚĎõ3mu1=ldots=mun=3: O3‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äč3=Ōą¬Į‚Äčő≥3‚Äči‚ÄčD3‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äči‚ÄčD3‚ÄčŌą‚ąítraces,superscriptūĚĎā3‚Ä¶3¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚõĺ3ūĚĎĖsuperscriptūĚź∑3‚Ä¶ūĚĎĖsuperscriptūĚź∑3ūĚúďtracesO3ldots 3=overlinepsigamma3iD3ldots iD3psi-rm traces,, with the trace terms containing operators with again at most n‚ąí2ūĚĎõ2n-2 Lorentz indices. Because of Lorentz invariance, matrix elements of the trace terms in the nucleon state are at most (P3)n‚ąí2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚĎõ2(P3)n-2 multiplied by őõQCD2subscriptsuperscriptőõ2QCDLambda2rm QCD. On the other hand citebib.bib96, ő†3‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äč3=‚ąĎjn/2c‚Äč(j,n)‚Äč((P3)2)n2‚ąíj‚Äč(MN2)j,superscriptő†3‚Ä¶3superscriptsubscriptūĚĎóūĚĎõ2ūĚĎźūĚĎóūĚĎõsuperscriptsuperscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉ32ūĚĎõ2ūĚĎósuperscriptsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2ūĚĎóPi3ldots 3=sumjn/2,c(j,n)left((P3)2right)fracn2-jleft(MN2right)j, where c‚Äč(j,n)ūĚĎźūĚĎóūĚĎõc(j,n) is a combinatorial coefficient and MNsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀMN is the nucleon mass. As a consequence, we find that ‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äčő≥3‚Äči‚ÄčD3‚Äč‚Ä¶‚Äči‚ÄčD3‚ÄčŌą|P‚ü©=2‚Äčan‚Äč(őľ2)‚Äč(P3)n‚Äč(1+ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2(P3)2,MN2(P3)2)). quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚõĺ3ūĚĎĖsuperscriptūĚź∑3‚Ä¶ūĚĎĖsuperscriptūĚź∑3ūĚúďūĚĎÉ2subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚĎõ1ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉ32superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉ32 P|overlinepsigamma3iD3ldots iD3psi|P=2an(mu2)(P3)nleft(1+Oleft(fracLambdarm QCD2(P3)2,fracMN2(P3)2right)right). The form of this expression implies that using an operator with the Wilson line in a spatial direction, in a nucleon state with finite momentum, leads to the light-cone PDF up to power-suppressed corrections in the inverse squared momentum. The corrections are of two kinds ‚Äď generic higher-twist corrections and ones resulting from the non-zero mass of the nucleon. As we will discuss below, the latter can be calculated analytically and subtracted out. However, the former can only be overcome by simulating at a large enough nucleon boost and by using a matching procedure. In the original paper that introduced the quasi-distribution approach citebib.bib45, Ji pointed out an intuitive way to understand the above result: ‚Äú(‚Ä¶) consider the Lorentz transformation of a line segment connecting (0,0,0,z)000ūĚĎß(0,0,0,z) with the origin of the coordinates. As the boost velocity approaches the speed of light, the space-like line segment is tilted to the light-cone direction. Of course, it cannot literally be on the light-cone because the invariant length cannot change for any amount of boost. However, this slight off-light-cone-ness only introduces power corrections which vanish asymptotically. ‚ÄĚ This intuition is schematically represented in Fig. . We turn now to discussing how to match results obtained on the lattice, with a hadron momentum that is finite and relatively small, to the IMF. The subtlety of this results from the fact that regularizing the UV divergences does not commute with taking the infinite momentum limit. When defining PDFs, the latter has to be taken first, i. e. before removing the UV cutoff, whereas on the lattice one is bound to take all scales, including the momentum boost of the nucleon, much smaller than the cutoff, whose role is played by the inverse lattice spacing. To overcome this difficulty, one needs to formulate an effective field theory, termed Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) citebib.bib74, which takes the form of matching conditions that take the quasi-distribution to the IMF, or light-cone, distribution. LaMET is an effective theory of QCD in the presence of a large momentum scale P3superscriptūĚĎÉ3P3, in a similar sense as Heavy Quark Effective Theory (HQET) citebib.bib97 is an effective theory of QCD in the presence of a heavy quark, that can have a mass larger than the lattice UV cutoff. The parallels of LaMET with HQET are more than superficial. We again follow Ji‚Äôs discussion citebib.bib74. In HQET, a generic observable OūĚĎāO depends on the heavy mass mbsubscriptūĚĎöūĚĎŹmb and a cutoff őõőõLambda. The matching with an observable oūĚĎúo defined in the effective theory, in which the heavy quark has infinite mass, can be written in the following way, due to asymptotic freedom: O‚Äč(mb/őõ)=Z‚Äč(mb/őõ,őõ/őľ)‚Äčo‚Äč(őľ)+ūĚí™‚Äč(1/mb),ūĚĎāsubscriptūĚĎöūĚĎŹőõūĚĎćsubscriptūĚĎöūĚĎŹőõőõūĚúáūĚĎúūĚúáūĚí™1subscriptūĚĎöūĚĎŹO(mb/Lambda)=Z(mb/Lambda,Lambda/mu)o(mu)+O(1/mb),, where oūĚĎúo is renormalized at a scale őľūĚúámu in the effective theory. Additionally, renormalization of the full theory translates the cutoff scale őõőõLambda to a renormalization scale őľūĚúámu. The crucial aspect is that OūĚĎāO and oūĚĎúo have the same infrared physics. Thus, the matching coefficient, ZūĚĎćZ, is perturbatively computable as an expansion in the strong coupling constant. Apart from the perturbative matching, there are power-suppressed corrections, which can also be calculated. Using the same ideas, one can write the relation between an observable in the lattice theory, QūĚĎĄQ, dependent on the analogue of a heavy mass, i. e. a large momentum P3superscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 (and on the cutoff scale), and an observable in a theory in the IMF, qūĚĎěq, thus corresponding to Feynman‚Äôs parton model or to a light-cone correlation. This is again valid because of asymptotic freedom. The matching reads: Q‚Äč(P3/őõ)=C‚Äč(P3/őõ,őõ/őľ)‚Äčq‚Äč(őľ)+ūĚí™‚Äč(1/(P3)2). ūĚĎĄsuperscriptūĚĎÉ3őõūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎÉ3őõőõūĚúáūĚĎěūĚúáūĚí™1superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉ32Q(P3/Lambda)=C(P3/Lambda,Lambda/mu)q(mu)+O(1/(P3)2),. We have, therefore, established the close analogy between HQET and the IMF parton model and the latter plays the role of an effective theory for a nucleon moving with a large momentum, just as HQET is an effective theory for QCD with a heavy mass. The infrared properties are, again, the same in both theories and the matching coefficient, CūĚź∂C, can be computed in perturbation theory. There are power-suppressed corrections in inverse powers of (P3)2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉ32(P3)2, vs. inverse powers of mbsubscriptūĚĎöūĚĎŹmb in HQET. To summarize, the need for LaMET when transcribing the finite boost results to light-cone parton distributions is the consequence of the importance of the order of limits. Parton physics corresponds to taking P3‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎÉ3P3rightarrowinfty in the observable QūĚĎĄQ first, before renormalization. On the lattice, in turn, UV regularization is necessarily taken first, before the infinite momentum limit, since no scale in the problem can be larger than the UV cutoff. However, interchanging the order of limits does not influence infrared physics and, hence, only matching in the ultraviolet has to be carried out and can be done perturbatively. The underlying factorization can be proven order by order in perturbation theory. It is important to emphasize that any partonic observable can be accessed within this framework, with the same universal steps: 1. Construction of a Euclidean version of the light-cone definition. The Euclidean observable needs to approach its light-cone counterpart in the limit of infinite momentum; 2. Computation of the appropriate matrix elements on the lattice and renormalize them; 3. Calculation of the matching coefficient in perturbation theory and use of LaMET, Eq. , to extract the light-cone distribution. There is complete analogy also with accessing parton physics from scattering experiments, using factorization theorems and, thus, separating the non-perturbative (low-energy) and perturbative (high-energy) scales. To have similar access to partonic observables from lattice computations, LaMET plays the role of a tool for scale separation. Moreover, just as parton distributions can be extracted from a variety of different scattering processes, they can also be approached with distinct lattice operators. We continue the discussion of LaMET by considering now the matching process in more detail. In the first paper devoted to the matching in the framework of LaMET, the non-singlet PDF case was discussed citebib.bib98. We remind here the definition of the quasi-PDF: q‚Äč(x,P3)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčz4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčz‚Äčk3‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčő≥3‚ÄčW‚Äč(z)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉ3superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎß4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎė3quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚõĺ3ūĚĎäūĚĎßūĚúď0ūĚĎÉtildeq(x,P3)=int-inftyinftyfracdz4pie-izk3 P|barpsi(z)gamma3W(z)psi|P, taking the original choice of the Dirac structure, i. e. ő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma3 for the unpolarized case (see discussion about mixing for certain Dirac structures in Sec. ). The matching condition should take the form: q‚Äč(x,őľ2,P3)=‚ąę‚ąí11d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčC‚Äč(xy,őľP3)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ2)+ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2/P32,MN2/P32),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚúá2subscriptūĚĎÉ3subscriptsuperscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚúá2ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ32superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ32tildeq(x,mu2,P3)=int1-1fracdy|y|,Cleft(fracxy,fracmuP3right)q(y,mu2)+Oleft(Lambdarm QCD2/P32,MN2/P32right) , where the quasi-PDF, q‚Äč(x,őľ2,P3)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚúá2subscriptūĚĎÉ3tildeq(x,mu2,P3), is renormalized at a scale őľūĚúámu. The calculation of the matching is performed in a simple transverse momentum cutoff scheme, regulating the UV divergence, and later in Sec. , we will consider further developments, including matching from different schemes to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. The motivation behind using the transverse momentum cutoff scheme is to take trace of the linear divergence related to the presence of the Wilson line, which would not be possible when using dimensional regularization. The tree-level of both the quasi- and light-cone distributions is the same, i. e. a Dirac delta őī‚Äč(1‚ąíx)ūĚõŅ1ūĚĎ•delta(1-x). At one-loop level, two kinds of contributions appear ‚Äď the self-energy diagram (left one in Fig. ) and the vertex diagram (right one in Fig. ). The quasi-distribution receives, hence, the following one-loop correction: q‚Äč(x,őõ,P3)=(1+ZF‚Äč(őõ,P3))‚Äčőī‚Äč(1‚ąíx)+q‚Äč(x,őõ,P3)+ūĚí™‚Äč(őĪs2),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•őõsubscriptūĚĎÉ31subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎć1ūĚźĻőõsubscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚõŅ1ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎě1ūĚĎ•őõsubscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2tildeq(x,Lambda,P3)=(1+tildeZF(Lambda,P3))delta(1-x)+tildeq(x,Lambda,P3)+O(alphas2),, where ZFsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎć1ūĚźĻtildeZF are one-loop self-energy corrections (wave function corrections) and qsuperscriptūĚĎě1tildeq are the one-loop vertex corrections. Expressions for an explicit form of ZFsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎć1ūĚźĻtildeZF and qsuperscriptūĚĎě1tildeq are given in Ref. citebib.bib98. Their crucial aspect is that they are non-zero not only in the canonical range x‚ąą[0,1]ūĚĎ•01xin[0,1], but also outside of it, for any positive and negative xūĚĎ•x. This corresponds to the loss of the standard partonic interpretation mentioned above. An important aspect is the particle number conservation, ‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě+‚ąěūĚĎĎx‚Äčq‚Äč(x,őľ2,P3)=1subscriptsuperscriptdifferential-dūĚĎ•ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚúá2subscriptūĚĎÉ31int+infty-inftydx,tildeq(x,mu2,P3)=1. Different kinds of singularities appear: ‚ÄĘ linear (UV) divergences due to the Wilson line, taking in this scheme the form őõ/(1‚ąíx)2‚ÄčP3őõsuperscript1ūĚĎ•2subscriptūĚĎÉ3Lambda/(1-x)2P3, ‚ÄĘ collinear (IR) divergences, only in x‚ąą(0,1)ūĚĎ•01xin(0,1), expected to be the same as in the light-cone distribution, ‚ÄĘ soft (IR) divergences (singularities at x=1ūĚĎ•1x=1), canceling between the vertex and self-energy corrections (‚Äúplus prescription‚ÄĚ), ‚ÄĘ logarithmic (UV) divergences in self-energy corrections, regulated with another cutoff 7For proper treatment thereof, see Sec. . We turn now to the light-cone distribution. It can be calculated in the same transverse momentum cutoff scheme by taking the limit P3‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎÉ3P3rightarrowinfty √† la Weinberg citebib.bib99. We do not write here the final formulae, which can be found again in Ref. citebib.bib98. The result is the same as obtained from the light-cone definition. Crucially, the collinear divergence is the same as in the quasi-PDF, as anticipated based on physical arguments that the construction of quasi-distributions should not modify the IR properties. Obviously, the diagrams in this case are non-zero only for x‚ąą[0,1]ūĚĎ•01xin[0,1], i. e. xūĚĎ•x has a partonic interpretation. Having computed the one-loop diagrams, one is ready to calculate the matching coefficient CūĚź∂C in Eq. . Its perturbative expansion can be written as C‚Äč(őĺ,őľP3)=őī‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ)+őĪs2‚ÄčŌÄ‚ÄčCF‚ÄčC‚Äč(őĺ,őľP3)+ūĚí™‚Äč(őĪs2),ūĚź∂ūĚúČūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚõŅ1ūĚúČsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2ūĚúčsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻsuperscriptūĚź∂1ūĚúČūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2Cleft(xi,fracmuP3right)=delta(1-xi)+fracalphas2pi,CF,,C!left(xi,fracmuP3right)+O(alphas2), with the following one-loop function: C‚Äč(őĺ,őľP3)superscriptūĚź∂1ūĚúČūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉ3displaystyle Cleft(xi,fracmuP3right) =displaystyle= 1+őĺ21‚ąíőĺ‚Äčln‚Ā°őĺőĺ‚ąí1+1+1(1‚ąíőĺ)2‚ÄčőõP3őĺ>11+őĺ21‚ąíőĺ‚Äčln‚Ā°P32őľ2‚Äč(4‚Äčőĺ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))‚ąí2‚Äčőĺ1‚ąíőĺ+1+1(1‚ąíőĺ)2‚ÄčőõP301[10.0pt] displaystylefrac1+xi21-xilnfracP32mu2left(4xi(1-xi)right)-frac2xi1-xi+1+frac1(1-xi)2fracLambdaP301‚ąí1+y21‚ąíy‚Äčln‚Ā°P32őľ2‚Äč(4‚Äčy‚Äč(1‚ąíy))+2‚Äčy‚Äč(2‚Äčy‚ąí1)1‚ąíy+1‚ąí1(1‚ąíy)2‚ÄčőõP301[10.0pt] displaystyle-frac1+y21-ylnfracP32mu2left(4y(1-y)right)+frac2y(2y-1)1-y+1-frac1(1-y)2fracLambdaP30<y<1[10.0pt] displaystylefrac1+y21-ylnfracyy-1+1-frac1(1-y)2fracLambdaP3y<0. endarrayright. Note that the matching process effectively trades the dependence on the large momentum for renormalization scale dependence (the term with the logarithm of P3/őľsubscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚúáP3/mu), another characteristic feature of effective field theories. The antiquark distribution and the CūĚź∂C-factor satisfy q¬Į‚Äč(x)=‚ąíq‚Äč(‚ąíx)¬ĮūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚĎěūĚĎ•barq(x)=-q(-x), hence including antiquarks is straightforward. Similar matching formulae were also derived for the case of helicity and transversity distributions citebib.bib98. The early papers of Refs. citebib.bib45, bib.bib74, bib.bib98 provided the systematic framework for defining quasi-distributions and matching them to their light-cone counterparts. Since then, there have been several improvements of many aspects of this programme, including renormalization, matching, target mass corrections, other theoretical aspects, as well as developments for distributions other than the non-singlet quark PDF of the nucleon discussed here. Before we turn to them, we report the early numerical efforts in Lattice QCD that illustrate the state-of-the-art calculations of that time.

Ji‚Äôs proposal for a novel approach of extracting partonic quantities on the lattice, in particular PDFs, sparked an enormous wave of interest, including numerical implementation and model investigations (see Sec. ). The first lattice results were presented in 2014 in Ref. citebib.bib46 by H. -W. Lin et al. and later in Refs. citebib.bib47, bib.bib100 by the ETM Collaboration8Effective from this year, the European Twisted Mass Collaboration has officially changed its name to Extended Twisted Mass Collaboration, as it comprises now members also from non-European institutions. Along with the name change, there is a new logo. . Lin et al. used a mixed action setup of clover valence quarks on a HISQ sea, lattice volume 243√ó64superscript24364243times64, a‚Čą0.12ūĚĎé0.12aapprox0.12 fm, pion mass (MŌÄsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚúčMpi) around 310 MeV, while ETMC used a unitary setup with maximally twisted mass quarks, lattice volume 323√ó64superscript32364323times64, a‚Čą0.082ūĚĎé0.082aapprox0.082 fm, MŌÄ‚Čą370subscriptūĚĎÄūĚúč370Mpiapprox370 MeV. Both papers implemented the bare matrix elements of the isovector unpolarized PDF (u‚ąídūĚĎĘūĚĎĎu-d flavor structure, Dirac structure ő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma3). The statistics for Lin et al. is 1383 measurements, while ETMC used a larger statistics of 5430 measurements. The employed nucleon boosts were in both cases the three lowest multiples of 2‚ÄčŌÄ/L2ūĚúčūĚźŅ2pi/L, i. e.0.43, 0.86 and 1.29 GeV (Lin et al. ) and 0.47, 0.94, 1.42 GeV (ETMC), with noticeable increase of noise for the larger boosts, resulting in larger statistical errors. In view of the missing renormalization programme, both collaborations used HYP smearing citebib.bib101 to bring the renormalization functions closer to their tree-level values (ETMC also applied the renormalization factor ZVsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎČZV to correctly renormalize the local matrix element, i. e. one without the Wilson line). ETMC presented a study of the bare matrix elements dependence on the number of HYP smearing iterations, finding large sensitivity to this number especially for the imaginary part (the matrix elements are real only in the local case). Furthermore, ETMC tested the contamination by excited states by using two source sink separations (tssubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†ts) of 8‚Äča‚Čą0.668ūĚĎé0.668aapprox0.66 fm and 10‚Äča‚Čą0.8210ūĚĎé0.8210aapprox0.82 fm, finding compatible results, but within large uncertainties. The source-sink separation in the study of Lin et al. was not reported. We note that separations below 1 fm are more susceptible to excited states contamination. However, the goal of these preliminaries studies is to explore the approach of quasi-PDFs, postponing the investigation of excited states for later calculations. Having the bare matrix elements, the Fourier transform was taken to obtain the corresponding quasi-PDFs. The quasi-PDFs were matched to light-cone PDFs using the formulae of Ref. citebib.bib98 and nucleon mass corrections were also applied. The obtained final PDFs are shown in Fig. for each study. One observes a similar picture from both setups and certain degree of qualitative agreement with phenomenological PDFs citebib.bib102, bib.bib103, bib.bib104, shown for illustration purposes. Lin et al. also computed the helicity PDF (Dirac structure ő≥3‚Äčő≥5superscriptūĚõĺ3superscriptūĚõĺ5gamma3gamma5 in the matrix elements) and quoted the value of the sea quark asymmetry, but without showing the quasi- or final distributions. The two earliest numerical investigations of Ji‚Äôs approach showed the feasibility of lattice extraction of PDFs. However, they also identified the challenges and difficulties. On one side, these were theoretical, like the necessity of development of the missing renormalization programme and the matching from the adopted renormalization scheme to the desired MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. On the other side, it became also clear that the computation is technically challenging, in particular because of the decreasing signal-to-noise ratio when increasing the nucleon boost. The computational cost also increase with the source-sink separation, for which a large value (typically above 1 fm) is needed to suppress excited states. In addition, full control over typical lattice systematics, e. g. cut-off effects, finite volume effects or the pion mass dependence, was also missing. At this stage, some difficulties were still unidentified, for example the mixing between certain Dirac structures due to the chiral symmetry breaking in the used lattice discretizations, first identified by Constantinou and Panagopouloscitebib.bib105, bib.bib106. Further progress was reported in the next two papers by the same groups (with new members), early in 2016 by Chen et al. citebib.bib107 and later in the same year by Alexandrou et al. (ETMC) citebib.bib108. Both groups used the same setups as in Refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib100, but implemented a number of improvements and considered all three types of collinear PDFs: unpolarized, helicity and transversity. Chen et al. citebib.bib107 considered two source-sink separations, ts=8‚Äča‚Čą0.96subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†8ūĚĎé0.96ts=8aapprox0.96 fm and ts=10‚Äča‚Čą1.2subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†10ūĚĎé1.2ts=10aapprox1.2 fm and performed measurements on 449 gauge field configuration ensembles with 3 source positions on each configuration, using the same set of nucleon momenta as in Ref. citebib.bib46, 0.43, 0.86 and 1.29 GeV. They also derived and implemented nucleon mass corrections (NMCs, also called target mass corrections, TMCs9Note that the same abbreviation is used in phenomenological analyses for the corrections due to a non-zero mass of the target in scattering experiments. ) for all three cases of PDFs. The NMCs will be discussed below in Sec. . In the work of ETMC citebib.bib108, a large-statistics study was performed with 30000 measurements for each of the three momenta, 0.47, 0.94, 1.42 GeV, at an increased source-sink separation of 12‚Äča‚Čą0.9812ūĚĎé0.9812aapprox0.98 fm. In the course of this work, the method of momentum smearing was introduced citebib.bib109 (see Sec. for details) to overcome the difficulty of reaching larger nucleon boosts. The technique was implemented by ETMC and results were presented for additional momenta, 1.89 and 2.36 GeV with small statistics of 150 and 300 measurements, respectively. Moreover, a test of compatibility between standard Gaussian smearing, applied in the earlier work of both groups, and the momentum smearing was performed at P3‚Čą1.42subscriptūĚĎÉ31.42P3approx1.42 GeV for the unpolarized case. This revealed a spectacular property that similar statistical error as for Gaussian smearing with 30000 measurements can be obtained with only 150 measurements employing momentum smearing. As an illustration, we show the final helicity PDFs in Fig. . Direct visual comparison between the two results is not possible, since the plot by Chen et al. shows the PDF multiplied by xūĚĎ•x. Nevertheless, the qualitative picture is similar, revealing that no striking differences occur due to different lattice setups. The much smaller uncertainty in the plot by ETMC results predominantly from over 20 times larger statistics. Analogous plots for the unpolarized and transversity cases can be seen in Refs. citebib.bib107, bib.bib108. This concludes our discussion of the early explorations of the quasi-PDF approach. Refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib100, bib.bib107, bib.bib108 proved its feasibility on the lattice and initiated identification of the challenges, already mentioned above. Further progress was conditioned on theoretical and practical improvements that will be described in later sections.
content_sections.QUASI-DISTRIBUTIONS: MODEL INVESTIGATION: Apart from theoretical analyses and numerical investigations in Lattice QCD, insights about the quasi-distribution approach were obtained also from considerations in the framework of phenomenological or large-NcsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎźNc models. In this section, we take a closer look at quasi-PDFs, quasi-GPDs and quasi-DAs in such models and review the conclusions obtained in setups where direct access to analytical forms of quasi- and light-cone distributions is possible.

The aim of the early work of L. Gamberg et al. citebib.bib114 and I. Vitev et al. citebib.bib115 was to provide guidance about nucleon momenta P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 needed for a reliable approach to the light-cone PDFs, for all collinear twist-2 distributions, i. e. unpolarized, helicity and transversity PDFs. The Authors considered the diquark spectator model (DSM) citebib.bib28, a phenomenological model that captures many of the essential features of the parton picture. The central idea of the DSM is to insert a completeness relation with intermediate states in the operator definition of PDFs (or some quark-quark correlation functions) and then truncate them to a single state with a definite mass. Such state is called the diquark spectator. This procedure boils down to making a particular ansatz for the spectral decomposition of the considered observable. The diquark spectator, in the simplest picture, can have spin-0 (scalar diquark) or spin-1 (axial-vector diquark). Finally, the nucleon is viewed as a system consisting of a constituent quark of some mass mūĚĎöm and a scalar or axial-vector diquark. The basic object in this approximation is the nucleon-quark-diquark interaction vertex, which contains a suitably chosen form factor, taken in the so-called dipolar form in Ref. citebib.bib114. With such setup, one can derive the model expressions for all kinds of collinear quasi-PDFs, combining the expressions for scalar and axial-vector diquarks. The obtained relations can be used to study the approach to the light-cone PDFs, also calculated in the DSM. Gamberg et al. citebib.bib114 got 3 couplings, fixed by normalization and 9 parameters of the model that were fixed by fitting to experimental data, with good quality of fits. Then, they considered the quasi-PDFs for different boosts from 1 to 4 GeV. It was found that the shape of quasi-PDFs approaches the PDF for P3‚Č≥2greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscriptūĚĎÉ32P3gtrsim2 GeV. The agreement is especially good in the small to intermediate-xūĚĎ•x regime, while large-xūĚĎ•x needs significantly larger boost for a satisfactory agreement. The Authors also studied the Soffer inequality citebib.bib116, stating that the transversity distribution should not be larger than the average of unpolarized and helicity ones. It holds for the standard PDFs. For quasi-PDFs, it was found that the inequality is always satisfied for the dūĚĎĎd quark, while it is violated for the uūĚĎĘu quark in the entire range of xūĚĎ•x for small momenta of around 0.5 GeV. Further model study of quasi-PDFs was presented in Ref. citebib.bib117 in 2016. Bacchetta et al. confirmed the conclusions of Ref. citebib.bib114 and motivated by the conclusion that the large-xūĚĎ•x region of quasi-PDFs converges much more slowly to the appropriate light-cone PDF, they proposed a procedure to aid the large-xūĚĎ•x extraction of PDFs from the quasi-distribution approach. This is a relevant aspect for computations of quasi-PDFs in Lattice QCD. The main idea is to combine the result of a quasi-PDF and that of the corresponding moments of PDFs. One divides the whole xūĚĎ•x-region into two intervals, with a ‚Äúmatching‚ÄĚ point x0subscriptūĚĎ•0x0. For x‚Č§x0ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎ•0xleqx0, one assumes that the computed quasi-PDF is already a good approximation to the standard PDF. In turn, for x‚Č•x0ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎ•0xgeqx0, a parametrization is used, with parameters fixed by conditions of smoothness at x0subscriptūĚĎ•0x0 (for the value of the quasi-PDF and for its derivative) and by the available moments of PDFs. The procedure to reconstruct unpolarized and helicity PDFs was tested numerically in the DSM for two matching points, x0=0.2, 0.3subscriptūĚĎ•00.20.3x0=0.2,,0.3 and two nucleon momenta, P3=1.47, 2.94subscriptūĚĎÉ31.472.94P3=1.47,,2.94 GeV. In all cases, there is significant improvement of agreement with respect to the standard PDF, especially at x0=0.2subscriptūĚĎ•00.2x0=0.2. Excellent agreement was observed for the dūĚĎĎd quark even for the lower nucleon momentum, while the uūĚĎĘu quark seems to require a larger value of P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3, which is due to the worse agreement of the quasi-PDF and the standard PDF at the matching point. Overall, the procedure was proven to be successful in the DSM and the Authors are hopeful that it can be effectively applied also for actual Lattice QCD data. The DSM (with scalar diquarks) was also employed as a framework for studying quasi-GPDs citebib.bib118 in 2018. S. Bhattacharya, C. Cocuzza and A. Metz calculated twist-2 unpolarized quasi-GPDs (the so-called HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE functions; for some definitions of GPDs variables and functions, see Sec. ), using two Dirac structures, ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 and ő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma3, motivated by the discovery of mixing for one of them citebib.bib105, bib.bib106. They verified that in the forward limit, their expressions reduce to the ones for the respective quasi-PDFs and in the infinite momentum limit, their quasi-GPDs approach the appropriate GPDs. They found that all results for quasi-distributions are continuous, and argued that this feature should hold also at higher twist in the DSM. The Authors also checked the reliability of the cut-diagram approach, widely used in spectator models, and concluded it does not reproduce certain terms appearing from handling the calculation exactly. Thus, this approach is a simplification that should be avoided when dealing with quasi-distributions. Having the final analytical expressions for quasi-GPDs and quasi-PDFs, they studied numerically the approach to infinite nucleon boost. They found that P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 of order 2 GeV and larger yields quasi-functions within ūĚí™‚Äč(10%)ūĚí™percent10O(10%) of their light-cone counterparts in a wide range of xūĚĎ•x. The problematic region, as for quasi-PDFs, is the large xūĚĎ•x regime, and the discrepancies increase for larger skewness őĺūĚúČxi. Interestingly, the derivation of matching for GPDs citebib.bib119, described shortly in Sec. , indicates that no matching is required for the EūĚźłE function (at leading order). However, in this model study, no significant differences in the convergence of the HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE functions were seen. In the ERBL region, ‚ąíőĺ<x<őĺūĚúČūĚĎ•ūĚúČ-xi<x<xi, the agreement with standard GPDs is good, provided that őĺūĚúČxi is not too small. The results for both Dirac structures were found to be very similar at large enough momentum (P3‚Č≥2greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscriptūĚĎÉ32P3gtrsim2 GeV). To verify and strenghten the conclusions, the Authors also checked the sensitivity to parameter variations (constituent mass and spectator mass) and found no significant differences. As numerical exploration of quasi-GPDs on the lattice is still missing, the DSM results can provide very useful guidance to such attempts.

A model investigation of quasi-PDFs was performed also by A. Radyushkin in 2016-17 citebib.bib75, bib.bib76. He used his formalism of virtuality distribution functions (VDFs) citebib.bib78, bib.bib79. In the VDF formalism, a generic diagram for a parton-hadron scattering corresponds to a double Fourier transform of the VDF, ő¶‚Äč(x,ŌÉ;M2)ő¶ūĚĎ•ūĚúésuperscriptūĚĎÄ2Phi(x,sigma;,M2), where ŌÉūĚúésigma is related to the parton virtuality (giving the name to the VDF) and MūĚĎÄM is the hadron mass. The variables conjugate in the double Fourier transform are x‚ÜĒp‚čÖz‚ÜĒūĚĎ•‚čÖūĚĎĚūĚĎßxleftrightarrow pcdot z (pūĚĎĚp ‚Äď hadron momentum, zūĚĎßz ‚Äď separation of fields) and ŌÉ‚ÜĒz2‚ÜĒūĚúésuperscriptūĚĎß2sigmaleftrightarrow z2. The VDF representation holds for any pūĚĎĚp and zūĚĎßz, but the case relevant for PDFs is the light cone projection, z2=0superscriptūĚĎß20z2=0. Then, one can define primordial (straight-link) TMDs and derive relations between VDFs, TMDs and quasi-PDFs. Working in a renormalizable theory, one can represent the VDF as a sum of a soft part, i. e. generating a non-perturbative evolution of PDFs, and a hard tail, vanishing with the inverse of ŌÉūĚúésigma. Numerical interest in these papers was in the investigation of the non-perturbative evolution generated by the soft part of the VDF or, equivalently, the soft part of the primordial TMD. Radyushkin considers two models thereof, with a Gaussian-type dependence on the transverse momentum (‚ÄúGaussian model‚ÄĚ) and a simple non-Gaussian model (‚Äúm=0ūĚĎö0m=0 model‚ÄĚ). These models are two extreme cases of a family of models, one with a too fast and one with a too slow fall-off in the impact parameter. In the numerical part of Ref. citebib.bib75, the formalism was applied to a simple model PDF, f‚Äč(x)=(1‚ąíx)3‚Äčőł‚Äč(x)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•superscript1ūĚĎ•3ūĚúÉūĚĎ•f(x)=(1-x)3theta(x). Both TMD models give similar evolution patterns, implying that one observes some universal features related to the properties of quasi-PDFs. It was also observed that the approach to the limiting PDF is not uniform for different xūĚĎ•x and it can even be non-monotonic for small nucleon momenta. These conclusions can provide very useful guidance to lattice QCD calculations, meaning e. g. that simple extrapolations in the inverse squared momentum might not be justifiable. In the work of Ref. citebib.bib76, Radyushkin considered target mass corrections (TMCs) in quasi-PDFs using the same framework. In both TMD models, it was found that TMCs become negligible already significantly before the quasi-PDF approaches the standard PDF. The Author suggested that, given the realistic precision of lattice simulations, TMCs can be neglected for nucleon boosts larger than around twice the nucleon mass.

Further model studies of the quasi-distribution approach were performed in 2017 by W. Broniowski and E. Ruiz-Arriola citebib.bib120, bib.bib121. In the first paper citebib.bib120, the pion quasi-DA and quasi-PDFs were computed in the framework of chiral quark models, namely the Nambu-Jona-Lasinio (NJL) citebib.bib122, bib.bib123 and the spectral quark model (SQM) citebib.bib124, bib.bib125, bib.bib126. The NJL model is a well-known toy model of QCD, which is a low-energy approximation to it and encompasses a mechanism of spontanous chiral symmetry breaking from the presence of strong four-quark interactions. The SQM model, in turn, is a spectral regularization of the chiral quark model based on the introduction of the Lehmann representation of the quark propagator. The Authors derived analytical expressions for the quasi-DA and the quasi-PDF, together with their underlying unintegrated versions dependent on the transverse momentum, as well as the ITDs. They also verified the relations between different kinds of distributions found by Radyushkin citebib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77. This allowed them also to study the approach of the quasi-DA and quasi-PDF towards their light-cone counterparts and they found clear convergence for pion momenta in the range of a few GeV. Moreover, a comparison to lattice data citebib.bib127 was made. For the NJL model, very good agreement was found with the lattice results at both considered pion momenta, P3=0.9, 1.3subscriptūĚĎÉ30.91.3P3=0.9,,1.3 GeV. In the case of the SQM model, similar agreement was observed at P3=1.3subscriptūĚĎÉ31.3P3=1.3 GeV and at the smaller momentum there were some differences between the model and the lattice data, but the agreement was still satisfactory. This implies that both models are able to capture the essential physical features. In the second paper citebib.bib121, Broniowski and Ruiz-Arriola explored further the relations between nucleon quasi-PDFs, PDFs and TMDs, following the work of Radyushkin citebib.bib75, bib.bib76. They derived certain sum rules, e. g. relating the moments of quasi-PDFs, PDFs and the width of TMDs. Furthermore, Broniowski and Ruiz-Arriola modeled the factorization separating the longitudinal and transverse parton dynamics. They applied this model to study the expected change of shape of ITDs and reduced ITDs, both for quarks and gluons. They also considered the breakdown of the longitudinal-transverse factorization induced by the evolution equations, in the context of consequences for present-day lattice simulations, finding that the effects should be rather mild in quasi-PDFs, but could be visible in ITDs. Finally, they also performed comparisons to actual lattice data of the ETM Collaboration citebib.bib108 for isovector unpolarized PDFs of the nucleon. The model quasi-PDFs, resulting from the assumed factorization, do not agree well with the ETMC data at 4 values of the nucleon boost between 0.94 and 2.36 GeV (see Sec. for more details about these results). The discrepancy was attributed to the large pion mass, shifting the distributions to the right of the phenomenological ones, and to other lattice systematics (see also discussion about the role of the pion mass in Sec. and Ref. citebib.bib128). More successful was the test of the aforementioned sum rule, predicting linearly increasing deviation of the second central moment of the quasi-PDF from that of the PDF with increasing 1/P321superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ321/P32, with the slope giving the TMD width. Using the ETMC data, they indeed observed the linear behavior and moreover, extrapolating to infinite momentum, they found the second central moment to be compatible with a phenomenological analysis. In the last part of the paper, the Authors offered considerations for the pion case, presenting predictions for the valence-quark quasi-PDFs and ITDs.

Meson DAs were first considered in the quasi-distribution formalism in 2015 by Y. Jia and X. Xiong citebib.bib129. They calculated the one-loop corrections to quasi-DAs and light-cone DAs employing the framework of non-relativistic QCD (NRQCD). This resulted to analytical formulae for quasi- and light-cone DAs for three SūĚĎÜS-wave charmonia: the pseudoscalar ő∑csubscriptūĚúāūĚĎźetac and both the longitudinally and transversely polarized vector J/ŌąūĚźĹūĚúďJ/psi. They checked analytically the convergence of quasi-DAs to standard DAs and performed also a numerical investigation of the rate of convergence. A function was introduced, called degree of resemblance, that quantifies the difference between the quasi and standard DAs. In general, momentum of around 3 times the meson mass is needed to bring the quasi-distribution to within 5% of the light-cone one. The Authors also considered first inverse moments of quasi and light-cone DAs, concluding that their rate of convergence is somewhat smaller and the difference at P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 equal to three times the hadron mass may still be of order 20%, with 5% reached at P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 six times larger than the meson mass. Following the NRQCD investigation, Y. Jia and X. Xiong continued their work related to model quasi-distributions of mesons. In 2018, together with S. Liang and R. Yu citebib.bib130, they presented results on meson quasi-PDFs and quasi-DAs in two-dimensional QCD in its large-NcsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎźNc limit, often referred to as the ‚Äôt Hooft model citebib.bib131. The Authors used the Hamiltonian operator approach and Bars-Green equations in equal-time quantization citebib.bib132, instead of the more standard diagrammatic approach in light-cone quantization. They performed a comprehensive study comparing the quasi-distributions and their light-cone counterparts, studying the approach of the former to the latter at increasing meson momentum. Among the most interesting conclusions is the observation that the approach to the standard distributions is slower for lighter mesons than for heavier quarkonia of Ref. citebib.bib129. This observation was illustrated with numerical studies of the derived analytical equations for the different distributions. It was found that for the pion, even momentum 8 times larger than the pion mass leads to significant discrepancies between the shapes of quasi-distributions and light-cone ones. For s‚Äčs¬ĮūĚĎ†¬ĮūĚĎ†sbars (c‚Äčc¬ĮūĚĎź¬ĮūĚĎźcbarc) meson, in turn, momentum of five (two) times the meson mass already leads to the two types of distributions almost coinciding. An analogous phenomenon is also beginning to emerge in lattice studies and provides a warning that e. g. pion PDFs might be more difficult to study than nucleon PDFs, i. e. require relatively larger momentum boosts. Additionally, Jia et al. studied both types of distributions in perturbation theory, thus being able to consider the matching between quasi- and light-cone PDFs/DAs. The very important aspect of this part is that they were able to verify one of the crucial features underlying LaMET ‚Äď that quasi- and light-cone distributions share the same infrared properties at leading order in 1/P31subscriptūĚĎÉ31/P3. This is interesting, because the two-dimensional model has a more severe IR divergence than standard QCD. As such, this work in two-dimensional QCD provides a benchmark for lattice studies of quasi-distributions in four-dimensional QCD. It is expected that many of the obtained conclusions regarding the ‚Äôt Hooft model hold also in standard QCD. Moreover, the setup can also be used to study other proposals for obtaining the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of light-cone distributions, in particular pseudo-PDFs and LCSs.
content_sections.THEORETICAL CHALLENGES OF QUASI-PDFS: In this section, we summarize the main theoretical challenges related to quasi-PDFs, that have been identified early on. Addressing and understanding these challenges was very critical in order to establish sound foundations for the quasi-distribution method. We concentrate on two of them, the role of the Euclidean signature (whether an equal-time correlator in euclidean spacetime can be related to light-cone parton physics in Minkowski) and renormalizability. The latter is not trivial due to the power-law divergence inherited from the Wilson line included in the non-local operator. It is clear that problems related to either challenge could lead to abandoning the whole programme for quasi-PDFs. Therefore, it was absolutely crucial to prove that both of these aspects do not hide insurmountable difficulties.
content_sections.THEORETICAL CHALLENGES OF QUASI-PDFS Euc: One of the crucial assumptions of the quasi-distribution approach is that these distributions computed on the lattice with Euclidean spacetime signature are the same as their Minkowski counterparts. In particular, they should share the collinear divergences, such that the UV differences can be matched using LaMET. In Ref. citebib.bib133, C. Monahan and K. Orginos considered the Mellin moments of bare PDFs and bare quasi-PDFs in the context of smeared quasi-distributions that differ from the standard ones only in the UV region, by construction (see Sec. for more details about the smeared quasi-PDFs). They found that the Wick rotation from the bare Euclidean quasi-PDF to the light-cone PDF is simple. However, in Ref. citebib.bib134, a perturbative investigation was performed by C. Carlson and M. Freid, who discovered that there are qualitative differences between loop corrections in Euclidean and Minkowski spacetimes. In particular, it seemed that the IR divergence of the light-cone PDF is absent in the Euclidean quasi-PDF, which would be a problem at the basic root of LaMET. The complication emerged in certain diagrams, because the integration contours along real and imaginary axes of the complex loop temporal momentum plane could not be linked by smooth deformation, with physical observables being related to the integration along real k0superscriptūĚĎė0k0 and the lattice objects being extracted from integration along the imaginary k0superscriptūĚĎė0k0 axis. The Authors gave also a physical intuition justifying this finding. The IR divergence in Minkowski spacetime comes from collinear configurations of nearly on-shell quarks and gluons, with quark mass preventing exactly parallel configuration. Such a parallel situation is not possible in Euclidean spacetime and hence, the Authors argued that no divergence can appear, invoking, thus, also mismatch of IR regions that could not be corrected for, perturbatively. The serious doubts about the importance of spacetime signature were addressed in Ref. citebib.bib135 by R. Brice√Īo, M. Hansen and C. Monahan. They formulated a general argument that for a certain class of matrix elements computed on the lattice, observables from the Euclidean-time dependence and from the LSZ reduction formula in Minkowski spacetime coincide.
