In closing, let us add that a proper determination of the renormalization functions computed non-perturbatively in an RI-type scheme (e. g. , the works presented in paragraphs and ) requires a few improvements. For once, dedicated calculations are needed on ensembles with all flavor quarks degenerate. These ensembles should correspond to the same value of the coupling constants as the ensembles used for the calculation of the hadron matrix elements. For instance, matrix elements obtained on Nf=2+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď21Nf=2+1 or Nf=2+1+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď211Nf=2+1+1 ensembles, should be renormalized using Nf=3subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď3Nf=3 and Nf=4subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď4Nf=4, respectively. Renormalization functions should then be computed on multiple ensembles with different quark masses, so the chiral limit can be taken. Several values of the RI scale (őľ0subscriptūĚúá0mu0) should be employed for each ensemble in order to take the (a‚Äčőľ0)2‚Üí0‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎésubscriptūĚúá020(amu0)2to 0 limit upon conversion to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS and at a common scale. This will eliminate residual dependence on the initial RI scale, and give more reliable estimates for the renormalization functions. We note that the extrapolation (a‚Äčőľ0)2‚Üí0‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎésubscriptūĚúá020(amu0)2to 0 has been performed in the work of Ref. citebib.bib156 (see left panel of Fig. ). Potential improvements could also be a two-loop conversion factor from an RI-type to MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme, and also a subtraction technique of finite aūĚĎéa effects using one-loop perturbation theory. This method was successfully employed for local operators of different lattice formulationscitebib.bib195, bib.bib167. It is anticipated that both aforementioned improvements will be available in the near future.
content_sections.RENORMALIZATION OF NON-LOCAL OPERATORS A: An alternative proposal for the renormalization of non-local operators is based on an auxiliary field method, a formulation also adopted to prove the renormalizability of the operators under studycitebib.bib139. The use of this approach is not new, but originates from other studies in the continuumcitebib.bib177, bib.bib178, adopting an auxiliary scalar field results to a pair of operators in an extended theory instead of the usual non-local operators. In this case, a renormalization prescription reduces to a three-parameter equation instead of a single equation for each zūĚĎßz value, which characterizes the RI-type renormalization. J. Green et al. presented in Ref. citebib.bib191 this non-perturbative approach and employed the twisted mass formulation on two ensembles that have pion mass of around 370 MeV and different lattice spacings (a=0.082,0.064ūĚĎé0.0820.064a=0.082,0.064 fm), in order to determine the three parameters of the auxiliary field renormalization scheme. The auxiliary scalar color triplet field (ő∂‚Äč(őĺ)ūĚúĀūĚúČzeta(xi)) is defined on the line x+őĺ‚ÄčnūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõx+xi n, where őĺūĚúČxi is the length of the Wilson line in physical units. The main component of the approach is the replacement of correlation functions with ones from the extended theory including the ő∂ūĚúĀzeta field, which involve the local color singlet bilinear Ōē‚Č°ő∂¬Į‚ÄčŌąitalic-Ōē¬ĮūĚúĀūĚúďphiequivbarzetapsi. The introduction of the auxiliary field requires modification of the action (for details, see Ref. citebib.bib191), which yields a bare propagator in a fixed gauge background: ‚ü®ő∂‚Äč(x+őĺ‚Äčn)‚Äčő∂¬Į‚Äč(x)‚ü©ő∂=őł‚Äč(őĺ)‚Äče‚ąím‚Äčőĺ‚ÄčW‚Äč(x+őĺ‚Äčn,x),m=a‚ąí1‚Äčlog‚Ā°(1+a‚Äčm0). formulae-sequencesubscriptdelimited-‚ü®‚ü©ūĚúĀūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõ¬ĮūĚúĀūĚĎ•ūĚúĀūĚúÉūĚúČsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎöūĚúČūĚĎäūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõūĚĎ•ūĚĎösuperscriptūĚĎé11ūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎö0leftzeta(x+xi n)barzeta(x)rightzeta=theta(xi)e-mxiW(x+xi n,x),,quad m=a-1log(1+am0),. In the above expression WūĚĎäW is a straight Wilson line between points xūĚĎ•x and x+őĺ‚ÄčnūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõx+xi n, and the exponent with the mass is an O‚Äč(a‚ąí1)ūĚĎāsuperscriptūĚĎé1O(a-1) counterterm. One obtains for the operator including the Wilson line, whose renormalization we are seeking: ūĚí™őď‚Äč(x,őĺ,n)=‚ü®Ōē¬Į‚Äč(x+őĺ‚Äčn)‚Äčőď‚ÄčŌē‚Äč(x)‚ü©ő∂,for‚Äčőĺ>0,m=0. formulae-sequencesubscriptūĚí™őďūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõsubscriptdelimited-‚ü®‚ü©¬Įitalic-ŌēūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõőďitalic-ŌēūĚĎ•ūĚúĀformulae-sequenceforūĚúČ0ūĚĎö0OGamma(x,xi,n)=leftbarphi(x+xi n)Gammaphi(x)rightzeta,,quadrm for,,xi>0,,,m=0,. Besides the counterterm m0subscriptūĚĎö0m0, the renormalization functions of the bilinear Ōēitalic-Ōēphi (ZŌēsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-ŌēZphi) and the operator ūĚí™őďsubscriptūĚí™őďOGamma (ZūĚí™őďsubscriptūĚĎćsubscriptūĚí™őďZOGamma) must be calculated. Due to mixing allowed by the breaking of chiral symmetry, a proper renormalization is in this case: ŌēR=ZŌē‚Äč(Ōē+rmix‚ÄčnŐł‚ÄčŌē),Ōē¬ĮR=ZŌē‚Äč(Ōē¬Į+rmix‚ÄčŌē¬Į‚ÄčnŐł). formulae-sequencesubscriptitalic-ŌēūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-Ōēitalic-ŌēsubscriptūĚĎümixitalic-nŐłitalic-Ōēsubscript¬Įitalic-ŌēūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-Ōē¬Įitalic-ŌēsubscriptūĚĎümix¬Įitalic-Ōēitalic-nŐłphiR=Zphileft(phi+rtextmixnotnphiright),quadbarphiR=Zphileft(barphi+rtextmixbarphinotnright),. A different basis of operators may be employed to achieve diagonal renormalization in a mixing matrix, that is ūĚí™őďR‚Äč(x,őĺ,n)=ZŌē2‚Äče‚ąím‚Äč|őĺ|‚ÄčūĚí™őď‚Ä≤‚Äč(x,őĺ,n),őď‚Ä≤=őď+rmix‚Äčsgn‚Ā°(őĺ)‚ÄčnŐł,őď+rmix2‚ÄčnŐł‚Äčőď‚ÄčnŐł. formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscriptūĚí™őďūĚĎÖūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-Ōē2superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎöūĚúČsubscriptūĚí™superscriptőď‚Ä≤ūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎõsuperscriptőď‚Ä≤őďsubscriptūĚĎümixsgnūĚúČitalic-nŐłőďsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎümix2italic-nŐłőďitalic-nŐłbegingatheredOGammaR(x,xi,n)=Zphi2e-m|xi|OGammaprime(x,xi,n), Gammaprime=Gamma+rtextmixoperatornamesgn(xi)notn,Gamma+rtextmix2notnGammanotn. endgathered As can be seen from the equations above, the renormalization of ūĚí™őďsubscriptūĚí™őďOGamma requires knowledge of the linearly divergent mūĚĎöm, the log-divergent ZŌēsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-ŌēZphi, and the finite rmixsubscriptūĚĎümixrtextmix. Note that rmixsubscriptūĚĎümixrtextmix is of similar nature as the mixing identified in Ref. citebib.bib106. In addition, this approach is not applicable for őĺ=0ūĚúČ0xi=0, in which case ūĚí™őďsubscriptūĚí™őďOGamma is a local operator and its renormalization can be extracted from standard RI-type techniques. In the work of Ref. citebib.bib191, the Authors renormalized nucleon matrix elements obtained from two ensembles of Nf=2+1+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď211Nf=2+1+1 twisted mass fermions. For extracting the renormalization functions, they used ensembles of four degenerate quarks (Nf=4subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď4Nf=4) as expected for mass independent renormalization schemes. However, the chiral limit is yet to be taken for this approach. In summary, the three parameters and the auxiliary field renormalization, Ső∂subscriptūĚĎÜūĚúĀSzeta, are determined by the RI-xMOM conditions ‚ąídd‚Äčőĺ‚Äčlog‚Ā°Tr‚Ā°Ső∂‚Äč(őĺ)|őĺ=őĺ0+m=0,evaluated-atūĚĎĎūĚĎĎūĚúČTrsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚúĀūĚúČūĚúČsubscriptūĚúČ0ūĚĎö0displaystyle-fracddxilogoperatornameTrSzeta(xi)Bigr|xi=xi0+m=0, [Ző∂3‚ÄčTr‚Ā°Ső∂‚Äč(őĺ0)]2=Ző∂3‚ÄčTr‚Ā°Ső∂‚Äč(2‚Äčőĺ0),superscriptdelimited-subscriptūĚĎćūĚúĀ3TrsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚúĀsubscriptūĚúČ02subscriptūĚĎćūĚúĀ3TrsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚúĀ2subscriptūĚúČ0displaystyleleft[fracZzeta3operatornameTrSzeta(xi0)right]2=fracZzeta3operatornameTrSzeta(2xi0), 16‚ÄčZŌē¬ĪZő∂‚ÄčZŌą‚Äč‚Ąú‚Ā°Tr‚Ā°[Ső∂‚ąí1‚Äč(őĺ0)‚ÄčG¬Ī‚Äč(őĺ0,p0)‚ÄčSŌą‚ąí1‚Äč(p0)]=1,16superscriptsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-Ōēplus-or-minussubscriptūĚĎćūĚúĀsubscriptūĚĎćūĚúďTrsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚúĀ1subscriptūĚúČ0superscriptūĚźļplus-or-minussubscriptūĚúČ0subscriptūĚĎĚ0superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚúď1subscriptūĚĎĚ01displaystylefrac16fracZphipmsqrtZzetaZpsiReoperatornameTrleft[Szeta-1(xi0)Gpm(xi0,p0)Spsi-1(p0)right]=1, where SŌąsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚúďSpsi is the usual fermion field renormalization obtained from a standard RI-type prescription. As in the case of the non-perturbative schemes described in the previous paragraphs, a prescription is needed to bring the renormalized quasi-PDFs into the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme and a conversion factor is necessary. This has been computed in DR to one-loop level in perturbation theory and the formula is given in Ref. citebib.bib191. Here, we present selected results from Ref. citebib.bib191 and Fig. showing the quantity in Eq. (left panel) for the two ensembles discussed above with a=0.082ūĚĎé0.082a=0.082fm (ő≤=1.95ūĚõĹ1.95beta=1.95) and a=0.064ūĚĎé0.064a=0.064fm (ő≤=2.10ūĚõĹ2.10beta=2.10). As can be seen, smearing of the gauge links reduces the statistical noice, but more importantly reduces the difference between the two ensembles. This is an evidence of reduction of the linear divergence. In case of no mixing (axial operator ő≥5‚Äčő≥őľsuperscriptūĚõĺ5superscriptūĚõĺūĚúágamma5gammamu), rmixsubscriptūĚĎümixrrm mix is not relevant and only m0subscriptūĚĎö0m0 and ZŌēsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-ŌēZphi need to be determined. ZŌēsubscriptūĚĎćitalic-ŌēZphi is shown in the right panel of Fig. upon conversion to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme and evolution to the scale 2GeV. The one-loop conversion factor removes the bulk of the dependence on the scheme parameter |p|‚ÄčőĺūĚĎĚūĚúČ|p|xi, and the two-loop evolution removes most of the dependence on the scale |p|ūĚĎĚ|p|.
content_sections.RENORMALIZATION OF NON-LOCAL OPERATORS O: In this subsection, we review some other developments related to the renormalization of PDF-related operators, in particular the Wilson-line-induced power divergence. In 2016, the idea of removing such divergence by smearing was proposed by C. Monahan and K. Orginos citebib.bib133. It takes advantage of the properties of the gradient flow (GF), introduced by M. L√ľscher a few years ago citebib.bib196, bib.bib197 and applied to many problems in Lattice QCD citebib.bib198. As shown by L√ľscher and P. Weisz in Ref. citebib.bib199, it defines a 4+1-dimensional field theory, wherein the extra dimension is the flow time. The crucial property of GF, proven to all orders in perturbation theory, is that the correlation functions defined at non-zero flow time are finite after usual renormalization of the 4-dimensional theory. As such, GF defines a renormalization scheme with the flow time, ŌĄūĚúŹtau, being the renormalization scale. Thus, using GF, one can define smeared quasi-distributions, which are finite, in particular devoid of the power divergence from the presence of the Wilson line. Note that GF only regulates the UV behavior, leaving the IR structure intact, which is a prerequisite for factorization. The quasi-PDF results in the GF scheme can be converted perturbatively to other renormalization schemes or directly matched to light-cone PDFs, e. g. in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. The Authors of Ref. citebib.bib133 demonstrated a simple relation between the moments of the smeared quasi-PDF and the renormalized moments of the light-cone PDF. For this relation to be valid and to allow matching to light-cone PDFs, the scales in the problem have to satisfy MN‚Č™P3‚Č™ŌĄ‚ąí1/2much-less-thansubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀsubscriptūĚĎÉ3much-less-thansuperscriptūĚúŹ12MNll P3lltau-1/2. Apart from usual higher-twist corrections, ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2/P32)ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ32O(Lambdarm QCD2/P32), there are also corrections of ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2‚ÄčŌĄ)ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2ūĚúŹO(Lambdarm QCD2tau). The explicit one-loop perturbative analysis of smeared quasi-PDFs was performed, in 2017, by C. Monahan citebib.bib200. It was shown that indeed the IR divergences of smeared quasi and light-cone PDFs are the same. The perturbative computation led in the end to establishing the matching equation that could be used to extract light-cone PDFs from a lattice computation. An interesting aspect also shown by Monahan is that the smeared matrix element satisfies an relation akin to a usual renormalization group equation. This could, in principle, allow a non-perturbative step-scaling procedure to be defined, which would connect lattice-extracted matrix elements to high scales at which matching could be performed with much reduced truncation effects. Smeared operators are the fundament of another method, introduced in 2012 by Z. Davoudi and M. Savage citebib.bib201. It aims at calculations of arbitrarily many moments of PDFs or other structure functions, that could, in principle, allow to reconstruct the full distributions. The main idea is to avoid the power-divergent mixings with lower-dimensional operators in higher moments by removing their source ‚Äď the breaking of rotational invariance by the lattice. The paper considers a mechanism for the restoration of this symmetry in the continuum limit of lattice field theories, in particular the őĽ‚ÄčŌē4ūĚúÜsuperscriptitalic-Ōē4lambdaphi4 theory and QCD. In general, the interpolating operators that are used to excite a hadron do not have definite angular momentum, i. e. it is not possible to assign a well-defined angular momentum to a lattice state and the latter is a linear combination of infinitely many different angular momentum states. The essence of the approach of Ref. citebib.bib201 is to construct appropriate operators on the hypercubic lattice with maximum overlap with states with definite angular momentum in the continuum. Such operators are constructed on multiple lattice sites using smearing that renders the contributions of both lower and higher dimensional operators subleading and totally suppressed in the continuum limit. The Authors performed detailed calculations in the őĽ‚ÄčŌē4ūĚúÜsuperscriptitalic-Ōē4lambdaphi4 theory demonstrating the mechanism. For the QCD case, things are complicated by the gauge symmetry. However, Davoudi and Savage showed that the idea can also be applied for this case, relevant for moments of partonic functions. Apart from smearing of the operators, the essential ingredient is tadpole improvement. Recently, this approach has been revisited and exploratory numerical results were recently presentedcitebib.bib202 We finalize by shortly discussing one more method of dealing with the power divergence related to the Wilson line in quasi-distributions. In 2016, H. -n. Li proposed citebib.bib203 to modify the definition of such distributions by using ‚Äúnondipolar‚ÄĚ gauge links, i. e. two pieces of links oriented in orthogonal directions. He showed with an explicit calculation of one-loop corrections that the linear divergence of the standard quasi-approach (with dipolar links) is absent in such a case and the IR region is untouched. In general, the hadron boost direction needs to differ from the direction of the Wilson line links to avoid the linear divergence. However, due to developments in the renormalization of the power divergence (and other divergences present in quasi-distributions), in particular the full non-perturbative renormalization, this interesting idea of Li has not been implemented in numerical calculations. Clearly, the implementation itself is possible, but much less practical than with straight links for the standard definition. It is also worth to mention that Ref. citebib.bib203 discussed also a potential problem with two-loop factorization of standard quasi-PDFs (but absent in the nondipolar ones). The power divergence in such setup induces an additional collinear divergence at the two-loop order, rendering the matching kernel IR divergent and breaking the factorization. However, the problem does not appear if the power divergence is properly renormalized citebib.bib170.
content_sections.MATCHING OF QUASI-PDFS TO LIGHT-CONE PDF: In this section, we focus on the matching from quasi-PDFs to light-cone PDFs. Since the inception of LaMET, there has been a lot of effort devoted to understanding many aspects of this procedure. In particular, the first matching paper citebib.bib98, discussed in Sec. , considered the non-singlet quark quasi- and light-cone PDFs in a simple transverse momentum cutoff scheme. Later work concentrated on matching from different renormalization schemes to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme, on the issue of particle number conservation and on observables different than non-singlet quark PDFs, in particular gluon PDFs, singlet quark PDFs, GPDs, TMDs and meson DAs. We review all of these below and we also include a discussion on the matching of pseudo-PDFs/ITDs. For convenience, we repeat here the general factorization formula for the matching: q‚Äč(x,őľP3)=‚ąę‚ąí11d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčC‚Äč(xy,őľ|y|‚ÄčP3)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ2)+ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2P32,MN2P32),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉ3subscriptsuperscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúáūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚúá2ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ32superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ32tildeqleft(x,fracmuP3right)=int1-1fracdy|y|,C!left(fracxy,fracmu|y|P3right)q(y,mu2)+Oleft(fracLambdarm QCD2P32,fracMN2P32right),, where őľūĚúámu is the common factorization and renormalization scale, and the second argument of the matching kernel CūĚź∂C emphasizes that the relevant momentum is that of a parton. Let us first briefly revisit the early attempt to remove the Wilson-line-related power divergence, discussed in Sec. , from the point of view of the matching process. We will then move to the presentation of the matching of MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS-renormalized and RI-renormalized quasi-PDFs. T. Ishikawa et al. discussed in Ref. citebib.bib136 that the counterterm that subtracts this divergence to all orders in the coupling can be provided by an independent lattice observable that shares the same power divergence as the non-local operator defining the quasi-PDF. It was noted that a natural and simple choice for such an observable is the static q‚Äčq¬ĮūĚĎě¬ĮūĚĎěqbarq potential. The Authors calculated the matching (to PDFs in the UV cutoff scheme) in one-loop lattice perturbation theory for the case of naive fermions. The idea was also followed in Ref. citebib.bib182, where J. -W. Chen, X. Ji and J. -H. Zhang defined improved quasi-PDFs with the power divergence, calculated e. g. from the static potential, subtracted. The modification amounts to multiplication of bare matrix elements by an exponential factor, exp‚Ā°(‚ąíőī‚Äčm‚Äč|z|)ūĚõŅūĚĎöūĚĎßexp(-delta m|z|). The matching formulae of Ref. citebib.bib98 are then modified by ignoring the terms containing the cutoff őõőõLambda.

One of the possibilities of renormalizing the quasi-PDF is to obtain it in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. Obviously, this scheme cannot be directly applied on the lattice and hence, non-perturbative renormalization of lattice matrix elements proceeds via an intermediate scheme, like a variant of RI (see Sec. ). Having renormalization functions in such an intermediate scheme, one then converts them perturbatively to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme and evolves to some reference scale, like 2 GeV. The last step is the Fourier transform that yields the quasi-PDF in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. The first paper that considered the matching from MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS quasi-PDF was Ref. citebib.bib146 by W. Wang, S. Zhao and R. Zhu. The Authors presented complete matching for quarks and gluons, that we discuss more below in Sec. . For the case of non-singlet quark PDFs (with őď=ő≥3őďsubscriptūĚõĺ3Gamma=gamma3 or őď=ő≥5‚Äčő≥3őďsubscriptūĚõĺ5subscriptūĚõĺ3Gamma=gamma5gamma3 Dirac structure), it was found that the change with respect to Ref. citebib.bib98 is simple ‚Äď the terms with the transverse momentum cutoff őõőõLambda do not appear and there is a modified polynomial dependence in the physical region of quasi-PDFs. Explicitly, the matching kernel reads: CRef. Wangetal. MS¬Į‚Äč(őĺ,őľ|y|‚ÄčP3)=subscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂¬ĮMSRef. Wangetal. ūĚúČūĚúáūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎÉ3absentdisplaystyle Coverlinerm MStextRef. cite[cite]@@bibrefAuthors Phrase1YearPhrase2Wang:2017qyg@@citephrase(@@citephrase)!!left(xi,muover|y|P3right)!= őī‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ)+őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(1+őĺ21‚ąíőĺ‚Äčln‚Ā°őĺőĺ‚ąí1+1)+őĺ>1(1+őĺ21‚ąíőĺ‚Äčln‚Ā°y2‚ÄčP32őľ2‚Äč(4‚Äčőĺ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))+őĺ2‚ąí5‚Äčőĺ+21‚ąíőĺ)+01 displaystyleleft(1+xi2over 1-xilny2P32overmu2big(4xi(1-xi)big)+xi2-5xi+2over 1-xiright)+,01ūĚúČ1xi>1) or 3/2‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ)321ūĚúČ3/2(1-xi) (for őĺ1(1+őĺ21‚ąíőĺ‚Äčln‚Ā°y2‚ÄčP32őľ2‚Äč(4‚Äčőĺ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))‚ąíőĺ‚Äč(1+őĺ)1‚ąíőĺ+2‚ÄčőĻ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))+01[10.0pt] displaystyleleft(1+xi2over 1-xilny2P32overmu2big(4xi(1-xi)big)-xi(1+xi)over 1-xi+2iota(1-xi)right)+,01(1+őĺ21‚ąíőĺ‚Äč[ln‚Ā°y2‚ÄčP32őľ2‚Äč(4‚Äčőĺ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))‚ąí1]+1+2‚ÄčőĻ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ)+32‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))+01 displaystyle!left(1+xi2over 1-xi!left[lny2P32overmu2big(4xi(1-xi)big)!-!1right]!+1+2iota(1-xi)+3over 2(1-xi)!right)+!!!0!1[1+őĺ21‚ąíőĺ‚Äčln‚Ā°y2‚ÄčP32őľ2‚Äč(4‚Äčőĺ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))‚ąíőĺ‚Äč(1+őĺ)1‚ąíőĺ+2‚ÄčőĻ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ)]+01[10.0pt] displaystyle!left[frac1+xi21-xilnfracy2P32mu2left(4xi(1-xi)right)-fracxi(1+xi)1-xi+2iota(1-xi)right]+!!01[2‚Äčőĺ1‚ąíőĺ‚Äčln‚Ā°y2‚ÄčP32őľ2‚Äč(4‚Äčőĺ‚Äč(1‚ąíőĺ))‚ąí2‚Äčőĺ1‚ąíőĺ]+01[10.0pt] displaystyleleft[frac2xi1-xilnfracy2P32mu2(4xi(1-xi))-frac2xi1-xiright]+,0<xi<1. [10.0pt] displaystyleleft[-frac2xi1-xilnfracxixi-1+frac21-xiright]+,xi<0endarrayright. The formula for the transversity case is not the same as the unpolarized and helicity distributions due to the different splitting function, different polynomial dependence in the physical region and different term added in the non-physical regions to renormalize the UV divergence in the self-energy corrections. An alternative way of bringing the results from the intermediate RI renormalization scheme to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme is to match directly the RI-renormalized quasi-PDFs onto the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS light-cone PDFs. This way was advocated by I. Stewart and Y. Zhao citebib.bib171, including the derivation of the relevant formulae. Such one-step procedure can be used as the sole means of obtaining light-cone PDFs or compared to the two-step procedure (first conversion to MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS and evolution to a reference scale and matching as the second step), with differences in the final PDFs taken as a measure of systematic uncertainty. Both procedures have been derived to one-loop order in perturbation theory, but clearly they can differ in the magnitude of neglected higher-order contributions. The derivation of the RI‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üíabsent¬ĮMSrightarrowoverlinerm MS matching is somewhat more complicated than for the MS¬Į‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üí¬ĮMS¬ĮMSoverlinerm MSrightarrowoverlinerm MS case. Stewart and Zhao presented it (for the őď=ő≥3őďsubscriptūĚõĺ3Gamma=gamma3 or őď=ő≥5‚Äčő≥3őďsubscriptūĚõĺ5subscriptūĚõĺ3Gamma=gamma5gamma3 Dirac structures) in the general covariant gauge, including the practically relevant case of the Landau gauge, typically implemented on the lattice. They also showed a detailed numerical study of the dependence on the choice of the gauge and on the initial and final scales. While the MS¬Į‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üí¬ĮMS¬ĮMSoverlinerm MSrightarrowoverlinerm MS matching has only one scale involved, the RI‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üíabsent¬ĮMSrightarrowoverlinerm MS case depends on three scales: the final MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scale and the two scales of the RI scheme: the overall scale and the scale defined by the momentum in the 333-direction. Explicit checks showed that when aiming at a result at some reference MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scale, the dependence on the intermediate RI scales is rather small. It is important to note that the RI‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üíabsent¬ĮMSrightarrowoverlinerm MS conserves the particle number and also that the problem with the UV divergence in self-energy corrections does not appear, since the RI scheme introduces a counterterm to the quasi-PDF that cancels this divergence. Results for the őď=ő≥0őďsubscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0 case and for transversity PDFs matching were presented in Refs. citebib.bib204, bib.bib49 by Y. -S. Liu et al. For final RI‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üíabsent¬ĮMSrightarrowoverlinerm MS matching formulae, we refer to the original publications.

In this section, we review other developments in the matching of quasi-distributions to their light-cone counterparts. We also shortly discuss the matching process for the pseudo-PDFs/ITDs. GPDs. Apart from PDFs, also other kinds of parton distributions can be accessed on the lattice via LaMET. Already in 2015, matching was worked out for GPDs ‚Äď for (non-singlet) unpolarized and helicity in Ref. citebib.bib119 and for transversity in Ref. citebib.bib205. In both papers, the transverse momentum cutoff was used, as in the first paper for the matching of PDFs. The lattice matrix elements are extracted in a similar way as for quasi-PDFs, but there is momentum transfer in the boost direction between the source and the sink, őĒ3superscriptőĒ3Delta3. The obtained quasi-GPD can be decomposed into two functions, ‚Ąč‚Äč(x,őĺ,t,P3)‚ĄčūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎÉ3H(x,xi,t,P3) and ‚Ąį‚Äč(x,őĺ,t,P3)‚ĄįūĚĎ•ūĚúČūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎÉ3E(x,xi,t,P3) for the unpolarized case, ‚Ąč‚ĄčtildeH, ‚Ąį‚ĄįtildeE for helicity (chiral-even) and four functions ‚ĄčTsubscript‚ĄčūĚĎáHT, ‚ĄčTsubscript‚ĄčūĚĎátildeHT, ‚ĄįTsubscript‚ĄįūĚĎáET, ‚ĄįTsubscript‚ĄįūĚĎátildeET for transversity (chiral-odd), where the additional variables with respect to standard PDFs are őĺ=őĒ3/2‚ÄčP3ūĚúČsuperscriptőĒ32subscriptūĚĎÉ3xi=Delta3/2P3 and t=őĒ2ūĚĎ°superscriptőĒ2t=Delta2. For őĺ=t=0ūĚúČūĚĎ°0xi=t=0, the ‚Ąč‚Äč(x,0,0)‚ĄčūĚĎ•00H(x,0,0), ‚Ąč‚Äč(x,0,0)‚ĄčūĚĎ•00tildeH(x,0,0) and ‚ĄčT‚Äč(x,0,0)subscript‚ĄčūĚĎáūĚĎ•00HT(x,0,0) quasi-functions become the standard quasi-PDFs and all the ‚Ąį‚ĄįE functions and ‚ĄčTsubscript‚ĄčūĚĎátildeHT have no quasi-PDF counterparts. After matching, the xūĚĎ•x-integrals of unpolarized HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE give the Dirac and Pauli form factors F1‚Äč(t)subscriptūĚźĻ1ūĚĎ°F1(t) and F2‚Äč(t)subscriptūĚźĻ2ūĚĎ°F2(t), respectively. The xūĚĎ•x-integrals of helicity HūĚźĽtildeH and EūĚźłtildeE give the generalized axial and pseudoscalar form factors GA‚Äč(t)subscriptūĚźļūĚźīūĚĎ°GA(t) and GP‚Äč(t)subscriptūĚźļūĚĎÉūĚĎ°GP(t). Finally, the first moments of transversity GPDs give the generalized tensor form factors GT‚Äč(t)subscriptūĚźļūĚĎáūĚĎ°GT(t), AT‚Äč10‚Äč(t)subscriptūĚźīūĚĎá10ūĚĎ°tildeAT10(t), BT‚Äč10‚Äč(t)subscriptūĚźĶūĚĎá10ūĚĎ°BT10(t) and BT‚Äč10‚Äč(t)=0subscriptūĚźĶūĚĎá10ūĚĎ°0tildeBT10(t)=0, for HTsubscriptūĚźĽūĚĎáHT, HTsubscriptūĚźĽūĚĎátildeHT, ETsubscriptūĚźłūĚĎáET and ETsubscriptūĚźłūĚĎátildeET, respectively. In the papers citebib.bib119, bib.bib205, it was shown that the matching is non-trivial for the functions ‚Ąč‚ĄčH, ‚Ąč‚ĄčtildeH and ‚ĄčTsubscript‚ĄčūĚĎáHT and reduces to the matching for the corresponding quasi-PDFs in the forward limit, as expected. In turn, the matching kernel for all the ‚Ąį‚ĄįE functions is a trivial őīūĚõŅdelta-function at leading order in the coupling. The fourth transversity quasi-GPD, ‚ĄčTsubscript‚ĄčūĚĎátildeHT, is power-suppressed by the hadron momentum and omitted at leading power accuracy. We refer to the original publications for the final matching formulae. It is worth to mention that for quasi-PDFs, the formulae decompose into three intervals in xūĚĎ•x, the physical one and two non-physical ones outside of the partonic support for xūĚĎ•x, whereas for quasi-GPDs there are, in general, four intervals with different matching functions for the physical ERBL (‚ąíőĺ<x<őĺūĚúČūĚĎ•ūĚúČ-xi<x<xi) and DGLAP (‚ąí1<x<‚ąíőĺ1ūĚĎ•ūĚúČ-1<x<-xi and őĺ<x<1ūĚúČūĚĎ•1xi<x<1) regions. Complete matching for quark and gluon PDFs. In 2017, the first calculation of the matching of gluon quasi-PDFs to light-cone PDFs was done by W. Wang, S. Zhao and R. Zhu citebib.bib146. This paper was already discussed in the previous subsection in the context of MS¬Į‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üí¬ĮMS¬ĮMSoverlinerm MSrightarrowoverlinerm MS matching for non-singlet quark PDFs. However, the aim of the paper was more broad ‚Äď to consider the complete matching for quark and gluon quasi-PDFs. The Authors used two ways to regulate UV divergences: the UV cutoff scheme and DR, and also two ways for IR divergences: finite gluon mass and offshellness. The gluon quasi-PDF was defined as the Fourier transform of a boosted nucleon matrix element of two gluon field strength tensors Főľ‚ÄčőĹsubscriptūĚźĻūĚúáūĚúąFmunu displaced by length zūĚĎßz and connected with a Wilson line in the adjoint representation, ‚ü®P|Fi‚Äč3‚Äč(z)‚ÄčW‚Äč(z)‚ÄčFi‚Äč3‚Äč|P‚ü©quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎĖ3ūĚĎßūĚĎäūĚĎßsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎĖ30ūĚĎÉ P|Fi3(z)widetildeW(z)Fi3|P, with a sum over transverse directions, i=1,2ūĚĎĖ12i=1,2. The Authors calculated the one-loop expressions for gluon quasi-PDFs and light-cone PDFs in the UV cutoff scheme and confirmed they have the same infrared structure, with IR divergences present only in the physical xūĚĎ•x region, as expected. They also noted the presence of a linear divergence in the quasi-distribution, however, related in the gluon case not only to the presence of the Wilson line. Having performed the calculation in the UV cutoff scheme, they pointed to a difficulty in the self-energy diagram, coming from the breaking of gauge invariance by this scheme. This motivated further computations in DR, performed both for quark and gluon quasi- and light-cone PDFs. They considered all possible cases of quark-in-quark, gluon-in-gluon, gluon-in-quark and quark-in-gluon distribution functions, required for the complete matching. The quark-in-quark matching, the only relevant for the non-singlet quark distributions, has already been described above, see Eq. . Together with the derived equations for the other cases, one is ready to write the final matching formula: fi|H‚Äč(x,P3)=‚ąę‚ąí11d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčZi‚Äčj‚Äč(xy,őľP3)‚Äčfj|H‚Äč(y,őľ),subscriptūĚĎďconditionalūĚĎĖūĚźĽūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉ3superscriptsubscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎćūĚĎĖūĚĎóūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉ3subscriptūĚĎďconditionalūĚĎóūĚźĽūĚĎ¶ūĚúátildefi|H(x,P3)=int-11fracdy|y|,Zijleft(fracxy,fracmuP3right)fj|H(y,mu),, where fūĚĎďf (fūĚĎďtildef) denotes the light-cone (quasi) distribution. The indices i,j=q,gformulae-sequenceūĚĎĖūĚĎóūĚĎěūĚĎĒi,j=q,g and the four cases mentioned above correspond to matching kernels Zq‚ÄčqsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎěūĚĎěZqq, Zg‚ÄčgsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎĒūĚĎĒZgg, Zg‚ÄčqsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎĒūĚĎěZgq and Zq‚ÄčgsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎěūĚĎĒZqg, respectively. The matching equation implies mixing under matching between quark and gluon distributions, which can only be avoided in non-singlet quark distributions. Finally, the Authors derived P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 evolution formulae for quasi-distributions that turned out to be the DGLAP evolution equations of light-cone PDFs. In a follow-up work citebib.bib140, W. Wang and S. Zhao considered in more detail the issue of the power divergence in quasi-gluon PDFs, see Sec. for more details from the point of view of renormalizability. As remarked above, linear divergences exist also in one-loop diagrams without a Wilson line, which means that the divergence can not be absorbed into the renormalization of the Wilson line. The adopted definition of the gluon quasi-PDF was slightly modified with respect to Ref. citebib.bib146 by extending the sum in ‚ü®P|Főľ‚Äč3‚Äč(z)‚ÄčW‚Äč(z)‚ÄčFőľ‚Äč3‚Äč|P‚ü©quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚźĻūĚúá3ūĚĎßūĚĎäūĚĎßsubscriptūĚźĻūĚúá30ūĚĎÉ P|Fmu 3(z)widetildeW(z)Fmu 3|P from the transverse directions to all directions except the direction of the boost, i. e. őľ=0,1,2ūĚúá012mu=0,1,2. The calculation of one-loop corrections to quasi-gluon distributions was performed in a UV cutoff scheme, with the cutoff interpreted as the lattice cutoff. The Authors included diagrams arising in lattice perturbation theory (counterterm from the measure in the path integral and quark and ghost tadpoles) that preserve the gauge invariance, broken in the naive cutoff scheme. The main result of the paper, derived in the auxiliary field formalism, is that the linear divergences can be renormalized by considering the contribution from operator mixing (only with certain gluonic operators, i. e. no mixing with quark quasi-PDFs occurs) and the mass counterterm of the Wilson line. This allowed the Authors to define an improved quasi-gluon PDF with matrix elements multiplied by exp‚Ā°(‚ąíőī‚Äčm‚Äč|z|)ūĚõŅūĚĎöūĚĎßexp(-delta m|z|), where the mass counterterm can be determined non-perturbatively, and with a subtraction of the mixing calculated in perturbation theory. In addition to the one-loop calculation, they discussed two-loop corrections and conjectured that they hold to all orders. Finally, they provided the formula for the one-loop matching of the improved gluon quasi-PDF, which is IR finite and free from the linear UV divergence. The proof of renormalizability to all orders was indeed provided a few months later by the same Authors, together with J. -H. Zhang, X. Ji and A. Sch√§fer citebib.bib141 (see Sec. for more details on this paper and another proof of renormalizability of gluon quasi-PDFs citebib.bib142). From the point of view of matching, the important contribution of this paper was to confirm that the conclusions of Ref. citebib.bib140 hold when using gauge-invariant DR instead of the UV cutoff scheme. Moreover, it was pointed out that one can construct gluonic operators that are multiplicatively renormalizable, i. e. they evince no mixing under renormalization. However, mixing still occurs at the level of matching, as in Eq. . The Authors wrote schematic matching equations for the proposed non-perturbatively renormalized gluon quasi-PDFs in the RI/MOM scheme, postponing the calculation of the matching kernels RI‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üíabsent¬ĮMSrightarrowoverlinerm MS to a forthcoming publication. The latter computation, as well as the alternative possibility of RI‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üíabsent¬ĮMSrightarrowoverlinerm MS conversion and MS¬Į‚ÜíMS¬Į‚Üí¬ĮMS¬ĮMSoverlinerm MSrightarrowoverlinerm MS matching, will open the prospect of obtaining light-cone gluon PDFs in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme from the lattice. TMDs. Yet another important class of partonic distributions that can, in principle, be accessed on a Euclidean lattice is TMDs. The quasi-TMDs were considered already in 2014 by X. Ji and collaborators in Ref. citebib.bib206. They performed a one-loop perturbative calculation of quasi-TMDs in the Drell-Yan process. The crucial subtlety that makes the TMD case much more cumbersome than the PDF case is the subtraction of the soft term. It needs to be constructed in a such a way to make it computable on the lattice. It is related to the presence of a light-cone singularity in TMDs. The unsubtracted matrix element for quasi-TMDs, q‚Äč(x,kT)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎėūĚĎáq(x,kT), is defined as the correlation between a quark and an antiquark in a boosted nucleon, with the quark fields spatially separated by a distance zūĚĎßz and connected by two gauge links: one going from the quark field to infinity (for Drell-Yan) and the second one from infinity to the antiquark (in the covariant gauge; in the axial gauge an explicit link at infinity is additionally needed). The TMD depends on the longitudinal momentum fraction, xūĚĎ•x, and the transverse momentum, kTsubscriptūĚĎėūĚĎákT, where the latter is often exchanged for the impact parameter, bTsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎábT, via a two-dimensional Fourier transform. Having defined the quasi-TMD, the Authors proposed a lattice-calculable subtraction of the soft factor. The latter was conjectured to also play an important role in the two-loop matching for quasi-PDFs, where it could be handled similarly. Further, they proceeded with the derivation of the one-loop formulae and demonstrated the one-loop factorization. Finally, they also considered the TMD evolution (Collins-Soper evolution) in the scale ő∂ūĚúĀzeta related to the hadron momentum or the hard scale of the scattering process. Early in 2018, X. Ji et al. reinvestigated quasi-TMDs citebib.bib207. They considered gauge links of finite lengths (staples), instead of infinite ones. Moreover, they redefined the subtraction of the soft factor, since the one defined in Ref. citebib.bib206 could have practical implementation difficulties on the lattice. With this modified subtraction and finite-link TMDs, the Authors could show that the so-called pinch pole singularities are regulated. The new subtraction leads to an additional term in the one-loop computation of the quasi-TMD. Before establishing the matching formula, resummation of large logarithms needed to be performed to avoid scheme dependence in regulating light-cone singularities. This could be done using the Collins-Soper evolution derived in Ref. citebib.bib206. Finally, the matching equation was given to the TMDs in the standard TMD scheme. Very recently, a third paper considering quasi-TMDs appeared by M. Ebert, I. W. Stewart and Y. Zhao citebib.bib208. TMDs depend on two scales, the virtuality scale őľūĚúámu and the scale ő∂ūĚúĀzeta introduced above. Evolution in the former can usually be done fully perturbatively, √° la DGLAP. For the latter, the (Collins-Soper) evolution involves the impact parameter dependent anomalous dimension, ő≥ő∂q‚Äč(őľ,bT)superscriptsubscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚĎěūĚúásubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎágammazetaq(mu,bT) (qūĚĎěq ‚Äď parton flavor index), and becomes non-perturbative for transverse momenta of the order of őõQCDsubscriptőõQCDLambdarm QCD, even for őľ‚ČęőõQCDmuch-greater-thanūĚúásubscriptőõQCDmuggLambdarm QCD. The focus of this paper was on this aspect. The Authors proposed a method of a first-principle non-perturbative determination of ő≥ő∂isuperscriptsubscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚĎĖgammazetai, using the quasi-TMD formalism. They defined the quasi-beam function (unsubtracted quasi-TMD) with finite-length (LūĚźŅL) gauge links that can be related to the corresponding light-cone beam function. However, for the soft function that provides the subtraction of the soft term, they argued that a straightforward definition of a quasi analogue is not possible, since the Wilson lines of the soft function involve both light-cone directions and would require opposite boosts to be recovered from Wilson lines in the spatial directions. A detailed study of this aspect was postponed to a forthcoming publication. For this paper, the Authors introduced a function that describes the missing IR physics and bTsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎábT-dependence, őĒSq‚Äč(bT,a,L)superscriptsubscriptőĒūĚĎÜūĚĎěsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚĎéūĚźŅDeltaSq(bT,a,L). This function removes the L/aūĚźŅūĚĎéL/a linear divergences in the Wilson line self-energy and an explicit form that cancels all divergences in LūĚźŅL may be used in the form proposed in Ref. citebib.bib207. The crucial aspect for the extraction of ő≥ő∂isuperscriptsubscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚĎĖgammazetai is that the őĒSqsuperscriptsubscriptőĒūĚĎÜūĚĎěDeltaSq factor cancels in the ratios of quasi-TMDs defined at different nucleon boosts. The matching between quasi-TMDs and light-cone TMDs can be spoiled by the issue in the soft function and the Authors introduced a function gqSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎěūĚĎÜgqS expressing the mismatch between quasi and light-cone soft functions and allowed it to be non-perturbative. They expressed the quasi-TMD in terms of the light-cone TMD for the non-singlet case via the perturbative kernel (matching between quasi and light-cone beam functions), the unknown gqSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎěūĚĎÜgqS and the Collins-Soper anomalous dimension. Knowing the őĒSqsuperscriptsubscriptőĒūĚĎÜūĚĎěDeltaSq that matches the IR physics of the light-cone soft function, the gqSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎěūĚĎÜgqS could also be calculated perturbatively. The interpretation of the matching equation differs from the analogous one in Ref. citebib.bib207, wherein the analogue of gqSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎěūĚĎÜgqS is assumed to be fully perturbative, which is claimed to be incorrect due to missing the non-perturbative physics when bTsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎábT is of order őõQCD‚ąí1superscriptsubscriptőõQCD1Lambdarm QCD-1. Taking the ratio of two matching equations, the P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3-independent factor gqSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎěūĚĎÜgqS drops out and one can extract the anomalous dimension based on the perturbative matching relation between the quasi and standard beam functions. The method was illustrated by an explicit one-loop computation. It was also remarked that it is restricted to the non-singlet quark channel, because of mixings between singlet quarks and gluons under matching (see the previous paragraph). Meson DAs. Another type of observables that can be considered in the framework of LaMET is meson DAs. They are defined as vacuum-to-meson matrix elements of the same operator as for PDFs, quark and antiquark connected with a Wilson line, with őďőďGamma-structure of e. g. ő≥5‚Äčő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ5superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma5gamma3 for pseudoscalar mesons. They are easier to calculate, since they require only two-point functions, as the pion is not annihilated in the matrix element. The matching can be extracted as a limit of the matching formula for GPDs by crossing the initial quark to the final state and it was extracted for the first time (for the pseudoscalar case) in the paper citebib.bib119 commented on above. We refer to this paper for explicit matching formulae in the transverse momentum cutoff scheme. Further, (pseudoscalar) meson mass corrections were calculated analytically in Ref. citebib.bib127, yielding an infinite series in which the few first terms are enough to take into account for practical application. The heavy quarkonium case was considered in Ref. citebib.bib129 by Y. Jia and X. Xiong, with the one-loop corrections to both quasi and light-cone DAs computed in the framework of NRQCD factorization. The matching for meson DAs and PDFs was also analyzed by Jia et al. within two-dimensional QCD citebib.bib130. In both papers, the UV divergences were regulated with a transverse momentum cutoff, interpreted as a renormalization scale. For more details about these two papers, see Sec. . The matching for vector meson DAs was also considered citebib.bib209, by J. Xu, Q. -A. Zhang and S. Zhao. They derived the formulae in the UV cutoff scheme and in DR (with MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS subtraction), both for longitudinally and transversely polarized mesons. Recently, the matching for meson DAs was also obtained for the case of RI-renormalized quasi-DAs to bring them into MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS-renormalized light-cone DAs citebib.bib210, by Y. -S. Liu et al. They considered the cases of pseudoscalar, as well as longitudinally and transversely polarized vector mesons. The quasi-DA can be renormalized with the same renormalization factors as the corresponding quasi-PDF, in a variant of the RI/MOM scheme. The one-loop calculation of the matching relation proceeded along the lines of analogous computations for quasi-PDFs, first done in Ref. citebib.bib171, and we refer to the original paper for the final formulae. Pseudo-PDFs. The one-loop corrections to pseudo-PDFs were first considered in Ref. citebib.bib85 by K. Orginos et al. , in the leading logarithmic approximation (LLA), appropriate to study the ln‚Ā°z2superscriptūĚĎß2ln z2 dependence. In the LLA, pseudo-PDFs are simply related to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS PDFs at the scale őľūĚúámu: őľ2=4‚Äčexp‚Ā°(‚ąí2‚Äčő≥E)/z2superscriptūĚúá242subscriptūĚõĺūĚźłsuperscriptūĚĎß2mu2=4exp(-2gammaE)/z2, where 1/z1ūĚĎß1/z plays the role of the renormalization scale for the pseudo-distribution. The full one-loop corrections to pseudo-PDFs were calculated by X. Ji, J. -H. Zhang, Y. Zhao citebib.bib170 and also by A. Radyushkin citebib.bib80. Further insights about the structure of these corrections were given in Ref. citebib.bib81 of Radyushkin, which also contains the explicit matching between reduced ITDs and standard light-cone PDFs in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. The matching was also simultanously computed by two other independent studies: J. -H. Zhang, J. -W. Chen, C. Monahan citebib.bib82 and T. Izubuchi et al. citebib.bib83, and the preprints were made available for all three papers almost simultaneously. After initial discrepancies due to finite terms, all three results agree with one another. The matching of pseudo-PDFs is, to some extent, simpler than for quasi-PDFs, since there are no complications related to the non-perturbative renormalization of the pseudo-PDF when taking the ratio of matrix elements to construct the reduced ITD. Crucially, taking the ratio does not alter the IR properties and the factorization framework can be applied, as in the case of matching quasi-distributions. We write here the final matching formula in the notation of Ref. citebib.bib81: ‚Ąź‚Äč(őĹ,őľ2)=ūĚĒź‚Äč(őĹ,z2)+őĪs2‚ÄčŌÄ‚ÄčCF‚Äč‚ąę01ūĚĎĎw‚ÄčūĚĒź‚Äč(w‚ÄčőĹ,z2)‚ÄčB‚Äč(w)‚Äč[ln‚Ā°(z2‚Äčőľ2‚Äče2‚Äčő≥E4)+1]+[4‚Äčln‚Ā°(1‚ąíw)1‚ąíw‚ąí2‚Äč(1‚ąíw)]+,‚ĄźūĚúąsuperscriptūĚúá2ūĚĒźūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎß2subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2ūĚúčsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎ§ūĚĒźūĚĎ§ūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎß2ūĚźĶūĚĎ§delimited-superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚõĺūĚźł41subscriptdelimited-41ūĚĎ§1ūĚĎ§21ūĚĎ§I(nu,mu2)=mathfrakM(nu,z2)+fracalphas2piCFint01dw,mathfrakM(wnu,z2)leftB(w)left[lnleft(z2mu2frace2gammaE4right)+1right]+left[4fracln(1-w)1-w-2(1-w)right]+right, where ‚Ąź‚Äč(őĹ,őľ2)‚ĄźūĚúąsuperscriptūĚúá2I(nu,mu2) is the light-cone ITD, at Ioffe time őĹ=P3‚ÄčzūĚúąsubscriptūĚĎÉ3ūĚĎßnu=P3z and renormalized at the scale őľūĚúámu in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme, ūĚĒź‚Äč(őĹ,z2)ūĚĒźūĚúąsuperscriptūĚĎß2mathfrakM(nu,z2) is the pseudo-ITD at the scale 1/z21superscriptūĚĎß21/z2 and B‚Äč(w)=[(1+w2)/(1‚ąíw)]+ūĚźĶūĚĎ§subscriptdelimited-1superscriptūĚĎ§21ūĚĎ§B(w)=left[(1+w2)/(1-w)right]+ is the Altarelli-Parisi kernel. The first term under the integral corresponds to the LLA result used in Ref. citebib.bib85, i. e. the invoked above multiplicative difference between the pseudo-ITD and MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scales. The term containing ln‚Ā°(1‚ąíw)/(1‚ąíw)1ūĚĎ§1ūĚĎ§ln(1-w)/(1-w) leads to a large negative contribution and causes that the zūĚĎßz-dependence of vertex diagrams involving the gauge link is generated by an effective scale smaller than zūĚĎßz. This can be seen by rewriting the matching equation in such a way that the logarithmic term has an argument (1‚ąíw)‚Äčz‚Äčőľ‚Äčeő≥E+1/2/21ūĚĎ§ūĚĎßūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚõĺūĚźł122(1-w)zmu egammaE+1/2/2, i. e. it involves (1‚ąíw)‚Äčz1ūĚĎ§ūĚĎß(1-w)z instead of zūĚĎßz. In this way, the evolution is governed by this combined logarithm instead of simple ln‚Ā°(z2)superscriptūĚĎß2ln(z2), which leads to őľ‚ąľk/z3similar-toūĚúáūĚĎėsubscriptūĚĎß3musim k/z3 rescaling with a coefficient kūĚĎėk, numerically found to be relatively large, around 4 for the setup of Ref. citebib.bib85 (cf. its LLA value of approximately1.12). In Ref. citebib.bib83, the relation between quasi-PDFs, pseudo-PDFs and ITDs was emphasized. This relation implies that their matching involves a unique factorization formula that involves small distances and large nucleon boosts. For these reasons, Izubuchi et al. claim that LaMET and pseudo/Ioffe-time distribution approaches are, in principle, equivalent. However, it should be noted that the structure of one-loop corrections is different between them and, obviously, the lattice systematics are not equivalent. Because of this, in the absence of all-order (or non-perturbative) matching formulae and under realistic lattice situations, it seems more proper to view them as complementary approaches that aim at the same physical observables.
content_sections.QUARK QUASI-PDFS OF THE NUCLEON: The preliminary studies presented in Sec. have evolved based on the progress on various aspects of PDFs, including simulations with improved parameters, renormalization and choice for the matching procedure. It is the goal of this section to present the advances in the numerical simulations, including a critical discussion on the systematic uncertainties outlined in Sec. . We first present results on ensembles with the quark masses tuned to produce a pion mass larger than its physical value, and we extend the discussion for the simulations with physical values for the quark masses (physical point). To avoid repetition, let us point out that all the works presented here correspond to the isovector flavor combination u‚ąídūĚĎĘūĚĎĎu-d, which receives contributions only from the connected diagram (up to cut-off effects).
content_sections.QUARK QUASI-PDFS OF THE NUCLEON Simulati: Once the non-perturbative renormalization of the non-local operators with straight Wilson line has been developed and presented to the communitycitebib.bib105 (see Sec. ), the first implementation for the quasi-PDFs appeared in the literature in 2017, by ETMCcitebib.bib156 in the RI scheme, and a modification of the proposal in the RI/MOM scheme by the LP collaborationcitebib.bib186. In the original proposal for the non-perturbative renormalizationcitebib.bib156, Alexandrou et al. (ETMC) applied the renormalization prescription on their previous work of Ref. citebib.bib108 for an ensemble with MŌÄ‚Čą370subscriptūĚĎÄūĚúč370Mpiapprox370 MeV (see Sec. for a discussion on the simulations). This employed large-statistics results for nucleon momentum 1.42 GeV and source-sink separation of about 0.98 fm, to demonstrate the effect of the renormalization for the helicity PDFs, which has a multiplicative renormalization, ZőĒ‚ÄčhsubscriptūĚĎćőĒ‚ĄéZDelta h11We remind the Reader that prior to 2018 all available lattice data in the literature corresponded to the ‚Äúő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma3‚ÄĚ operator for the unpolarized PDFs, which has a finite mixing in lattice regularization due to chiral symmetry breaking. . The renormalization function was extracted in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme at a scale of 2 GeV, and the remaining dependence on the RI scale (őľ¬Į0subscript¬ĮūĚúá0barmu0) was reduced by an extrapolation ZőĒ‚ÄčhMS¬Į=Z0,őĒ‚ÄčhMS¬Į+Z1,őĒ‚ÄčhMS¬Į‚Äč(a‚Äčőľ¬Į0)2,subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎć¬ĮMSőĒ‚ĄésuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎć0őĒ‚Ąé¬ĮMSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎć1őĒ‚Ąé¬ĮMSsuperscriptūĚĎésubscript¬ĮūĚúá02Zoverlinerm MSDelta h=Z0,,Delta hoverlinerm MS+Z1,,Delta hoverlinerm MS,(a,barmu0)2,, where Z0,őĒ‚ÄčhMS¬ĮsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎć0őĒ‚Ąé¬ĮMSZ0,,Delta hoverlinerm MS is the desired quantity. In the work of Ref. citebib.bib156, the fit was performed in the range (a‚Äčőľ¬Į0)2‚ÄčŌĶ‚Äč[1.4, 2.0]superscriptūĚĎésubscript¬ĮūĚúá02italic-ŌĶ1.42.0(a,barmu0)2,epsilon,[1.4,,2.0]. One technical consequence of the renormalization is the behavior of the renormalized matrix element in the large-zūĚĎßz region: while the real (imaginary) part of the bare matrix element decays to zero for z/a>10ūĚĎßūĚĎé10z/a>10 (z/a>13ūĚĎßūĚĎé13z/a>13), the renormalization function grows exponentially due to the power divergence. This leads to the unwanted effect of enhanced values for the matrix elements that are almost compatible with zero within uncertainties. This effect is propagated to the quasi-PDF (with the truncation of the integration limits of the Fourier transform), as well as the final extraction of the PDFs. Let as also add that in the RI-type renormalization prescription, each value of z/aūĚĎßūĚĎéz/a is renormalized independently. More discussion on this systematic effect can be found in Sec. . A Fourier transform is applied on renormalized matrix elements leading to xūĚĎ•x-dependent quasi-PDFs, followed by the matching procedure and target mass correction to finally extract the light-cone PDFs. The obtained helicity PDF from the aforementioned work is shown in Fig. . To demonstrate the effect of a proper renormalization, we compare the PDF computed with either fully renormalized matrix elements (blue band) or with matrix elements renormalized with the local axial vector current renormalization function ZAsubscriptūĚĎćūĚźīZA for all zūĚĎßz values (magenta band) that was previously used in Ref. citebib.bib108. As can be seen from the figure, the blue band has a form that is closer to the phenomenological PDFs, as compared to the magenta band. There is also a visual improvement fo the anti-quark region (x<0ūĚĎ•0x<0), which, however, should not be considered conclusive, as this region is, to date, unreliably extracted. Despite the improvement from previous works on quasi-PDFs, a number of further improvements were still necessary at this point, as described in Sec. . Let us also add that Ref. citebib.bib156 employed the matching formula of Xiong et al. citebib.bib98 that was obtained in the transverse momentum cutoff scheme and was later replaced by matching formulae calculated in dimensional regularization (see Sec. ). In the work of J. -W. Chen et al. (LP) presented in Ref. citebib.bib186, a non-perturbative renormalization was also applied, using the RI/MOM scheme. They focused on results for the unpolarized PDF, which however uses the ‚Äúő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma3‚ÄĚ vector operator. The mixing present in this operator was ignored under the assumption that it is small. Indeed, the mixing coefficient is smaller than the multiplicative factor (see Fig. ), but the scalar operator (that mixes with ‚Äúő≥3superscriptūĚõĺ3gamma3‚ÄĚ) is expected to be sizable. This can also be seen from the extraction of the scalar charge using the same ensemble, that has the bare value gSu‚ąíd=0.96‚ÄčsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎÜūĚĎĘūĚĎĎ0.965gSu-d=0.96citebib.bib211. The RI/MOM renormalization scale is fixed to the nucleon momentum, P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3, which also appears in the matching and, thus, cancels to leading order. Even so, residual dependence on this scale can be non-negligible (estimated to up to 10%percent% based on studies with ultra-local operators (z=0ūĚĎß0z=0)) and an extrapolation would be desirable, otherwise this systematic uncertainty cannot be assessed. The renormalization function of this work was used on the results obtained in Ref. citebib.bib107 (for an ensemble with MŌÄ‚Čą310subscriptūĚĎÄūĚúč310Mpiapprox310 MeV) for the unpolarized PDF, together with the matching obtained by I. Stewart and Y. Zhaocitebib.bib171 (see also Sec. ). The matching formula of the latter work was the first one obtained for renormalized quasi-PDFs in the RI scheme matched to the light-cone PDFs in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. A different kind of comparison between lattice and phenomenological data is presented in Fig. . The renormalized matrix elements for the unpolarized case are compared to phenomenological datacitebib.bib212 on which an inverse Fourier transform and matching have been applied to bring them to coordinate space. This procedure was applied on the central values and thus, statistical and systematic uncertainties are absent. It is found that the lattice data have a narrower peak around z‚ÄčP3=0ūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉ30zP3=0 (real part), and are not compatible with the CJ15 data for large values of the Ioffe time, z‚ÄčP3ūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉ3zP3. Note, however, that the lattice data carry very large uncertainties for the large-zūĚĎßz region that prevents proper comparison. In addition, there are concerns on whether such a comparison is meaningful due to higher-twist effects. A recent effort to quantify systematic uncertainties was presented by Y. -S. Liu et al. (LP) in Ref. citebib.bib204, using an ensemble with pion mass value of about 310310310MeVcitebib.bib193. Clover valence fermions were employed on an Nf=2+1+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď211Nf=2+1+1 HISQ ensemblecitebib.bib213. The lattice spacing is a‚Čą0.06ūĚĎé0.06aapprox0.06fm, and the volume has a spatial extent L‚Čą2.9ūĚźŅ2.9Lapprox2.9fm. In this work, Liu et al. computed the unpolarized PDF with nucleon momentum 1.7, 2.15, and 2.6 GeV, and source-sink separations that correspond to 0.60, 0.72, 0.84, 0.96, and 1.08fm. The main goal of this work was to study uncertainties related to excited states, the non-perturbative renormalization, and the matching to light-cone PDFs. For the Fourier transform to momentum (xūĚĎ•x) space, the Authors used the derivative methodcitebib.bib165, which is based on an integration by parts, instead of the standard Fourier transform. In this procedure, the corresponding surface term is neglected (see Sec. for details), which carries systematic uncertainties; the latter is not addressed in this work. Possibly the largest systematic effect comes from the excited states contamination, which is sensitive to the pion mass (worsens for simulations at the physical point)citebib.bib151, an issue that also appears in matrix elements of local operators. In fact, the situation for the non-local operators entering the quasi-PDFs calculation is more severe, as the number of excited states increases with an increase of the nucleon momentum. The effect of excited states can be understood using different analysis methods, as presented in Sec. , with the single- and two-state fits being crucial for identifying the ground state of the nucleon. This is particularly important for non-local operators that are limitedly studied and are less understood than other hadron structure quantities. Ideally, one should perform a combined analysis with source-sink separations higher than 1 fm. The need of two different analysis techniques is to ensure that the dominant excited states are eliminated by achieving convergence between different techniques. In addition, a single-state fit (applied on each source-sink separation separately) gives important information on the statistical uncertainties of the lattice data. Such information is not to be underestimated, as multi-state fits will be driven by the most accurate data. Since statistical noise increases exponentially with the source-sink separation, the most accurate data typically correspond to small separations, which are severely affected by excited states contamination. In the work of Liu et al. , the analysis is exclusively based on two-state fits using either all five separations, or four/three largest ones. The Authors did not provide any details on the statistics used in this work, nor the statistical accuracy of the data on each separation, leading to an inadequacy in the quality of their analysis procedure. The work of Ref. citebib.bib204 addressed systematic uncertainties related to a convenient choice of an RI-type scheme, by examining two possible projectors in the renormalization prescription. This was motivated by the fact that the Green‚Äôs function, őõő≥t‚Äč(p,z)subscriptőõsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°ūĚĎĚūĚĎßLambdagammat(p,z), of the unpolarized operator has additional tensor structures, that is őõő≥t‚Äč(p,z)=Ft‚Äč(p,z)‚Äčő≥t+Fz‚Äč(p,z)‚Äčpt‚Äčő≥zpz+Fp‚Äč(p,z)‚Äčpt‚ÄčpŐłp2,subscriptőõsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°ūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°subscriptūĚźĻūĚĎßūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎ°superscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎĚūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎ°italic-pŐłsuperscriptūĚĎĚ2Lambdagammat(p,z)=tildeFt(p,z)gammat+tildeFz(p,z)fracptgammazpz+tildeFp(p,z)fracptnotpp2,, where FisubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎĖtildeFi‚Äôs are form factors. The minimal projection only projects out FtsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎ°tildeFt, while an alternative choice for the projection is pŐł/(4‚Äčpt)italic-pŐł4superscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎ°notp/(4pt)citebib.bib171, which we call the pŐłitalic-pŐłnotp projection, leading to the conditions Zm‚Äčp‚Äč(z,pzR,a‚ąí1,őľR)subscriptūĚĎćūĚĎöūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎé1subscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖdisplaystyle Zmp(z,pRz,a-1,muR) ‚Č°displaystyleequiv Ft‚Äč(p,z)|p2=‚ąíőľR2pz=pzR,evaluated-atsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚĎĚūĚĎßmatrixsuperscriptūĚĎĚ2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2subscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßdisplaystyletildeFt(p,z)Big|tinybeginmatrixp2=-muR2 !!!!pz=pRzendmatrix,, ZpŐł‚Äč(z,pzR,a‚ąí1,őľR)subscriptūĚĎćitalic-pŐłūĚĎßsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎé1subscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖdisplaystyle Znotp(z,pRz,a-1,muR) ‚Č°displaystyleequiv [Ft‚Äč(p,z)+Fz‚Äč(p,z)+Fp‚Äč(p,z)]|p2=‚ąíőľR2pz=pzR. evaluated-atdelimited-subscriptūĚźĻūĚĎ°ūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎßūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptūĚźĻūĚĎĚūĚĎĚūĚĎßmatrixsuperscriptūĚĎĚ2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2subscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßdisplaystyleBig[tildeFt(p,z)+tildeFz(p,z)+tildeFp(p,z)Big]bigg|tinybeginmatrixp2=-muR2 !!!!pz=pRzendmatrix,. An appropriate matching formula to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme had been also derived for each RI scheme, and it was concluded that the minimal projector leads to better controlled final estimates, shown in Fig. , compared with global-analysis PDFscitebib.bib214, bib.bib215, bib.bib216. The Authors reported reasonable agreement with global analyses in small- and large-xūĚĎ•x regions, while the slope of the lattice data at intermediate xūĚĎ•x-values is different, possibly due to uncertainties in the derivative method for the Fourier transform. The pion mass of the ensemble used for the data is 310 MeV, making the comparison only qualitative.

One of the highlights of the current year is the appearance of lattice results on quasi-PDFs using simulations at the physical point12Preliminary results have been presented last yearcitebib.bib165, bib.bib217 by ETMCcitebib.bib128, bib.bib48 and LPcitebib.bib218, bib.bib219, bib.bib49. Unlike previous studies, these results include proper non-perturbative renormalization and an appropriate matching procedure, for the unpolarized, helicity and transversity PDFs. We note that in these works, the use of the Dirac structure parallel to the Wilson line, ő≥őľsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚúágammamu, has been abandoned due to the mixing discussed in Sec. and replaced by the vector operator with the Dirac structure in the temporal direction, ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0. Here we outline the most important results from each work.The work by C. Alexandrou et al. (ETMC) presented in Ref. citebib.bib128 is the first complete calculation of ETMC with several of the systematic uncertainties under control: simulations at the physical point, non-perturbative renormalization, matching to light-cone PDFs computed in dimensional regularization in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. The ensemble corresponds to Nf=2subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď2Nf=2 twisted mass fermions (at maximal twist) with a clover improvementcitebib.bib220. The ensemble has a lattice spacing of 0.093 fm, lattice spatial extent of 4.5 fm (483√ó96superscript48396483times96), and a pion mass of 130 MeV. The nucleon matrix elements of the non-local vector and axial operator were computed for three values of the momentum, 0.83, 1.11, and 1.38GeV, and employ momentum smearing on the nucleon interpolating fieldcitebib.bib109, that leads to a better signal for the high momenta at a reasonable computational cost (see also Sec. for more details about optimization of the lattice setup). In addition, stout smearingcitebib.bib155 was applied to the links of the Wilson line entering the operator, that reduces the power divergence, and it was checked that different numbers of steps for the stout smearing lead to compatible (almost equivalent) renormalized matrix elements. A large number of configurations is necessary to keep the statistical uncertainties under control, in particular, as the nucleon momentum increases. The work of Ref. citebib.bib128 analyzed 9600, 38250 and 58950 independent correlators for the momenta 0.83, 1.11, and 1.38GeV, respectively, so that statistical uncertainties are at the same level. A first study of excited states contamination was presented using only two values of the source-sink separation, 0.93 and 1.12 fm, and demonstrating that within statistical uncertainties the matrix elements are compatible. Nevertheless, a dedicated study of excited states is missing from the presentation, and was recently completedcitebib.bib221, concluding that the separation 1.12 fm is sufficient for a nucleon momentum of about 1.5 GeV. We will discuss this investigation below. The renormalization was performed according to the procedure outlined in Sec. and the quasi-PDFs were extracted by the standard Fourier transform. The matching formula used in the work of ETMC was a modified expression with respect to the one suggested in Ref. citebib.bib83 (see discussion is Sec. ), that preserves the normalization of the distribution functions. However, there is a small mismatch in the renormalization procedure and the matching process, as the conversion factor brings the quasi-PDFs to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme, while the matching assumes that the quasi-PDFs are given in the M‚ÄčMS¬ĮM¬ĮMSrm Moverlinerm MS scheme. Preliminary investigation showed a small effect, but this mismatch adds to the overall systematic uncertainties. A follow-up work by ETMC eliminated this uncertainty be computing the quasi-PDFs in the proper M‚ÄčMS¬ĮM¬ĮMSrm Moverlinerm MS schemecitebib.bib51, bib.bib190. Nucleon mass corrections were applied according to the formulae of Ref. citebib.bib107. In Fig. , we show the final results for the unpolarized (left) and helicity (right) distributions for the three values of the nucleon boost. For qualitative comparison, we also include the phenomenological determinations: CJ15citebib.bib212, ABMP16citebib.bib222, NNPDF3.1citebib.bib215, DSSV08citebib.bib110, NNPDF1.1polcitebib.bib111 and JAM17citebib.bib223. The Authors reported that the increase of the nucleon momentum shifts the lattice data towards the phenomenological results. For the unpolarized PDF, the two largest momenta give compatible result, while it is not the case for the helicity PDF. For the latter, there is better agreement with phenomenology, compared to the unpolarized case. As seen from the plots, the large-xūĚĎ•x region suffers from the so-called oscillations that are unphysical. These result from the fact that the bare matrix element does not decay to zero fast enough for large zūĚĎßz (due to finite momentum), while the renormalization grows exponentially. It is worth mentioning that the oscillations become milder as the momentum increases from 0.83GeV to 1.38GeV. It is clear that there are several aspects of the current studies to be improved and the removal of the oscillations is one of them. For this to be achieved while systematic uncertainties are under control, different directions must be pursued, for instance new techniques that can contribute to a reduction of the gauge noise in the correlators. An interesting discussion presented in Ref. citebib.bib128 is the comparison between results at the physical point and results from an ensemble with pion mass of about 370 MeVcitebib.bib108 (labeled as B55), as shown in Fig. . The nucleon momentum is the same for both ensembles (‚Čą1.4absent1.4approx1.4 GeV) and a clear pion mass dependence is observed. This is not surprising, as similar pion mass dependence is found in the first moment, ‚ü®x‚ü©u‚ąídsubscriptdelimited-‚ü®‚ü©ūĚĎ•ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎ xu-d, computed with other techniques in Lattice QCD. A follow-up study by ETMC was presented recentlycitebib.bib221 and focused on understanding systematic uncertainties originating from excited states contamination. This study used a high-statistics analysis for the physical point ensemble used in Ref. citebib.bib128. Four (three) source-sink separations (tssubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†ts) were used for the unpolarized (helicity and transversity) case, corresponding to 0.75, 0.84, 0.93, 1.12 fm (0.75, 0.93, 1.12 fm) in physical units. All three analyses techniques described in Sec. , that is a single state fit for each separation tssubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†ts, a two-state fit and the summation method, were used. For a reliable analysis, it is absolutely critical to keep the statistical uncertainties at the same level for all separations, and this is achieved with 4320, 8820, 9000, 72990 measurements for the unpolarized PDFs at the four separations. For the helicity and transversity PDFs, the number of measurements is 3240, 7920 and 72990 for the separations ts=8‚Äča,10‚Äča,12‚ÄčasubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†8ūĚĎé10ūĚĎé12ūĚĎéts=8a,10a,12a, respectively. A comparison of the three methods for the helicity is presented in Fig. , where one clearly observes the discrepancy between separations 0.75 and 1.12 fm for both the real and imaginary parts. In addition, the real part (left plot) obtained for 0.93 fm is compatible with both 0.75 and 1.12 fm. The most striking effect of excited states for this particular study can be seen in the imaginary part (right plot), where separations 0.75 and 0.93 fm are compatible, but in huge disagreement with ts=1.12subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†1.12ts=1.12 fm, indicating that excited states are severe and one should focus on separations above 1 fm. The two-state fit is compatible with the results from the largest separation ts=12‚ÄčasubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†12ūĚĎéts=12a, but not with the two lower separations in the imaginary part. The summation method has large statistical uncertainties and is not providing any useful information. Based on these findings, the Authors concluded that a source-sink separation of 1.12 fm for nucleon momentum up to ‚ąľ1.5similar-toabsent1.5sim1.5 GeV is sufficient for isolating the ground state dominance within statistical uncertainties. We would like to stress the importance of having raw lattice data with similar statistical precision to avoid bias in the various analysis techniques. We now continue the discussion with a presentation of the work of LP for the unpolarized distribution of Ref. citebib.bib218. The calculation was carried out using a mixed action setup of clover fermions in the valence sector on a HISQ Nf=2+1+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď211Nf=2+1+1 ensemble that has lattice spacing a=0.09ūĚĎé0.09a=0.09fm, with spatial lattice extent L‚Čą5.8ūĚźŅ5.8Lapprox5.8fm and a pion mass ‚Čą135absent135approx135MeVcitebib.bib193. A single step of hypercubic smearing (HYP) was employed to improve discretization effects, but also to possibly address a delicate issue: the mixed action setup of clover on HISQ is non-unitary and suffers from exceptional configurations as the quark masses approach their physical value for a fixed lattice spacingcitebib.bib224, bib.bib225. As a consequence, the results would be biased in the presence of exceptional configurations. Based on the empirical evidence of Refs. citebib.bib224, bib.bib225 for local operators, it is expected that for physical value of the pion mass, the ensembles with lattice spacing above 0.09 fm could be vulnerable to exceptional configurations. However, this problem is not addressed in the work of LP for the quasi-PDFs, and a more concrete investigation is imperative to eliminate possible bias in the results. In this work, the Gaussian momentum smearingcitebib.bib109 was employed, and the nucleon was boosted with momentum 2.2, 2.6 and 3GeV. As pointed out by the Authors, one should be particularly cautious in the investigation of excited states contamination, which are expected to worsen with momentum boost, as the energy states come closer to each other. Thus, four variations of two-state fits were tested using source-sink separation of 0.72, 0.81, 0.90, 1.08fm giving compatible results. Despite the effort to employ different analysis techniques with the intention to eliminate excited states contamination, we believe that it unlikely for this procedure to be conclusive, as the two-state fit alone does not guarantee reliability and the different variations used in the work of Ref. citebib.bib218 are correlated. In addition, the success of the fits relies on having all correlators with similar accuracy, otherwise the fit is biased by the accurate data (typically at small values of the separation). Note that Ref. citebib.bib218 does not report any measurements for the nucleon matrix elements. We stress that the statistical accuracy for the data should be verified from plots of the ratio on each separation that enters the fit. The lattice data were properly renormalized using an RI-type schemecitebib.bib186, as described in Sec. , and the quasi-PDFs were obtained using the ‚Äúderivative‚ÄĚ method. Finally, a matching appropriate for the choice of renormalization was appliedcitebib.bib171, bib.bib186 to bring the final estimates in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. This is an alternative to the procedure of ETMC in which a two-step process is used in order to bring the renormalized quasi-PDFs in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS and then match using a proper matching formula. Both processes are equivalent to a one-loop correction, which is currently the level at which both the conversion and the matching formula are available. It is yet to be identified which process brings the final results closer to a two-loop correction; this will be possible once the two-loop expressions are extracted. The final result for the unpolarized PDF is shown in the left plot of Fig. , together with global fit data from CT14citebib.bib214, with agreement between the two within uncertainties. The same setup was applied for the helicity PDF presented in Ref. citebib.bib219, where two values for the source-sink separations were added, giving ts=0.54,0.72,0.81,0.90,0.99,1.08subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†0.540.720.810.900.991.08ts=0.54,0.72,0.81,0.90,0.99,1.08fm. The number of measurements for each separation is 16000, 32000, 32000, 64000, 64000, and 128000, respectively; these data are used exclusively for a two-state fit, but it would certainly be critical to compare with plateau values and the summation method. The renormalization program includes various choices for the scales appearing in the RI and MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS schemes, and we refer the Reader to Ref. citebib.bib219 for details. The final estimates are given in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme at 3GeV, and are shown in the right plot of Fig. (red curve with grey band for reported systematics). The lattice data have similar behavior as the phenomenological estimatescitebib.bib111, bib.bib226, bib.bib223.Extracting the transversity PDF is a powerful demonstration of the advances in the quasi-PDFs approach using Lattice QCD simulations. Preliminary studies can be found in the literature already in 2016citebib.bib107, bib.bib108. However, these lack two major components that prevent comparison with global analysis fits: proper renormalization and matching procedures. Complete studies of the transversity quasi-PDFs appeared this year by ETMCcitebib.bib48 and by LPcitebib.bib49 using the same lattice setup as their work for the unpolarized and helicity PDFs described above. The main motivation for first-principle calculations of the transversity PDF is the fact that it is less known experimentallycitebib.bib227, bib.bib228, bib.bib229, bib.bib230, bib.bib231, bib.bib232, because it is chirally odd, and totally inclusive processes cannot be used. In particular, one may extract information on the transversity PDF from e+‚Äče‚ąísuperscriptūĚĎísuperscriptūĚĎíe+e- annihilation into dihadrons with transverse momentumcitebib.bib233, bib.bib234, bib.bib235, bib.bib235 and semi-inclusive deep-inelastic scattering (SIDIS) TMD data for single hadron productioncitebib.bib236, bib.bib237, bib.bib238. This method requires disentanglement of the dependence on the momentum fraction from the transverse momentum on TMD form factors and TMD PDFs. Alternatively, dihadron SIDIS cross section data can be analyzed to obtain the transversity distribution directly from the measured asymmetry citebib.bib239, bib.bib240, bib.bib241. However, this analysis leads to large uncertainties, as the available data are less precise, and the collinear factorization at large xūĚĎ•x is problematiccitebib.bib242. The ETM Collaboration presented the first computation of the xūĚĎ•x-dependence for the transversity PDF in Ref. citebib.bib48 in Lattice QCD which includes a non-perturbative renormalization in lattice regularization (RI), and a matching procedure similar to the M‚ÄčMS¬ĮM¬ĮMSrm Moverlinerm MS scheme of Ref. citebib.bib128. The latter was recalculated using the appropriate tensor non-local operator. We remind the Reader the parameters for the Nf=2subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď2Nf=2 ensemble at a pion mass of 130 MeVcitebib.bib220, which has the lattice spacing a=0.093ūĚĎé0.093a=0.093 and the volume of 483√ó96superscript48396483times96. As in the case of the unpolarized and helicity PDFs, the nucleon was boosted with momentum 0.83, 1.11, and 1.38GeV, while the source-sink separation was fixed to ts=12‚Äča‚ąľ1.12subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†12ūĚĎésimilar-to1.12ts=12asim1.12 fm for the final results. This value has been chosen after a thorough investigation of excited statescitebib.bib221. The statistics increases with the nucleon momentum, that is 9600, 38250, 72990 measurements for momentum 0.83, 1.11, and 1.38GeV, respectively. The final lattice data for the transversity isovector PDF, h1u‚ąíd,latticesuperscriptsubscript‚Ąé1ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎlatticeh1u-d,rm lattice, are shown in Fig. in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme and at a scale of 22sqrt2 GeV, so that they can be compared to phenomenological fits extracted at the same scale. In the left plot, we show the dependence on the nucleon momentum, which is found to be small for most values of xūĚĎ•x, with the highest momentum having milder oscillatory behavior. In the right panel, we present the lattice data for the highest momentum P=10‚ÄčŌÄLūĚĎÉ10ūĚúčūĚźŅP=frac10piL and compare with phenomenological fits on SIDIS data withoutcitebib.bib231 or withcitebib.bib231 constraints from lattice estimates of the tensor charge gTsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎágT (‚ÄúSIDIS+lattice‚ÄĚ). The difference in the statistical accuracy between the global fit and the lattice data is impressive and, with the data of Ref. citebib.bib48 being more accurate than both the constrained and unconstrained SIDIS results. One way to check for systematic uncertainties is to compare the tensor charge as extracted: (a) directly from the local tensor operator, and (b) by integrating over xūĚĎ•x within the interval [‚ąí1, 1]11[-1,,1] of PDFs. This consistency check reveals that both results are well compatible within uncertainties and both give a value of gT=1.09‚ÄčsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎá1.0911gT=1.09 (the exact matching of the two numbers is to some degree accidental). Even though the agreement is non-trivial, as the steps leading to both values are different, it is, obviously, not sufficient for a complete quantitative understanding of systematic effects. The latest work of LP on the quasi-PDFs was very recently extended to the transversity distributioncitebib.bib49, using the same Nf=2+1+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď211Nf=2+1+1 ensemble with clover valence quarks on a HISQ seacitebib.bib193, physical pion mass, the lattice spacing a‚Čą0.09ūĚĎé0.09aapprox0.09 fm, and the volume of 643√ó96superscript64396643times96. For the lattice setup, we refer the Reader to Sec. and Refs. citebib.bib219, bib.bib218. Six source-sink separations were used with the highest at 1.08 fm and the same statistics as in Ref. citebib.bib219. These were analyzed based on different variations of a two-state fit, and the extracted matrix elements are shown in Fig. for the three momenta employed in this work, that is 2.2, 2.6 and 3GeV. It is observed that the dependence on the nucleon momentum is weak within the uncertainties, which also holds for the matched PDFs. This can be seen in Fig.3 of Ref. citebib.bib49, with the exception of the very small-xūĚĎ•x region. However, this is not conclusive, as lattice calculations have limitations on the reliability for this region. The observed convergence could be partly due to limitations in the matching formula, which is available to one-loop level only. Given the latter, a convergence can be possibly achieved at smaller nucleon momentum, which has the advantage that excited states can be better controlled. Evidence of non-negligible excited states contamination for momenta as high as 3GeV can be seen in Fig. , particularly in the real part where the matrix element becomes negative for large values of zūĚĎßz. The latter is a clear evidence of excited states and it has been observed in other works that increasing source-sink separation (thus decreasing the contamination) brings the real part of large-zūĚĎßz bare matrix elements to values compatible with zero, see e. g. the upper left plot of Fig.1 in Ref. citebib.bib221 and, to a lesser extent, the left panel of Fig. above.
