content_sections.I Introduction: Parton distribution functions (PDFs) are key quantities for gaining an understanding of hadron structure and for making predictions for the cross sections in high-energy scattering experiments. In QCD factorization theorems for hard scattering processescitebib.bib1, the relevant PDFs are defined in terms of the nucleon matrix elements of light-cone correlation operators. For example, in dimensional regularization with d=4‚ąí2‚ÄčŌĶūĚĎĎ42italic-ŌĶd=4-2epsilon, the bare unpolarized quark PDF is q‚Äč(x,ŌĶ)‚Č°‚ąęd‚Äčőĺ‚ąí4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčx‚ÄčP+‚Äčőĺ‚ąí‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí)‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčU‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•italic-ŌĶūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚúČ4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúČquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚúČsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎąsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉdisplaystyle q(x,epsilon)equiv!int!dxi-over 4pie-ixP+xi-! P|barpsi(xi-)gamma+U(xi-,0)psi|P, where xūĚĎ•x is the momentum fraction, the nucleon momentum Pőľ=(P0,0,0,Pz)superscriptūĚĎÉūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎÉ000superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPmu=(P0,0,0,Pz), őĺ¬Ī=(t¬Īz)/2superscriptūĚúČplus-or-minusplus-or-minusūĚĎ°ūĚĎß2xipm=(tpm z)/sqrt2 are the light-cone coordinates, and the Wilson line is U‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)=P‚Äčexp‚Ā°(‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0őĺ‚ąíūĚĎĎő∑‚ąí‚ÄčA+‚Äč(ő∑‚ąí)). ūĚĎąsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚĎÉūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0superscriptūĚúČdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚúāsuperscriptūĚźīsuperscriptūĚúādisplaystyle U(xi-,0)=Pexpbiggl(-igint0xi-deta-A+(eta-)biggr) . Most often the bare PDF is renormalized in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineoperatornameMS scheme to obtain q‚Äč(x,őľ)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚúáq(x,mu), and this renormalized PDF is used to make predictions for experiment. The relation is q‚Äč(x,ŌĶ)=‚ąęx1d‚Äčyy‚ÄčZMS¬Į‚Äč(xy,ŌĶ,őľ)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•italic-ŌĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•1ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎć¬ĮMSūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶italic-ŌĶūĚúáūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle q(x,epsilon)=intx1dyover y>ZoverlineoperatornameMSBigl(xover y,epsilon,muBigr)q(y,mu) , where őľūĚúámu is the renormalization scale, and we have suppressed the flavor indices in the renormalization constant ZMS¬ĮsuperscriptūĚĎć¬ĮMSZoverlineoperatornameMS and PDFs. In light-cone quantization with A+=0superscriptūĚźī0A+=0, the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineoperatornameMS definition has an interpretation as a parton number density. So far our main knowledge of the PDF is obtained from global fits to deep inelastic scattering and jet data, see for examplecitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6. On the other hand, calculating the PDF from first principles with QCD has been an attractive subject, which can for example provide access to spin and momentum distributions that are hard to determine experimentally. Several different approaches to this have been considered using the lattice theory which is a nonperturbative method to solve QCD. Since the lattice theory is defined in a discretized Euclidean space with imaginary time, it is very difficult to calculate Minkowskian quantities with real-time dependence such as the PDF. The first and most well explored option is calculating the moments of the PDFcitebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11 that are matrix elements of local gauge-invariant operators. However, since the lattice regularization breaks O‚ÄčūĚĎā4O rotational symmetry, the consequent mixing between operators of different dimensions makes it difficult to compute higher moments, which in practice has limited the amount of information that can be extracted from this approach. A method to improve this situation by restoring the rotational symmetry has been proposed in Ref. citebib.bib12. Other proposals include extracting the PDF from the hadronic tensorcitebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16 and the forward Compton amplitudecitebib.bib17, possibly with flavor changing currentscitebib.bib18, and the more general ‚Äúlattice cross sections‚ÄĚcitebib.bib19, bib.bib20. Systematic lattice analyses of these approaches are under investigation, but challenges remain. In Ref citebib.bib21 Ji proposed that the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of the PDF can be extracted from a Euclidean distribution on the lattice, which can be understood in the language of the large momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib22. This Euclidean distribution is referred to as the quasi-PDF, whose bare matrix element is defined using a spatial correlation of quarks along the zūĚĎßz direction, q‚Äč(x,Pz,ŌĶ)‚Č°‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčz4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚ÄčPz‚Äčz‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčőď‚ÄčU‚Äč(z,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßitalic-ŌĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎß4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎßquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßőďūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉdisplaystyletildeq(x,Pz,epsilon)equivint-inftyinftydzover 4pieixPzz P|barpsi(z)Gamma U(z,0)psi|P,, where őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz, zőľ=z‚ÄčeőľsuperscriptūĚĎßūĚúáūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúázmu=zemu, eőľ=(0,0,0,1)superscriptūĚĎíūĚúá0001emu=(0,0,0,1), and the Wilson line is U‚Äč(z,0)=P‚Äčexp‚Ā°(‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0zūĚĎĎz‚Ä≤‚ÄčAz‚Äč(z‚Ä≤)). ūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚĎÉūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎßdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤superscriptūĚźīūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤displaystyle U(z,0)=Pexpleft(-igint0zdzprimeAz(zprime)right) . For finite momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, q‚Äč(x,Pz,ŌĶ)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßitalic-ŌĶtildeq(x,Pz,epsilon) has support in ‚ąí‚ąěCBigl(xover y,muRovermu,muover pzBigr),q(y,mu) +ūĚí™‚Äč(M2Pz2,őõQCD2Pz2),ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎÄ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2displaystyle+Obiggl(M2over Pz2,LambdaoperatornameQCD2over Pz2biggr) , where the renormalized quasi-PDF q‚Äč(x,Pz,őľR)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖtildeq(x,Pz,muR) is defined in a particular scheme at renormalization scale őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR, and ūĚí™‚Äč(M2/Pz2,őõQCD2/Pz2)ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎÄ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2O(M2/Pz2,Lambdarm QCD2/Pz2) are power corrections suppressed by the nucleon momentum. In general the result for CūĚź∂C will depend on the choice of őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz or ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 and renormalization schemes. For őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz the matching coefficient CūĚź∂C has been computed for the iso-vector quark quasi-PDF at one-loop level, first with a transverse momentum cutoff incitebib.bib24, confirmed incitebib.bib19, bib.bib25, and also recently determined in the regularization-invariant momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) schemecitebib.bib26. Matching for the gluon quasi-PDF is calculated incitebib.bib27, bib.bib28. The matching coefficient CūĚź∂C is independent of the choice of states used for qūĚĎětildeq and qūĚĎěq.1The scheme definition itself may separately involve a choice of state, such as in the RI/MOM scheme, but the result is still an operator renormalization that can be used for different choices of hadronic states for qūĚĎětildeq and qūĚĎěq. Since matching calculations are carried out with quark states of momentum pzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz, it can be tricky to know what the right choice to make is for CūĚź∂C, and in some of the literature the choice of pz=PzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz has been suggested when utilizing CūĚź∂C for the hadronic nucleon state. This is for example the case in the original quasi-PDF paperscitebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib24 and in the pioneering lattice calculations of the PDF from the quasi-PDF incitebib.bib29, bib.bib25, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, which was summarized in Ref. citebib.bib37. In the quasi-generalized parton distribution analysis incitebib.bib38 it was observed that one should take pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz. Through our rigorous analysis of Eq. we show that the correct result for this equation is indeed pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz. When Fourier transformed this correlation gives the PDF q‚Äč(y,ŌĶ)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčő∂2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčy‚Äčő∂‚ÄčQ‚Äč(ő∂,ŌĶ). ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶italic-ŌĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚúĀ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ¶ūĚúĀūĚĎĄūĚúĀitalic-ŌĶdisplaystyle q(y,epsilon)=int-inftyinftydzetaover 2pi eiyzeta>Q(zeta,epsilon),. In short the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF are different representations of the Euclidean spatial correlator, as summarized in Table. . It was pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib45 that to obtain enough information to extract the PDF for the spatial correlator with small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2, one has to do lattice calculations with large momenta PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, which is the same requirement as for the quasi-PDF. Ref. citebib.bib45 also proposed that the renormalized pseudo-PDF satisfies the following small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization, ūĚíę‚Äč(x,z2‚ÄčőľR2)ūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2displaystylePleft(x,z2muR2right) =‚ąęd‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčūĚíě‚Äč(xy,őľR2őľ2,z2‚ÄčőľR2)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ)absentūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚíěūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle=intdyover|y|>Cleft(xover y,muR2overmu2,z2muR2right)q(y,mu) +ūĚí™‚Äč(z2‚ÄčőõQCD2,z2‚ÄčM2),ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎÄ2displaystyle +cal O(z2Lambdarm QCD2,z2M2),, which they verified at order O‚Äč(őĪs)ūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†O(alphas) for the unpolarized iso-vector case with őď=ő≥0őďsuperscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0. (Again the coefficient ūĚíěūĚíěcal C will depend on the choice of őď=ő≥0őďsuperscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0 or ő≥zsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßgammaz. ) In Ref. citebib.bib19 a diagrammatic derivation of the factorization formula in Eq. for the quasi-PDF was given. Here we derive this factorization formula for the quasi-PDF in an alternate manner, and also show that spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF are different representations of the same fundamental factorization. Our approach is based on the operator product expansion (OPE) for spacelike separated local operatorscitebib.bib46. For such operators the OPE has been proven for scalar field theory to all orders in perturbation theorycitebib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, and is widely assumed to hold for any renormalizable quantum field theory including QCD. By introducing auxiliary fields in place of the Wilson linecitebib.bib50, the correlator in Eq. is known to be equivalent to a product of local renormalizable operators of this type. Through our derivation we find the explicit form of the large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz and small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization formulas in Eq. and Eq. respectively, as well as the relationship between the matching coefficients CūĚź∂C and ūĚíěūĚíěC. Since the requirement for large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz and small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 is the same for both the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF approaches, there is in principle no fundamental difference in applying either one to lattice calculations of the proton matrix element of Oőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßtildeOGamma(z). It is interesting to compare both approaches utilizing the same lattice data, although they shall not yield different result in principle. The rest of this paper is organized as follows: In Sec. , we use an OPE of Oőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßtildeOGamma(z) to derive the large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz factorization of LaMET in Eq. and small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization of the pseudo-PDF in Eq. . We prove that one must take pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz in Eq. , so the corresponding argument in CūĚź∂C is őľ/(|y|‚ÄčPz)ūĚúáūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßmu/(|y|Pz). (This OPE approach was used recently in Ref. citebib.bib20 to prove the factorization theorem for the ‚Äúlattice cross sections‚ÄĚ, and the OPE proof carried out here was done independently and first presented in Ref. citebib.bib51. ) In Sec. , we derive the spatial correlator, pseudo-PDF, and quasi-PDF distributions and matching coefficients at one-loop in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS and analyze the Fourier-transform relation between the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF. Unlike earlier results for the quasi-PDF in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS, we also use dimensional regularization with minimal subtraction to renormalize divergences at x=¬Ī‚ąěūĚĎ•plus-or-minusx=pminfty. In Sec. , we discuss how renormalization schemes other than MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS are easily incorporated into the factorization formulas. In Sec. we carry out a numerical analysis of the matching coefficients, by computing the convolution in Eq. numerically using the PDF determined by global fitscitebib.bib4. We show that the difference between using pz=PzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz and pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz in Eq. is an important effect, and that our MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS matching coefficients are insensitive to cutoffs in the convolution integral. In Sec. , we discuss the implications of our OPE analysis for the lattice calculation of the PDF in both the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF approaches. Finally, we conclude in Sec. .
content_sections.II Factorization from the OPE: In this section we make use of the operator product expansion to derive the matching relation for the quasi-PDF, as well as the equivalent matching relations for the spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF. For simplicity these three equivalent cases are presented in separate subsections.
content_sections.II Factorization from the OPE II.1 OPE a: The OPE is a technique to expand nonlocal operators with separation zőľsuperscriptūĚĎßūĚúázmu in terms of local ones in the Euclidean limit of z2‚Üí0‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎß20z2to 0. It can be applied to both bare regulated operators as well as renormalized operators, and our focus will be on the latter. Finally, using the inverse transform of the renormalized analog of Eq. , Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀūĚúádisplaystyle Q(zeta,mu) =‚ąę‚ąí11ūĚĎĎy‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčy‚Äčő∂‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ). absentsuperscriptsubscript11differential-dūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ¶ūĚúĀūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle=int-11!!dy>e-iyzeta,q(y,mu),. we obtain Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ2‚Äčz2)=ūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2absentdisplaystyletildeQ(zeta,mu2z2)= ‚ąę‚ąí11ūĚĎĎőĪ‚ÄčūĚíě‚Äč(őĪ,őľ2‚Äčz2)‚ÄčQ‚Äč(őĪ‚Äčő∂,őľ)+ūĚí™‚Äč(z2‚ÄčőõQCD2). superscriptsubscript11differential-dūĚõľūĚíěūĚõľsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2ūĚĎĄūĚõľūĚúĀūĚúáūĚí™superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2displaystyleint-11dalpha C(alpha,mu2z2),Q(alphazeta,mu)+cal O(z2Lambdarm QCD2),. The result in Eq. is the factorization formula for the lattice calculable spatial correlator Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ2‚Äčz2)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2tildeQ(zeta,mu2z2) which expresses it in terms of the light-cone correlation Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀūĚúáQ(zeta,mu) that defines the PDF. It has the same structure as the factorization formula for the spatial correlator used for the calculation of the pion distribution amplitude in Ref. citebib.bib59, bib.bib60.

We define the kernel as C‚Äč(xy,őľ|y|‚ÄčPz)‚Č°‚ąęd‚Äčő∂2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčxy‚Äčő∂‚Äč‚ąĎn=0Cn‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčő∂2(y‚ÄčPz)2)‚Äč(‚ąíi‚Äčő∂)nn!,ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúáūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎĎūĚúĀ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúĀsubscriptūĚĎõ0subscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúĀ2superscriptūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎĖūĚúĀūĚĎõūĚĎõdisplaystyle Cleft(xover y,fracmu|y|Pzright)equivint!fracdzeta2pi>eixover yzetasumn=0CnBigl(fracmu2zeta2(yPz)2Bigr),frac(-izeta)nn!,, and then Eq. can be rewritten as q‚Äč(x,őľPz)=‚ąę‚ąí11d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčC‚Äč(xy,őľ|y|‚ÄčPz)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúáūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyletildeqleft(x,fracmuPzright)=int-11fracdy|y|>CBigl(fracxy,fracmu|y|PzBigr)>qleft(y,muright),, which is the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS factorization formula for the quasi-PDF. This result shows that the factorization formula in Eq. must have pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz for the quasi-PDF in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. Taking the moment of the quasi-PDF using Eq. gives ‚ąę01ūĚĎĎx‚Äčxn‚Äčq‚Äč(x,őľPz)=(i‚Äčdd‚Äčő∂)n‚ÄčQ‚Äč(ő∂,őľ2‚Äčő∂2Pz2)|ő∂‚Üí0superscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßevaluated-atsuperscriptūĚĎĖūĚĎĎūĚĎĎūĚúĀūĚĎõūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2‚ÜíūĚúĀ0displaystyleint01!!dx>xntildeqleft(x,fracmuPzright)=Big(ifracddzetaBig)ntildeQbiggl(zeta,fracmu2zeta2Pz2biggr)Bigg|zetato 0 =‚ąĎn‚Ä≤(i‚Äčdd‚Äčő∂)n‚Äč[Cn‚Ä≤‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčő∂2Pz2)‚Äč(‚ąíi‚Äčő∂)n‚Ä≤n‚Ä≤!]|ő∂‚Üí0‚Äčan‚Ä≤+1‚Äč(őľ). absentevaluated-atsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎĖūĚĎĎūĚĎĎūĚúĀūĚĎõdelimited-subscriptūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎĖūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤‚ÜíūĚúĀ0subscriptūĚĎésuperscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤1ūĚúádisplaystylequad=sumnprimeBig(ifracddzetaBig)nbigg[CnprimeBig(fracmu2zeta2Pz2Big)frac(-izeta)nprimenprime!bigg]Bigg|zetato 0anprime+1left(muright),. Since the Cn‚Ä≤subscriptūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤Cnprime coefficients have ln‚Ā°(ő∂2)superscriptūĚúĀ2ln(zeta2) dependence, the derivative for n‚Ä≤=nsuperscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤ūĚĎõnprime=n will always have a logarithmic singularity as ő∂‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚúĀ0zetato 0, and there will be even more singular terms for n‚Ä≤<nsuperscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤ūĚĎõnprime<n. content_sections.II Factorization from the OPE II.3 Facto: The renormalized pseudo-PDF is the Fourier transform of the renormalized spatial correlator ūĚíę‚Äč(x,őľ2‚Äčz2)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčő∂2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚Äčő∂‚ÄčQ‚Äč(ő∂,őľ2‚Äčz2). ūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚúĀ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•ūĚúĀūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2displaystylePleft(x,mu2z2right)=int-inftyinfty!fracdzeta2pi>eixzeta>tildeQbigl(zeta,mu2z2bigr),. Since both the pseudo-PDF and spatial correlator are multiplicatively renormalized in a ő∂ūĚúĀzeta-independent manner, this follows immediately from Eq. . If we take Eq. and Fourier transform the spatial correlator Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ2‚Äčz2)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2tildeQ(zeta,mu2z2) into the pseudo-PDF, and light-cone correlation Q‚Äč(őĪ‚Äčő∂,őľ)ūĚĎĄūĚõľūĚúĀūĚúáQ(alphazeta,mu) into the PDF, then we immediately obtain the factorization formula for the pseudo-PDF, ūĚíę‚Äč(x,z2‚Äčőľ2)=ūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚúá2absentdisplaystyleP(x,z2mu2)= ‚ąę|x|1d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčūĚíě‚Äč(xy,őľ2‚Äčz2)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•1ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚíěūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyleint|x|1dyover|y| Cleft(xover y,mu2z2right)q(y,mu) +‚ąę‚ąí1‚ąí|x|d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčūĚíě‚Äč(xy,őľ2‚Äčz2)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ)subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•1ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚíěūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle+int-|x|-1dyover|y| Cleft(fracxy,mu2z2right)q(y,mu) +ūĚí™‚Äč(z2‚ÄčőõQCD2),ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2displaystyle+cal O(z2Lambdarm QCD2),, which is the small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization formula in Eq. . The upper and lower limits of the integrals in Eq. follow immediately from the support ‚ąí1‚Č§őĪ‚Č§11ūĚõľ1-1leqalphaleq 1 of the matching coefficient ūĚíě‚Äč(őĪ,z2‚Äčőľ2)ūĚíěūĚõľsuperscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚúá2C(alpha,z2mu2), and we recall that we also have ‚ąí1‚Č§x‚Č§11ūĚĎ•1-1leq xleq 1 for the pseudo-PDF on the LHS. Since the range of xūĚĎ•x is bounded for the pseudo-PDF the terms in the series expansion of the exponential in Eq. exist, Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ2‚Äčz2)=‚ąĎn=0‚ąě‚ąę‚ąí11ūĚĎĎx‚Äč(‚ąíi‚Äčő∂)n‚Äčxnn!‚ÄčūĚíę‚Äč(x,őľ2‚Äčz2). ūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎõ0superscriptsubscript11differential-dūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎĖūĚúĀūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõūĚĎõūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2displaystyletildeQ(zeta,mu2z2)=sumn=0inftyint-11!!dx>frac(-izeta)nxnn!cal P(x,mu2z2),. Comparing with Eq. this implies that the moments of the pseudo-PDF are given by ‚ąę‚ąí11ūĚĎĎx‚Äčxn‚ÄčūĚíę‚Äč(x,őľ2‚Äčz2)superscriptsubscript11differential-dūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2displaystyleint-11!!dx>xn>cal P(x,mu2z2) =Cn‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)‚Äčan+1‚Äč(őľ). absentsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõ1ūĚúádisplaystyle=Cn(mu2z2),an+1(mu),. So far we have proven the large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz factorization of the quasi-PDF and small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization of the spatial correlation and pseudo-PDFs. After deriving one factorization, it immediately leads to the others, since they are just different representations of the same spatial correlator. Indeed, we see that the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF are related at leading power by their definitions: q‚Äč(x,őľPz)=‚ąęd‚Äčő∂2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚Äčő∂‚Äč‚ąę‚ąí11ūĚĎĎy‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčy‚Äčő∂‚ÄčūĚíę‚Äč(y,őľ2‚Äčő∂2Pz2),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎĎūĚúĀ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•ūĚúĀsuperscriptsubscript11differential-dūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ¶ūĚúĀūĚíęūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2displaystyletildeqBigl(x,muover PzBigr)=int!dzetaover 2pi eixzetaint-11!!dy e-iyzeta>Pbiggl(y,mu2zeta2over Pz2biggr),, where we have used z=ő∂/PzūĚĎßūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßz=zeta/Pz. Based on Eq. and Eq. , the Wilson coefficients in their factorization theorems also maintain the same relationship, C‚Äč(őĺ,őľ|y|‚ÄčPz)ūĚź∂ūĚúČūĚúáūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyle Cleft(xi,muover|y|Pzright) =‚ąęd‚Äčő∂2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčőĺ‚Äčő∂‚Äč‚ąę‚ąí11ūĚĎĎőĪ‚Äče‚ąíi‚ÄčőĪ‚Äčő∂‚ÄčūĚíě‚Äč(őĪ,őľ2‚Äčő∂2(y‚ÄčPz)2). absentūĚĎĎūĚúĀ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚúČūĚúĀsuperscriptsubscript11differential-dūĚõľsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚõľūĚúĀūĚíěūĚõľsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚúĀ2superscriptūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2displaystyle=int!dzetaover 2pi eixizetaint-11!!dalpha>e-ialphazeta>Cleft(alpha,mu2zeta2over(yPz)2right). For the relations in Eqs. and the same choice of őď=ő≥0őďsuperscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0 or ő≥zsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßgammaz should be used in the quasi- and pseudo-PDFs, or their corresponding coefficients. In summary, there is a unique factorization formula that matches the quasi-PDF, spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF to the PDF. Since their factorizations into the PDF all require small distances and have large nucleon momentum, the setup for their lattice calculations must also be the same. Therefore, the LaMET and pseudo distribution approaches are in principle equivalent to each other, and they differ perhaps only by effects related to their implementation on the lattice. In Ref. citebib.bib39 it was speculated that one can study a ratio function Q‚Äč(ő∂,z2,a‚ąí1)/Q‚Äč(0,z2,a‚ąí1)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎé1ūĚĎĄ0superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎé1displaystyletildeQ(zeta,z2,a-1)/tildeQ(0,z2,a-1) on a lattice with spacing aūĚĎéa, and the O‚Äč(z2)ūĚĎāsuperscriptūĚĎß2O(z2) corrections may cancel approximately. This idea was tested in Ref. citebib.bib42 in lattice QCD, and the results show that the ratio evolves slowly in z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 at small values. It is then interesting to consider what type of non-perturbative information can be extracted from this ratio. This question can be answered using the small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization for the spatial correlator. According to Eq. , Q‚Äč(0,őľ2‚Äčz2)=C0‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)+O‚Äč(z2‚ÄčőõQCD2),ūĚĎĄ0superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2subscriptūĚź∂0superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2ūĚĎāsuperscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2displaystyletildeQ(0,mu2z2)=C0(mu2z2)+O(z2Lambdarm QCD2),, where in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme to one-loop C0‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)=1+őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč[32‚Äčln‚Ā°(őľ2‚Äčz2‚Äče2‚Äčő≥E/4)+52],subscriptūĚź∂0superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß21subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ2ūĚúčdelimited-32superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚõĺūĚźł452displaystyle C0(mu2z2)=1+alphasCFover 2pibiggl[3over 2ln(mu2z2e2gammaE/4)+5over 2biggr],, which was also derived recently in citebib.bib61. The same point was demonstrated by work done very recently incitebib.bib61, which appeared simultaneously with our paper.
content_sections.III Equivalence at one-loop order: As has been proven in Sec. , the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF as well as their matching coefficients are related by a simple Fourier transform in Eq. and Eq. . This relation is valid to all orders in perturbation theory. In this section we check the relations in Eq. and Eq. at one-loop order. We choose őď=ő≥0őďsuperscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0 for our main presentation, but also quote final results for the case őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz. Now, according to the relations between the quasi-PDF and the spatial correlator or pseudo-PDFs in Eqs. (,), we can do a Fourier or double Fourier transform of the results in Eqs. (,) to get the quasi-PDF. Despite its straightforwardness, the Fourier transform is subtle and the details are provided in App. . (, ) automatically satisfy the relation in Eq. . While retaining current conservation in the renormalized quasi-PDF, Eq. can be used for example as input to the two-step matching procedure in the lattice calculation of PDF in Refs. citebib.bib33. For the matching step, an equivalent procedure is to study the ratio given in Eq. in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme from the start, as advocated in Ref. citebib.bib39, bib.bib62, performing its matching onto the PDF, which will yield Eq. . This concludes our discussion of matching results and the equivalence between the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF at one-loop order.
content_sections.IV Other Renormalization Schemes: Although we derive the above matching formula assuming that the quasi-PDF is renormalized in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineoperatornameMS scheme, this is not a limitation to our result. Since the gauge-invariant Wilson line operator Oőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßtildeOGamma(z) has been proven to be multiplicatively renormalizable in the coordinate spacecitebib.bib52, bib.bib53, one can convert Qőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎĄőďūĚĎßtildeQGamma(z) from any other scheme to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineoperatornameMS scheme before using the above factorization formula. The renormalization of the quasi-PDF has been studied in many recent paperscitebib.bib64, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib65, bib.bib56, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib66, bib.bib26, bib.bib28. We will discuss some of these results and show how they can be incorporated into the factorization formula in Eq. . The MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineoperatornameMS scheme is convenient for our discussion of the OPE as it guarantees Lorentz and gauge invariances, but it is not practical for lattice renormalization. Since the lattice theory has a natural UV cut-off 1/a1ūĚĎé1/a with aūĚĎéa being the lattice spacing, the unrenormalized spatial correlator QūĚĎĄtildeQ inherits the power divergence from the Wilson line self-energy according to Eq. . For an arbitrary scheme XūĚĎčX, the renormalized spatial correlator QX‚Äč(ő∂,z2‚ÄčőľR2)=lima‚Üí0ZX‚ąí1‚Äč(z2‚ÄčőľR2,a2‚ÄčőľR2)‚ÄčQ‚Äč(ő∂,z2/a2)superscriptūĚĎĄūĚĎčūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎß2subscriptsuperscriptūĚúá2ūĚĎÖsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎé0subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎć1ūĚĎčsuperscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2superscriptūĚĎé2subscriptsuperscriptūĚúá2ūĚĎÖūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎé2displaystyletildeQX(zeta,z2mu2R)=limato 0Z-1X(z2muR2,a2mu2R),tildeQ(zeta,z2/a2) should be free of all the UV divergences and have a well-defined continuum limit as a‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎé0ato 0. One undesirable feature of the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme for the renormalized spatial correlator is that it does not have a smooth z‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎß0zto 0 limit, and hence no simple connection with the fact that the local operator for z=0ūĚĎß0z=0 is a conserved current. To avoid this one can simply make use of a different scheme that has a simple relation to MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS, such as by adding C0‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)subscriptūĚź∂0superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2C0(mu2z2) to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS renormalization constant. This removes the offending ln‚Ā°(őľ2‚Äčz2)superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2ln(mu2z2) terms and yields a scheme with a smooth connection to the conserved current. Besides the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme, the quasi-PDF has also been defined with a transverse momentum cut-offcitebib.bib24, bib.bib29, bib.bib19, bib.bib25 and in the RI/MOM schemecitebib.bib56, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib66, bib.bib26. The RI/MOM scheme has attracted strong interest recently as it can be implemented nonperturbatively on the lattice, so we consider it as an explicit example of the above relations. Z-1rm OM,tildeQ(zeta=zpz,z2/a2,-p2a2)right|p2=-muR2,pz=pRz =Qq‚Äč(z‚ÄčpRz,z2/a2,z2‚ÄčőľR2)=e‚ąíi‚Äčz‚ÄčpRz,absentsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĄ0ūĚĎěūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎé2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßdisplaystylequad=tildeQq(zpRz,z2/a2,z2muR2)=e-izpRz,, where qūĚĎěq denotes the quark state, pőľsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚúápmu is the external momentum, and ‚Äú0‚ÄĚ in the superscript stands for the tree-level matrix element. The choices of őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR and pRzsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßpRz are independent of őľūĚúámu and PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, and pRz=PzsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÖūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpRz=Pz was used in Refs. citebib.bib34, bib.bib26. It should be noted that on the lattice, due to the breaking of chiral symmetry, the vector-like quark Wilson line operator Oő≥őľ‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚúáūĚĎßtildeOgammamu(z) can mix with the scalar operator OūĚüŹ‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎā1ūĚĎßtildeObf 1(z), as has been discussed in Refs. citebib.bib56, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib66, bib.bib67. After considering the mixing effects, the same factorization formula can still be applied to the RI/MOM quasi-PDF from lattice QCD.
content_sections.V Numerical results: In this section we numerically analyze the quasi-PDF, spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF by studying how the matching coefficients in Eqs. (,) change the PDF. The quasi-PDF has already been studied in this manner for the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS, transverse momentum cut-off, and RI/MOM schemes in Ref. citebib.bib26. Our new MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS result for the matching is given in Eq. , and leads to stable convolution integrals. We also compare the differences between using hadron momentum pz=PzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz and the parton momentum pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz for the matching coefficient in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. We take őď=ő≥0őďsuperscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0 for the results here. As an example we use for our analysis the unpolarized iso-vector parton distribution, fu‚ąíd‚Äč(x,őľ)=fu‚Äč(x,őľ)‚ąífd‚Äč(x,őľ)‚ąífu¬Į‚Äč(‚ąíx,őľ)+fd¬Į‚Äč(‚ąíx,őľ),subscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĘūĚĎĎūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĘūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĎūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎď¬ĮūĚĎĘūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎď¬ĮūĚĎĎūĚĎ•ūĚúádisplaystyle fu-d(x,mu)=fu(x,mu)-fd(x,mu)-fbaru(-x,mu)+fbard(-x,mu), where we include fu¬Į‚Äč(‚ąíx,őľ)=‚ąífu¬Į‚Äč(x,őľ)subscriptūĚĎď¬ĮūĚĎĘūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎď¬ĮūĚĎĘūĚĎ•ūĚúáfbaru(-x,mu)=-fbaru(x,mu) and fd¬Į‚Äč(‚ąíx,őľ)=‚ąífd¬Į‚Äč(x,őľ)subscriptūĚĎď¬ĮūĚĎĎūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎď¬ĮūĚĎĎūĚĎ•ūĚúáfbard(-x,mu)=-fbard(x,mu), the anti-parton distributions. For ease of comparison, we use the next-to-leading-order iso-vector PDF fu‚ąídsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĘūĚĎĎfu-d from MSTW 2008citebib.bib4 with the corresponding running coupling őĪs‚Äč(őľ)subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†ūĚúáalphas(mu). To implement the plus functions in the numerical calculation, we impose a soft cutoff |y‚ąíx|10‚ąínūĚĎ¶superscript10ūĚĎõ|y|>10-n to test the convergence of the convolution integral. We find that all the results presented below are insensitive to mūĚĎöm and nūĚĎõn. The fact that our result in Eq. has terms outside the plus function at 111 in each of the őĺ‚ąą[1,‚ąě]ūĚúČ1xiin[1,infty] and őĺ‚ąą[‚ąí‚ąě,0]ūĚúČ0xiin[-infty,0] intervals is important for ensuring that our MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS result for CūĚź∂C is insensitive to the |y|>10‚ąínūĚĎ¶superscript10ūĚĎõ|y|>10-n cutoff. This was not the case for the quasi-PDF that was defined with a transverse momentum cutoffcitebib.bib24. The RI/MOM scheme resultcitebib.bib26 also does not suffer from this issue. In Fig. we compare the PDF with the quasi-PDF in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme obtained from the convolution in Eq. using our one-loop result in Eq. . We observe that changing from pz=PzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz to the correct pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz shifts the result in the physical region by a considerable amount. The same type of comparison can be made for the pseudo-PDF in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme by applying the factorization formula in Eq. and matching coefficient in Eq. . In Fig. a we compare the PDF and pseudo-PDF and their dependence on the factorization scale őľūĚúámu, while in Fig. b we include the dependence of the pseudo-PDF on the distance |z|ūĚĎß|z|. Since the matching coefficient in Eq. is similar to the parton splitting function except for the nontrivial finite constants, matching the PDF to the pseudo-PDF is analogous to evolving the PDF from őľūĚúámu to the scale of 1/|z|1ūĚĎß1/|z|. This evolution has been calculated in Refs. citebib.bib39. The variation of |z|ūĚĎß|z| has a similar effect to the PDF evolution, as is observed in the right panel of Fig. . When |z|‚Äčőľ=1ūĚĎßūĚúá1|z|mu=1, the logarithm is zero, and the matching effect from ūĚíěūĚíěcal C is determined by the nontrivial constants in Eq. , which shifts the PDF downward in the large-xūĚĎ•x region and upward in the small-xūĚĎ•x region. Finally, we can make a similar comparison for the spatial correlator in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme obtained with Eq. and Eq. . Its real and imaginary parts are even and odd functions of ő∂ūĚúĀzeta respectively, and are shown in Fig. . Again we show the residual dependence on őľūĚúámu and |z|ūĚĎß|z| which are similar to that for the pseudo-PDF. The matching broadens the curves in the coordinate space. The spatial correlator renormalized in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme does not exhibit vector current (or particle number) conservation, which can be clearly seen from the fact that the real part of the distribution is not equal to 1 at ő∂=0ūĚúĀ0zeta=0 (except for the special case where |z|‚ÄčőľūĚĎßūĚúá|z|mu is tuned to cancel the constant terms in the one-loop ūĚíěūĚíěcal C).
content_sections.VI Implications for lattice calculations: Our proof in Sec. makes clear the relationship between the renormalized quasi-PDF, spatial correlator, and pseduo-PDF distributions. As a practical matter there are a few different ways in which these equations can be used to convert a lattice calculation of the spatial correlator QūĚĎĄtildeQ into a PDF. Three examples are 1) first Fourier transform to the quasi-PDF with Eq. , and then use Eq. , 2) first Fourier transform to the pseudo-PDF with Eq. , and then use Eq. , and 3) first match to the Fourier transform the position space PDF Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀūĚúáQ(zeta,mu) using Eq. , and then transform it to the PDF with the inverse of Eq. . Since the numerical implementation of these steps may have slightly different systematics it is interesting to compare them, or to use more than one approach in order to reduce uncertainties. According to the analysis in Sec. , for the factorization formula of the Euclidean distributions to work, one must calculate the same spatial correlator with small distance z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 and large momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz so that the dynamical and kinematic higher-twist effects are suppressed. For practical lattice calculations, this means that there is only a finite number of useful data points in (z,Pz)ūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß(z,Pz) that we can use to extract the PDF. To illustrate this, consider a 483√ó64superscript48364483times 64 lattice with spacing a=0.09ūĚĎé0.09a=0.09 fm. The distance of the spatial correlation zūĚĎßz is in units of a‚ąľ1/2.2‚ÄčGeV‚ąí1similar-toūĚĎé12.2superscriptGeV1asim 1/2.2 rm GeV-1, and the nucleon momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz is in units of 2‚ÄčŌÄ/(48‚Äča)‚ąľ0.29similar-to2ūĚúč48ūĚĎé0.292pi/(48a)sim 0.29 GeV. Let us take őõQCD‚ąľ0.3similar-tosubscriptőõQCD0.3Lambdarm QCDsim 0.3 GeV. In principal the target mass corrections can be subtracted. If we consider various values z=m‚ÄčaūĚĎßūĚĎöūĚĎéz=ma and Pz=n‚ąó2‚ÄčŌÄ/(48‚Äča)superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎõ2ūĚúč48ūĚĎéPz=n*2pi/(48a) for integer mūĚĎöm and nūĚĎõn, then to satisfy z‚ÄčőõQCD‚Č™1much-less-thanūĚĎßsubscriptőõQCD1zLambdarm QCDll 1 and Pz‚ČęőõQCDmuch-greater-thansuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptőõQCDPzggLambdarm QCD, we must have m‚Č™11,n‚Čę1. formulae-sequencemuch-less-thanūĚĎö11much-greater-thanūĚĎõ1displaystyle mll 11,,,,,quad ngg 1,. To control the higher-twist correction at 20%percent2020%, i. e. z2‚ÄčőõQCD2‚ąľ0.2,őõQCD2/Pz2‚ąľ0.2formulae-sequencesimilar-tosuperscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD20.2similar-tosuperscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß20.2z2Lambdarm QCD2sim 0.2, Lambdarm QCD2/Pz2sim 0.2, we can only choose m=0,1,2,3,4,n=3,4,5,6,‚čĮ,formulae-sequenceūĚĎö01234ūĚĎõ3456‚čĮdisplaystyle m=0,1,2,3,4,,,,,,n=3,4,5,6,cdots,, where the largest value for nūĚĎõn is limited by what is practical in current lattice simulations. Six is the largest number of units attained in Ref. citebib.bib42. For quasi-PDF calculations, there are 4√ó2+1=942194times 2+1=9 useful data points for each fixed momentum |Pz|superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß|Pz|; for pseudo-PDF calculation, there are only 4√ó2=84284times 2=8 useful data points for each fixed |z|ūĚĎß|z|. In either case, it is anticipated that a direct Fourier transform with respect to zūĚĎßz or ő∂=z‚ÄčPzūĚúĀūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßzeta=zPz will lead to oscillation in xūĚĎ•x-space and incorrect prediction for the small-xūĚĎ•x region due to the truncation in coordinate space. This has been observed in a recent lattice calculation of the quasi-PDF in Ref. citebib.bib34. Methods have been developed in recent works to eliminate the oscillation from the truncation effectcitebib.bib35, bib.bib68 in the quasi-PDF, while the higher-twist contributions at large zūĚĎßz still need to be systematically corrected. It should be noted that the above is a rough estimate of the higher-twist corrections since the prefactor of z2‚ÄčőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2z2Lambdarm QCD2 could be smaller than 1. Their actual significance can only be quantitatively determined from lattice simulations. To fully take advantage of all the useful data points, we can evolve them to either the same z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 or PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz according to the perturbative analysis, which has been studied in Refs. citebib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib62 for the spatial correlator. However, since the evolution equation of the spatial correlator in ln‚Ā°z2superscriptūĚĎß2ln z2 or ln‚Ā°Pz2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2ln Pz2 follows a nonlocal convolution in ő∂=z‚ÄčPzūĚúĀūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßzeta=zPz or zūĚĎßz, one has to know the full information in coordinate space to do the evolution. With limited number of data points, either large uncertainties or adopting a model-dependent assumption about the shape is inevitable. To improve the precision of either approach, the only way forward is to have finer lattice spacing aūĚĎéa so that we could have more data points which satisfy |z|‚Č™őõQCD‚ąí1much-less-thanūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptőõQCD1|z|llLambdarm QCD-1 and larger nucleon momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz. With increasing PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, the valence distribution of the nucleon is contracted in the zūĚĎßz direction, so the spatial correlation of valence quarks is shrinked into smaller distance in zūĚĎßz. If PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz is large enough, the spatial correlation will fall off quickly within |z|<őõQCD‚ąí1ūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptőõQCD1|z|<Lambdarm QCD-1, then the truncation error from Fourier transform will be significantly reduced. On the other hand, if we interpret the spatial correlation as the spatial correlator, its shape will not change under a Lorentz boost because it is a scalar function of ő∂=z‚ÄčPzūĚúĀūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßzeta=zPz and z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2. Nevertheless, finer lattice spacing aūĚĎéa allows for calculation with a wider range of PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, thus covering larger values of ő∂=z‚ÄčPzūĚúĀūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßzeta=zPz to reduce the truncation error. Since the number of useful data points increases quadratically with 1/a1ūĚĎé1/a, a more precise lattice calculation with controlled systematic errors will be available in the future.
content_sections.VII Conclusions: Starting with a Euclidean operator product expansion for products of gauge invariant operators in QCD, we have derived the factorization formulas for the quasi-PDF, spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF. The three Euclidean distribution functions are related observables, and all follow from the same fundamental factorization. For the spatial correlator this derivation implies that the ratio in Eq. does scale in z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2, but needs the small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization formula in Eq. to extract the PDF. Our derivation for the factorization formula applies when the renormalized spatial correlator is defined in any scheme. The OPE used here could also be used to systematically derive factorization formulas for power corrections to Eq. , which will involve matching onto higher-twist parton distributions. (The numerical relevance of these corrections is considered in Ref. citebib.bib30. ) Note that LaMET is not equivalent to the expansion from the OPE, as the former is more general and can be applied to the lattice calculations of other quantities, for example the TMD-PDF where a simple OPE does not exist. Our derivation of the factorization formula for the quasi-PDF also verifies that the parton momentum pzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz in the matching coefficient in Eq. has to be pz=y‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=yPz, which makes a considerable difference for the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS matching result when compared with pz=PzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz (see Fig. ). The proper pzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz should therefore be used in lattice calculations of the PDF in the LaMET approach. As a non-trivial test of relations between the various distributions and factorization formulas we have considered results at one-loop in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. We have derived a corrected results for the coefficient CūĚź∂C for the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme, given in Eq. , which leads to convergent results in the convolution integral. We have also computed the one-loop MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS result for the Wilson coefficient ūĚíěūĚíěcal C appearing in the spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF factorizations. A numerical analysis of these one-loop corrections in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS has also been provided. The one-loop matching coefficient ūĚíěūĚíěcal C has a smaller effect for the pseudo-PDF than CūĚź∂C does for quasi-PDF, as can seen by comparing Figs. and . Given systematic uncertainties in manipulating the lattice data, it is potentially interesting to consider using the same lattice data on the spatial correlator to extract the parton distribution function using both the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF approaches. There are several different ways of implementing the factorization formula to calculate the PDF from lattice data for the spatial correlator QūĚĎĄtildeQ, which we have discussed in Sec. . One always has to work with short distance correlation and large nucleon momentum to reduce higher-twist corrections. This limits the number of useful data points from lattice calculations as described in Sec. . To achieve precision calculations without making model assumptions it will be highly desirable to move towards finer lattice spacing to increase the number of effective data points.

all_papers.title:
A guide to light-cone PDFs from Lattice QCD: an overview of approaches,
  techniques and results
all_papers.abstract:
Within the theory of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD), the rich structure of
hadrons can be quantitatively characterized, among others, using a basis of
universal non-perturbative functions: parton distribution functions (PDFs),
generalized parton distributions (GPDs), transverse-momentum dependent
distributions (TMDs) and distribution amplitudes (DAs). For more than half a
century, there has been a joint experimental and theoretical effort to obtain
these partonic functions. However, the complexity of the strong interactions
has placed severe limitations, and first-principle results on the distributions
was extracted mostly from their moments computed in Lattice QCD. Recently,
breakthrough ideas changed the landscape and several approaches were proposed
to access the distributions themselves on the lattice.
  In this paper, we review in considerable detail approaches directly related
to partonic distributions. We highlight a recent idea proposed by X. Ji on
extracting quasi-distributions that spawned renewed interest in the whole field
and sparked the largest amount of numerical studies of Lattice QCD. We discuss
theoretical and practical developments, including challenges that had to be
overcome, with some yet to be handled. We also review numerical results,
including a discussion based on evolving understanding of the underlying
concepts and theoretical and practical progress. Particular attention is given
to important aspects that validated the quasi-distribution approach, such as
renormalization, matching to light-cone distributions and lattice techniques.
  In addition to a thorough discussion of quasi-distributions, we consider
other approaches: hadronic tensor, auxiliary quark methods, pseudo-PDFs, OPE
without OPE and good lattice cross sections. In closing, we provide prospects
of the field, with emphasis on the necessary conditions to obtain results with
controlled uncertainties.

qualitative: Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) provides a framework to extract light-cone parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice QCD by studying quasi-PDFs at large hadron momentum.
LaMET is based on the factorization of the quasi-PDF into a hard matching kernel and a light-cone PDF, valid in the limit of large hadron momentum along the quantization axis.
The quasi-PDF is defined as the matrix element of a spatially separated quark-antiquark operator in a boosted hadron state, which can be computed on the lattice.
LaMET enables the connection between non-perturbative lattice calculations and physical light-cone PDFs through a perturbative matching procedure.
The matching between quasi-PDFs and light-cone PDFs is performed order-by-order in the strong coupling constant őĪ_s, with leading-order and higher-order corrections being systematically computed.
LaMET requires a large but finite momentum P_z of the hadron on the lattice, with the limit P_z ‚Üí ‚ąě taken in a controlled way to recover the light-cone PDF.

The validity of LaMET relies on the separation of scales: the hadron momentum P_z is much larger than the non-perturbative scale őõ_QCD, allowing for a perturbative treatment of short-distance physics.
Renormalization of quasi-PDFs is necessary and must be performed in a way consistent with the matching procedure to ensure gauge invariance and remove ultraviolet divergences.
The matching kernel in LaMET is universal and process-independent, enabling the reuse of matching coefficients across different hadrons and observables.
LaMET has been instrumental in revitalizing lattice QCD studies of parton distributions, leading to a surge in numerical simulations and theoretical developments.
quantitative: The quasi-PDF is defined as: Q(x, P_z) = ‚ąę dz e^{i x P_z z} ‚ü®P_z|ŌąŐĄ(z)ő≥^0 U(z,0)Ōą(0)|P_z‚ü©, where P_z is the large momentum along the z-axis.
The matching relation between the quasi-PDF and the light-cone PDF is: Q(x, P_z) = ‚ąę_x^1 dy K(x/y, őĪ_s(őľ), P_z/őľ) f(y, őľ), where K is the perturbatively calculable matching kernel.
At leading order in őĪ_s, the matching kernel is: K_LO(x/y, őĪ_s) = őī(x/y - 1) + őĪ_s/(2ŌÄ) (2(1 + (1 - x/y)^2)/(x/y) - 2(1 - x/y)) ln(P_z^2 / őľ^2) + O(őĪ_s^2).
The matching kernel is known up to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) in őĪ_s for the unpolarized case.
The LaMET factorization theorem requires P_z ‚Čę őõ_QCD, with typical lattice studies using P_z ‚Č• 2 GeV to ensure the validity of the perturbative expansion.
The convergence of the perturbative matching series is improved by using the őõ_QCD scale as the matching scale őľ ‚Čą őõ_QCD, typically őľ ‚Čą 0.3‚Äď0.5 GeV.
The leading-order matching correction is of order őĪ_s ln(P_z^2 / őľ^2), which grows logarithmically with P_z, necessitating higher-order corrections for accurate results.
The lattice spacing a and volume L^3 must satisfy a ‚Č™ 1/P_z and L ‚Čę 1/P_z to avoid discretization and finite-volume effects.
The momentum P_z is typically discretized as P_z = 2ŌÄn/L with n = 1, 2, ..., up to n_max ‚Čą L/2, with n = 1 corresponding to the lowest non-zero momentum mode.
The quasi-PDF is extracted from lattice correlation functions with a spatial separation z = a n_z, where n_z is an integer, and the Fourier transform is performed over z.

authors: Krzysztof Cichy, Martha Constantinou

content_sections.INTRODUCTION: Among the frontiers of nuclear and particle physics is the investigation of the structure of hadrons, the architecture elements of the visible matter. Hadrons consist of quarks and gluons (together called partons), which are governed by one of the four fundamental forces of nature, the strong force. The latter is described by the theory of Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). Understanding QCD can have great impact on many aspects of science, from the subnuclear interactions to astrophysics, and thus, a quantitative description is imperative. However, this is a very challenging task, as QCD is a highly nonlinear theory. This led to the development of phenomenological tools such as models, which have provided important input on the hadron structure. However, studies from first principles are desirable. An ideal ab initio formulation is Lattice QCD, a space-time discretization of the theory that allows the study of the properties of fundamental particles numerically, starting from the original QCD Lagrangian. Despite the extensive experimental program that was developed and evolved since the first exploration of the structure of the protoncitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, a deep understanding of the hadrons‚Äô internal dynamics is yet to be achieved. Hadrons have immensely rich composition due to the complexity of the strong interactions that, for example, forces the partons to exist only inside the hadrons (color confinement), making the extraction of information from experiments very difficult. Understanding internal properties of the hadrons requires the development of a set of appropriate quantities that can be accessed both experimentally and theoretically. The QCD factorization provides such a formalism and can relate measurements from different processes to parton distributions. These are non-perturbative quantities describing the parton dynamics within a hadron, and have the advantage of being universal, that is, do not depend on the process used for their extraction. The comprehensive study of parton distributions can provide a wealth of information on the hadrons, in terms of variables defined in the longitudinal direction (with respect to the hadron momentum) in momentum space, and two transverse directions. The latter can be defined either in position or momentum space. These variables are: 1. the longitudinal momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x carried by the parton, 2. the longitudinal momentum fraction őĺūĚúČxi obtained via the longitudinal momentum transferred to the hadron, 3. the momentum kTsubscriptūĚĎėūĚĎákT transverse to the hadron direction of movement. Parton distributions can be classified into three categories based on their dependence on xūĚĎ•x, őĺūĚúČxi, kTsubscriptūĚĎėūĚĎákT and the momentum transferred to the hadron, tūĚĎ°t, as described below. Parton distributions functions (PDFs) are one-dimensional objects and represent the number density of partons with longitudinal momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x while the hadron is moving with a large momentum. Generalized parton distributions (GPDs) citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7 depend on the longitudinal momentum fractions xūĚĎ•x and őĺūĚúČxi and in addition, on the momentum transferred to the parent hadron, tūĚĎ°t. They provide a partial description of the three-dimensional structure. Transverse-momentum dependent parton distribution functions (TMDs) citebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12 describe the parton distribution in terms of the longitudinal momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x and the transverse momentum kTsubscriptūĚĎėūĚĎákT. They complement the three-dimensional picture of a hadron from GPDs. As is clear from the above classification, PDFs, GPDs and TMDs provide complementary information on parton distributions, and all of them are necessary to map out the three-dimensional structure of hadrons in spatial and momentum coordinates. Experimentally, these are accessed from different processes, with PDFs being measured in inclusive or semi-inclusive processes such as deep inelastic scattering (DIS) and semi-inclusive DIS (SIDIS), see e. g. Ref. citebib.bib13 for a review of DIS. GPDs are accessed in exclusive scattering processes such as deeply virtual Compton scattering (DVCS) citebib.bib14, and TMDs in hard processes in SIDIS citebib.bib10, bib.bib11. Most of the knowledge on the hadron structure is obtained from DIS and SIDIS data on PDFs, while the GPDs and TMDs are less known. More recently, data emerge from DVCS and Deeply Virtual Meson Production (DVMP) citebib.bib15. This includes measurements from HERMES, COMPASS, RHIC, Belle and Babar, E906/SeaQuest and the 12 GeV upgrade at JLab. A future Electron-Ion-Collider (EIC), that was strongly endorsed by the National Academy of Science, Engineering and Medicinecitebib.bib16, will be able to provide accurate data related to parton distributions and will advance dramatically our understanding on the hadron tomography. Together with the experimental efforts, theoretical advances are imperative in order to obtain a complete picture of hadrons. First, to interpret experimental data, global QCD analysescitebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26 are necessary that utilize the QCD factorization formalism and combine experimental data and theoretical calculations in perturbative QCD. Note that these are beyond the scope of this review and we refer the interested Reader to the above references and a recent community white papercitebib.bib27. Second, theoretical studies are needed to complement the experimental program, and in certain cases, provide valuable input. This is achieved using models of QCD and more importantly calculations from first principles. Model calculations have evolved and consist an important aspect of our understanding of parton structure. An example of such a model is the diquark spectator modelcitebib.bib28 that has been used for studies of parton distributions (for more details, see Sec. ). The main focus of the models discussed in Sec. is the one-dimensional hadron structure (xūĚĎ•x-dependence of PDFs), but more recently the interest has been extended to the development of techniques that are also applicable to GPDs and TMDs (some aspects are discussed in this review). Let us note that there have been studies related to TMDs from the lattice, and there is intense interest towards that direction (see, e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31 and references therein). Despite the tremendous progress in both the global analyses and models of QCD, parton distributions are not fully known, due to several limitations: global analyses techniques are not uniquely definedcitebib.bib22; certain kinematic regions are difficult to access, for instance the very small xūĚĎ•x-region citebib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34; and models cannot capture the full QCD dynamics. Hence, an ab initio calculation within Lattice QCD is crucial, and synergy with global fits and model calculations can lead to progress in the extraction of distribution functions. Lattice QCD provides an ideal formulation to study hadron structure and originates from the full QCD Lagrangian by defining the continuous equations on a discrete Euclidean four-dimensional lattice. This leads to equations with billions of degrees of freedom, and numerical simulations on supercomputers are carried out to obtain physical results. A non-perturbative tool, such as Lattice QCD, is particularly valuable at the hadronic energy scales, where perturbative methods are less reliable, or even fail altogether. Promising calculations from Lattice QCD have been reported for many years with the calculations of the low-lying hadron spectrum being such an example. More recently, Lattice QCD has provided pioneering results related to hadron structure, addressing, for instance, open questions, such as the spin decompositioncitebib.bib35 and the glue spincitebib.bib36 of the proton. Another example of the advances of numerical simulations within Lattice QCD is the calculation of certain hadronic contributions to the muon g‚ąí2ūĚĎĒ2g-2, for example the connected and leading disconnected hadronic light-by-light contributions (see recent reviews of Refs. citebib.bib37, bib.bib38). Direct calculations of distribution functions on a Euclidean lattice have not been feasible due to the time dependence of these quantities. A way around this limitation is the calculation on the lattice of moments of distribution functions (historically for PDFs and GPDs) and the physical PDFs can, in principle, be obtained from operator product expansion (OPE). Realistically, only the lowest moments of PDFs and GPDs can be computed (see e. g. citebib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44) due to large gauge noise in high moments, and also unavoidable power-divergent mixing with lower-dimensional operators. Combination of the two prevents a reliable and accurate calculation of moments beyond the second or third, and the reconstruction of the PDFs becomes unrealistic. Recent pioneering work of X. Jicitebib.bib45 has changed the landscape of lattice calculations with a proposal to compute equal-time correlators of momentum boosted hadrons, the so-called quasi-distributions. For large enough momenta, these can be related to the physical (light-cone) distributions via a matching procedure using large momentum effective theory (LaMET) (see Sec. and Sec. ). This possibility has opened new avenues for direct calculation of distribution functions from Lattice QCD and first investigations have revealed promising resultscitebib.bib46, bib.bib47 (see Sec. ). Despite the encouraging calculations, many theoretical and technical challenges needed to be clarified. One concern was whether the Euclidean quasi-PDFs and Minkowski light-cone PDFs have the same collinear divergence, which underlies the matching programme. In addition, quasi-PDFs are computed from matrix elements of non-local operators that include a Wilson line. This results in a novel type of power divergences and the question whether these operators are multiplicatively renormalizable remained unanswered for some time. While the theoretical community was addressing such issues, the lattice groups had to overcome technical difficulties related to the calculation of matrix elements of non-local operators, including how to obtain reliable results for a fast moving nucleon, and how to develop a non-perturbative renormalization prescription (see Sec. ). For theoretical and technical challenges, see Secs. - . Our current understanding on various aspects of quasi-PDFs has improved significantly, and lattice calculations of quasi-PDFs have extended to quantities that are not easily or reliably measured in experiments (see Secs. - ), such as the transversity PDFcitebib.bib48, bib.bib49. This new era of LQCD can provide high-precision input to experiments and test phenomenological models. The first studies on Ji‚Äôs proposal have appeared for the quark quasi-PDFs of the proton (see Secs. , ). Recently, the methodology has been extended to other hadrons, in particular mesonic PDFs and distribution amplitudes (DAs). Progress towards this direction is presented in Sec. . Other recent reviews on the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of PDFs from Lattice QCD calculations can be found in Refs. citebib.bib50, bib.bib27, bib.bib51. The quasi-PDFs approach is certainly promising and can be generalized to study gluon quasi-PDFs, quasi-GPDs and quasi-TMDs. In such investigations, technical difficulties of different nature arise and must be explored. First studies are presented here. Apart from the quasi-distribution approach, we also review other approaches for obtaining the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of partonic functions, both the theoretical ideas underlying them (see Sec. ) and their numerical explorations (Sec. ). The central focus of the review are studies of the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of PDFs. We present work that appears in the literature until November 10, 2018 (published, or on the arXiv). The discussion is extended to conference proceedings for recent work that has not been published elsewhere. The presentation is based on chronological order, unless there is a need to include follow-up work by the same group on the topic under discussion. Our main priority is to report on the progress of the field, but also to comment on important aspects of the described material based on theoretical developments that appeared in later publications, or follow-up work. To keep this review at a reasonable length, we present selected aspects of each publication discussed in the main text and we encourage the interested Reader to consult the referred work. Permission for reproduction of the figures has been granted by the Authors and the scientific journals (in case of published work). The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , we introduce methods that have been proposed to access the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of PDFs from the lattice, which include a method based on the hadronic tensor, auxiliary quark field approaches, quasi and pseudo distributions, a method based on OPE, and the good lattice cross sections approach. A major part of this review is dedicated to quasi-PDFs, which are presented in more detail in Sec. , together with preliminary studies within lattice QCD. The numerical calculations of the early studies have motivated an intense theoretical activity to develop models of quasi-distributions, which are presented in Sec. . In Sec. , we focus on theoretical aspects of the approach of quasi-PDFs, that is whether a Euclidean definition can reproduce the light-cone PDFs, as well as the renormalizability of operators entering the calculations of quark and gluon quasi-PDFs. The lattice techniques for quasi-PDFs and difficulties that one must overcome are summarized in Sec. . Recent developments on the extraction of renormalization functions related to logarithmic and/or power divergences are explained in Sec. , while Sec. is dedicated to the matching procedure within LaMET. Lattice results on the quark quasi-PDFs for the nucleon are presented in Sec. . The quasi-PDFs approach has been extended to gluon distributions, as well as studies of mesons, as demonstrated in Sec. . In Sec. , we briefly describe results from the alternative approaches presented in Sec. . We close the review with Sec. that gives a summary and future prospects. We discuss the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of PDFs and DAs, as well as possibilities to study other quantities, such as GPDs and TMDs. A glossary of abbreviations is given in Appendix A.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS: In this section, we briefly outline different approaches for obtaining the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of partonic distribution functions, in particular collinear PDFs. We first recall the problem with directly employing the definitions of such functions on the lattice, using the example of unpolarized PDFs. The unpolarized PDF, denoted here q‚Äč(x)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•q(x), is defined on the light cone: q‚Äč(x)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě+‚ąěd‚Äčőĺ‚ąí4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčx‚ÄčP+‚Äčőĺ‚ąí‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí)‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčW‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚúČ4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúČquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚúČsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎäsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉq(x)=int-infty+inftyfracdxi-4pie-ixP+xi- P|overlinepsi(xi-)gamma+W(xi-,0)psi|P, where |P‚ü©ketūĚĎÉ|P is the hadron state with momentum PőľsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚúáPmu, in the standard relativistic normalization2In the remainder of the paper, the standard relativistic normalization is always assumed and the states are labeled with the hadron momentum and other labels, if necessary. , the light-cone vectors are taken as v¬Ī=(v0¬Īv3)/2superscriptūĚĎ£plus-or-minusplus-or-minussuperscriptūĚĎ£0superscriptūĚĎ£32vpm=(v0pm v3)/sqrt2, W‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)=e‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0őĺ‚ąíūĚĎĎő∑‚ąí‚ÄčA+‚Äč(ő∑‚ąí)ūĚĎäsuperscriptūĚúČ0superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0superscriptūĚúČdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚúāsuperscriptūĚźīsuperscriptūĚúāW(xi-,0)=e-igint0xi-deta-A+(eta-) is the Wilson line connecting the light-cone points 00 and őĺ‚ąísuperscriptūĚúČxi-, while the factorization scale is kept implicit. Such light-cone correlations are not accessible on a Euclidean spacetime, because the light cone directions shrink to one point at the origin. As discussed in the Introduction, this fact prevented lattice extraction of PDFs for many years, apart from their low moments, reachable via local matrix elements and the operator product expansion (OPE). However, since the number of moments that can be reliably calculated is strongly limited, alternative approaches were sought for to yield the full Bjorken-xūĚĎ•x dependence. The common feature of all the approaches is that they rely to some extent on the factorization framework. For a lattice observable Q‚Äč(x,őľR)ūĚĎĄūĚĎ•subscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖQ(x,muR) that is to be used to extract PDFs, one can generically write: 3For simplicity, we neglect possible mixings under factorization. Q‚Äč(x,őľR)=‚ąę‚ąí11d‚Äčyy‚ÄčC‚Äč(xy,őľF,őľR)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľF),ūĚĎĄūĚĎ•subscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖsuperscriptsubscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚúáūĚźĻsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖūĚĎěūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚúáūĚźĻQ(x,muR)=int-11fracdyy,Cleft(fracxy,muF,muRright)q(y,muF), where C‚Äč(x/y,őľF,őľR)ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚúáūĚźĻsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖC(x/y,muF,muR) is a perturbatively computable function and q‚Äč(y,őľF2)ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚźĻ2q(y,muF2) is the desired PDF. In the above expression we distinguish between the factorization scale, őľFsubscriptūĚúáūĚźĻmuF, and the renormalization scale, őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR. These scales are usually taken to be the same and, hence, from now on we will adopt this choice and take őľF=őľR‚Č°őľsubscriptūĚúáūĚźĻsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖūĚúámuF=muRequivmu. Lattice approaches use different observables QūĚĎĄQ that fall into two classes: 1. observables that are generalizations of light-cone functions such that they can be accessed on the lattice; such generalized functions have direct xūĚĎ•x-dependence, but xūĚĎ•x does not have the same partonic interpretation as the Bjorken-xūĚĎ•x, 2. observables in terms of which hadronic tensor can be written; the hadronic tensor is then decomposed into structure functions like F1subscriptūĚźĻ1F1 and g1subscriptūĚĎĒ1g1, which are factorizable into PDFs. Below, we provide the general idea for several proposals that were introduced in recent years.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Hadronic tenso: All the information about a DIS cross section is contained in the hadronic tensor citebib.bib52, bib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, defined by Wőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(p,q,őĽ,őĽ‚Ä≤)=14‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč‚ąęd4‚Äčx‚Äčei‚Äčq‚Äčx‚Äč‚ü®p,őĽ‚Ä≤|[Jőľ‚Äč(x),JőĹ‚Äč]|p,őĽ‚ü©,subscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąūĚĎĚūĚĎěūĚúÜsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤14ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎĎ4ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎ•quantum-operator-productūĚĎĚsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤subscriptūĚźĹūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚźĹūĚúą0ūĚĎĚūĚúÜWmunu(p,q,lambda,lambdaprime)=frac14piint,d4xeiqx p,lambdaprime|[Jmu(x),Jnu]|p,lambda, where |p,őĽ‚ü©ketūĚĎĚūĚúÜ|p,lambda is the hadron state labeled by its momentum pūĚĎĚp and polarization őĽūĚúÜlambda, qūĚĎěq ‚Äď virtual photon momentum, Jőľ‚Äč(x)subscriptūĚźĹūĚúáūĚĎ•Jmu(x) ‚Äď electromagnetic current at point xūĚĎ•x. The hadronic tensor can be related to DIS structure functions and hence, in principle, PDFs can be extracted from it. Wőľ‚ÄčőĹsubscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąWmunu is the imaginary part of the forward Compton amplitude and can be written as the current-current correlation function, Wőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(p,q)=‚ü®p,őĽ‚Ä≤|‚ąęd4‚Äčx4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčq‚Äčx‚ÄčJőľ‚Äč(x)‚ÄčJőĹ‚Äč|p,őĽ‚ü©őĽ‚ÄčőĽ‚Ä≤¬Į,subscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąūĚĎĚūĚĎěsubscriptquantum-operator-productūĚĎĚsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎĎ4ūĚĎ•4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscriptūĚźĹūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚźĹūĚúą0ūĚĎĚūĚúÜ¬ĮūĚúÜsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤Wmunu(p,q)= p,lambdaprime|intfracd4x4pieiqxJmu(x)Jnu|p,lambdaoverlinelambdalambdaprime, where the subscript őĽ‚ÄčőĽ‚Ä≤¬Į¬ĮūĚúÜsuperscriptūĚúÜ‚Ä≤overlinelambdalambdaprime denotes averaging over polarizations. The approach has been introduced as a possible way of investigating hadronic structure on the lattice by K. -F. Liu and S. -J. Dong already in 1993. They also proposed a decomposition of the contributions to the hadronic tensor according to different topologies of the quark paths, into valence and connected or disconnected sea ones. In this way, they addressed the origin of Gottfried sum rule violation. A crucial aspect for the implementation in Lattice QCD is the fact that the hadronic tensor Wőľ‚ÄčőĹsubscriptūĚĎäūĚúáūĚúąWmunu, defined in Minkowski spacetime, can be obtained from the Euclidean path-integral formalism citebib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, by considering ratios of suitable four-point and two-point functions. In the limit of the points being sufficiently away from both the source and the sink, where the hadron is created or annihilated, the matrix element receives contributions from only the ground state of the hadron. Reconstructing the Minkowski tensor from its Euclidean counterpart is formally defined by an inverse Laplace transform of the latter and can, in practice, be carried out using e. g. the maximum entropy method or the Backus-Gilbert method. Nevertheless, this aspect is highly non-trivial and improvements thereof are looked for. As pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib59, a significant role may be played by power-law finite volume effects related to the matrix elements being defined in Euclidean spacetime. A similar phenomenon was recently observed also in the context of K‚ąíK¬ĮūĚźĺ¬ĮūĚźĺK-barK mixing citebib.bib60. Another difficulty of the hadronic tensor approach on the lattice is the necessity to calculate four-point correlation functions, which is computationally more intensive than for three-point functions, the standard tools of hadron structure investigations on the lattice. However, the theoretical appeal of the hadronic tensor approach recently sparked renewed interest in it citebib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63. We describe some exploratory results in Sec. .
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Auxiliary scal: In 1998, a new method was proposed to calculate light-cone wave functions (LCWFs) on the lattice citebib.bib64. This finds its motivation from the fact that LCWFs enter in the description of many processes, such as electroweak decays and meson production. LCWF is the leading term of the full hadronic wave function in the őõQCD2/p2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎĚ2Lambdarm QCD2/p2 expansion, where pūĚĎĚp is the hadron momentum. For concreteness, we write the defining expression for the most studied LCWF, the one of the pion, ő¶ŌÄ‚Äč(u)subscriptő¶ūĚúčūĚĎĘPhipi(u), where uūĚĎĘu is the momentum fraction: ‚ü®0|d¬Į‚Äč‚ÄčW‚Äč(0,x)‚Äčő≥őľ‚Äčő≥5‚Äču‚Äč(x)|ŌÄ‚Äč(pőľ)‚ü©x2=0=‚ąíi‚Äčpőľ‚ÄčfŌÄ‚Äč‚ąę01ūĚĎĎu‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äču‚ÄčPŌÄ‚Äčx‚Äčő¶ŌÄ‚Äč(u),subscriptquantum-operator-product0¬ĮūĚĎĎ0ūĚĎä0ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚõĺūĚúásubscriptūĚõĺ5ūĚĎĘūĚĎ•ūĚúčsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎ•20ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúásubscriptūĚĎďūĚúčsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎĘsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎĘsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚúčūĚĎ•subscriptő¶ūĚúčūĚĎĘ 0|bardW(0,x)gammamugamma5u(x)|pi(pmu),x2=0=-ipmufpiint01du,e-iuPpixPhipi(u), with: |ŌÄ‚Äč(p)‚ü©ketūĚúčūĚĎĚ|pi(p) ‚Äď boosted pion state, |0‚ü©ket0|0 ‚Äď vacuum state, fŌÄsubscriptūĚĎďūĚúčfpi ‚Äď pion decay constant. W‚Äč(0,x)ūĚĎä0ūĚĎ•W(0,x) is the Wilson line that ensures gauge invariance of the matrix element. The essence of the idea is to ‚Äúobserve‚ÄĚ and study on the lattice the partonic constituents of hadrons instead of the hadrons themselves citebib.bib65, bib.bib66. As shown in Ref. citebib.bib64, the pion LCWF can be extracted by considering a vacuum-to-pion expectation value of the axial vector current with quark fields separated in spacetime. Gauge invariance is ensured by a scalar quark propagator with color quantum numbers of a quark, and at a large momentum transfer. The relation between the Fourier transform of this matrix element, computed on the lattice, and the pion LCWF, ő¶ŌÄsubscriptő¶ūĚúčPhipi, is given by the following formula: Főľ‚Äč(pŌÄ‚Üí,q‚Üí;t,tŌÄ)superscriptūĚźĻūĚúá‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúč‚ÜíūĚĎěūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúčdisplaystyle Fmu(vecppi,vecq;t,tpi) ‚Č°displaystyleequiv ‚ąęd3‚ÄčxŌÄ‚Äčd3‚Äčxt‚Äče‚ąíi‚ÄčpŌÄ‚Üí‚čÖxŌÄ‚Üí‚ąíi‚Äčq‚Üí‚čÖxt‚Üí‚ÄčeEŌÄ‚Äč(tŌÄ‚ąít)‚Äč‚ü®ŌÄ‚Äč(pŌÄ)|u‚Äč(xt‚Üí,t)‚ÄčS‚Äč(xt‚Üí,t;0)‚Äčő≥őľ‚Äčő≥5‚Äčd¬Į‚Äč|0‚ü©superscriptūĚĎĎ3subscriptūĚĎ•ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎĎ3subscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúč‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚúč‚čÖūĚĎĖ‚ÜíūĚĎě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚźłūĚúčsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúčūĚĎ°quantum-operator-productūĚúčsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚúčūĚĎĘ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°ūĚĎ°ūĚĎÜ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°ūĚĎ°0subscriptūĚõĺūĚúásubscriptūĚõĺ5¬ĮūĚĎĎ00displaystyleint d3xpid3xte-ivecppicdotvecxpi-ivecqcdotvecxteEpi(tpi-t)pi(ppi)|u(vecxt,t)S(vecxt,t;0)gammamugamma5bard|0 where q‚Üí‚ÜíūĚĎěvecq ‚Äď momentum transfer, S‚Äč(xt‚Üí,t;0)ūĚĎÜ‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎ°ūĚĎ°0S(vecxt,t;0) ‚Äď scalar colored propagator, uisubscriptūĚĎĘūĚĎĖui ‚Äď discrete set of partonic momentum fractions (allowed by the discretized momenta in a finite volume). The spacetime points are explained in Fig. , which shows the three-point function that needs to be computed. The interval tŌÄ‚ąítsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúčūĚĎ°tpi-t needs to be large to have an on-shell pion. To extract the LCWF, several conditions need to be satisfied: injected pion momentum needs to be large (to have a ‚Äúfrozen‚ÄĚ pion and see its partonic constituents), the scalar quark needs to carry large energy, the time tūĚĎ°t (time of momentum transfer and ‚Äútransformation‚ÄĚ of a quark to a scalar quark) has to be small (to prevent quantum decoherence and hadronization) and the lattice volume large enough (to minimize effects of discretizing parton momenta). We refer to the original papers for an extensive discussion of these conditions. An exploratory study of the approach was presented in Refs. citebib.bib65, bib.bib66 and later in Ref. citebib.bib67, both in the quenched approximation. Naturally, the conditions outlined above are very difficult to satisfy simultaneously on the lattice, due to restrictions from the finite lattice spacing and the finite volume. However, the knowledge of the full hadronic wave function from first principles would be very much desired and further exploration of this approach may be interesting. In particular, integrals of hadronic wave functions over transverse momenta yield distribution amplitudes and PDFs.
content_sections.xūĚĎ•x-DEPENDENCE OF PDFS Auxiliary heav: In 2005, another method was proposed citebib.bib58 to access hadron structure on the lattice, including PDFs. The idea relies on simulating on the lattice the Compton scattering tensor, using currents that couple physical light quarks of a hadron with a purely valence fictitious heavy quark. In the continuum limit, one can extract matrix elements of local operators in the OPE in the same renormalization scheme in which the Wilson coefficients are calculated. In this way, one gets the moments of PDFs. The crucial difference with respect to standard lattice calculations of moments is that the approach removes power divergent mixings with lower-dimensional operators, unavoidable in the lattice formulation for fourth and higher moments due to the breaking of the rotational invariance into the discrete hypercubic subgroup H‚ÄčūĚźĽ4H. This is derived and discussed in detail in Ref. citebib.bib58. Thus, in principle, any PDF moment can be extracted and the whole PDF can be reconstructed. Moreover, the heavy fictitious quark suppresses long-range correlations between the currents and also removes many higher-twist contaminations (twist=dimension‚ąí-spin). The results are independent of the mass of the heavy quark, mő®subscriptūĚĎöő®mPsi, as long as it satisfies a lattice window restriction, i. e. it should be much larger than őõQCDsubscriptőõQCDLambdarm QCD, but much smaller than the ultraviolet cutoff a‚ąí1superscriptūĚĎé1a-1. In practice, this means a requirement of rather small lattice spacings. The considered heavy-light current is defined as: Jő®,Ōąőľ‚Äč(x)=ő®¬Į‚Äč(x)‚Äčőďőľ‚ÄčŌą‚Äč(x)+Ōą¬Į‚Äč(x)‚Äčőďőľ‚Äčő®‚Äč(x),superscriptsubscriptūĚźĹő®ūĚúďūĚúáūĚĎ•¬Įő®ūĚĎ•superscriptőďūĚúáūĚúďūĚĎ•¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎ•superscriptőďūĚúáő®ūĚĎ•JPsi,psimu(x)=overlinePsi(x)Gammamupsi(x)+overlinepsi(x)GammamuPsi(x),, with Ōą‚Äč(x)ūĚúďūĚĎ•psi(x) denoting the light quark field and ő®‚Äč(x)ő®ūĚĎ•Psi(x) the fictitious heavy quark field. The Dirac structure, őďőľsuperscriptőďūĚúáGammamu can be general and is typically chosen according to the desired final observable. The Euclidean Compton scattering tensor is then constructed: Tő®,Ōąőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(p,q)‚Č°‚ąĎS‚ü®p,S|tő®,Ōąőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚Äč(q)|p,S‚ü©=‚ąĎS‚ąęd4‚Äčx‚Äčei‚Äčq‚čÖx‚Äč‚ü®p,S|T‚Äč[Jő®,Ōąőľ‚Äč(x)‚ÄčJő®,ŌąőĹ‚Äč]|p,S‚ü©,subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúąő®ūĚúďūĚĎĚūĚĎěsubscriptūĚĎÜquantum-operator-productūĚĎĚūĚĎÜsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ°ūĚúáūĚúąő®ūĚúďūĚĎěūĚĎĚūĚĎÜsubscriptūĚĎÜsuperscriptūĚĎĎ4ūĚĎ•superscripte‚čÖūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎ•quantum-operator-productūĚĎĚūĚĎÜūĚĎádelimited-superscriptsubscriptūĚźĹő®ūĚúďūĚúáūĚĎ•superscriptsubscriptūĚźĹő®ūĚúďūĚúą0ūĚĎĚūĚĎÜTmunuPsi,psi(p,q)equivsumS p,S|hskip 0.85355pttmunuPsi,psi(q)|p,S=sumSint d4x eiqcdot x p,S|Tleft[JPsi,psimu(x)JPsi,psinuright]|p,S,, where |p,S‚ü©ketūĚĎĚūĚĎÜ|p,S are hadron states with momentum pūĚĎĚp and spin SūĚĎÜS and the spins are summed over. The momentum transfer qūĚĎěq between the hadron and the scattered lepton in a DIS process should be smaller or at most of the order of the heavy quark mass. Furthermore, the momenta should satisfy the constraint (pM+qM)2<(mő®+őõQCD)2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎÄsubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎÄ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎöő®subscriptőõQCD2(pM+qM)2<(mPsi+Lambdarm QCD)2, where the momenta with subscript MūĚĎÄM are the Minkowski counterparts of the Euclidean ones. If this condition is satisfied, analytic continuation of the hadronic tensor to Euclidean spacetime is straightforward and can be achieved by relating q4subscriptūĚĎě4q4 to i‚Äčq0ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎě0iq0. Expanding this tensor, in the continuum, using OPE, one can relate it to moments of PDFs. On the lattice, one needs to compute four-point functions to access the Compton tensor for PDFs. Analogous procedure may be applied to compute distribution amplitudes, for which the hadronic interpolating operator needs to be applied only once to the vacuum state and hence a computation of only three-point functions is required. Numerical exploration, in the quenched approximation, is in progress citebib.bib68, aimed at extracting the moments of the pion DA. Since the matching to OPE has to be performed in the continuum, at least three values of the lattice spacing need to be employed for a reliable extrapolation. Preliminary results are presented in Sec. demonstrating the feasibility of this method.
