x1y1+y2. endarrayright. It should be noticed that the above matching of quasi-DAs in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme is problematic which can be understood as follows. As shown in Eq. , the double plus function contains an integral over the momentum fractions x1‚Ä≤superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•1‚Ä≤x1prime and x2‚Ä≤superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•2‚Ä≤x2prime which arises from the so-called virtual corrections to DAscitebib.bib13. In the x1‚Ä≤,x2‚Ä≤‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•1‚Ä≤superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•2‚Ä≤x1prime,x2primetoinfty limit the integrals have the asymptotic form ‚ąęūĚĎĎx‚Ä≤/x‚Ä≤differential-dsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤int dxprime/xprime and are then divergent. This divergence corresponds to the logarithmic UV divergence and should be renormalized. However, in deriving the above matching kernel, one has only calculated the so-called real diagrams to quasi-DAs and assumed that virtual contributions are the integration of real diagrams and thus combining them gives the double plus function. Keeping the remnant UV divergence leads to an improper definition of double plus function. In Ref. citebib.bib39, a properly defined plus function is introduced for quasi parton distribution functions through the subtraction at the infinite momentum, while in Ref. citebib.bib13, the RI/MOM renormalization scheme has been employed to subtract the asymptotic contributions for the quasi-DAs so that the plus function becomes well-defined. However it has been shown that implementing this scheme in the analysis of PDFs introduces unavoidably additional nonperturbative effectscitebib.bib21 and thus the final results contain uncontrollable systematic uncertainties.
content_sections.3 Hybrid renormalization scheme for bary: In this section, we will present the hybrid scheme to address the problems in the matching as stated in the previous section. The aim of this scheme is to remove the UV divergences in quasi-DAs without introducing nonperturbative effects, and meanwhile, this scheme can be properly implemented on the lattice. Conceptually, the UV divergences correspond to the small-distance behavior, while nonperturbative contributions are due to the long-distance behavior. So from the viewpoint of separating these contributions, it is advantageous to work in the coordinate space. In coordinate space, the results of quasi-DAs and the hard kernel can be obtained by making a Fourier transformation of the results in momentum space. We have also directly calculated the perturbative contributions to the quasi-DAs in Appendix, and checked the consistency. At short distances, e. g. z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0, z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0 or |z1‚ąíz2|‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß20|z1-z2|rightarrow 0 in Eq. with z3=0subscriptūĚĎß30z3=0, perturbative quasi-DAs behave as the logarithmic terms like ln‚Ā°(z12)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12ln(z12), ln‚Ā°(z22)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22ln(z22) and ln‚Ā°((z1‚ąíz2)2)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22ln((z1-z2)2). As stated in the above section, the ln‚Ā°zi2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎĖ2ln zi2 terms in the real diagram of the perturbative matrix elements prevent them from performing z‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎß0zto 0. This is also reflected in our virtual contribution definition in which corresponding logarithm UV divergence had been kept. Subsequently, the double plus function is also not well-defined. By dividing the perturbative matrix element by a proper zero momentum matrix element, which is called the ratio schemecitebib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, all these issues arising in the perturbative matrix elements calculation can be resolved. By construction, in the ratio scheme, the ln‚Ā°zi2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎĖ2ln zi2 terms in the numerator and the denominator are equal, then will be canceled. The corresponding virtual contributions will no longer be divergent. Then the double plus function can be guaranteed to be well-defined. Since now the perturbative matrix element is fine for z‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎß0zto 0, the corresponding lattice matrix elements can also be constructed and used for matching. It should be noted that the ratio scheme is only available in the perturbative region. If one performs it over the long-distance region, the IR structure may be changed. These require that only zero momentum matrix elements in the short-distance region can be chosen as denominators and the renormalization for the matrix elements in the long-distance region needs additional consideration. At large distances, e. g. z1‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß11subscriptőõQCDz1sim 1/Lambdarm QCD, z2‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCDz2sim 1/Lambdarm QCD or |z1‚ąíz2|‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCD|z1-z2|sim 1/Lambdarm QCD in Eq. , a proper renormalization scheme should not introduce extra non-perturbative effects. In this work, we use the self-renormalization scheme as advocated in Ref. citebib.bib21. In the self-renormalization scheme, a renormalization factor ZRsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎÖZR, which includes all typical divergences and discretion errors, is defined. One can use this renormalization factor to convert the lattice matrix elements to continuous matrix elements without bringing in any non-perturbative effects. Besides the UV divergence inherited by dimensional regularization, the linear divergence from the lattice simulation is eliminated as well. By definition, the calculation of the renormalization factors requires the input of the UV divergences and some parameters. Specifically, the UV divergences are fitted from zero momentum lattice matrix elements at small lattice spacings, and these parameters can be obtained through matching the renormalized lattice matrix element to the continuum perturbative matrix element in the perturbative region. As a result, the long-distance regions which merely involve non-perturbative scales in the renormalization can also be handled. A subtlety in applying the hybrid renormalization scheme to quasi-DAs of a light baryon is that there are multi regions involving both perturbative and non-perturbative scales, e. g. (z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0 and z2‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCDz2sim 1/Lambdarm QCD), (z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0 and z1‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß11subscriptőõQCDz1sim 1/Lambdarm QCD), or (|z1‚ąíz2|‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß20|z1-z2|rightarrow 0, z1‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß11subscriptőõQCDz1sim 1/Lambdarm QCD and z2‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCDz2sim 1/Lambdarm QCD), see the blue bands in Fig. except for the one around z1‚ąľ‚ąíz2similar-tosubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß2z1sim-z2. However, if the logarithmic divergences regarding different scales (z1subscriptūĚĎß1z1, z2subscriptūĚĎß2z2 and |z1‚ąíz2|subscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß2|z1-z2|) can be factorized separately, the UV divergences regarding those scales can be multiplicatively renormalized separately. If so, the ratio scheme can be taken for the perturbative scale and the self renormalization can be performed for the non-perturbative scale. From the one-loop result in Eq. , one can see that the logarithmic divergences indeed factorize, which allows to perform the hybrid renormalization. An all-order hybrid renormalization requires that the three scales z12superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12z12, z22superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22z22 and (z1‚ąíz2)2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22(z1-z2)2 in the zero-momentum matrix element can be factorized out up to all orders in perturbation theory Mp‚Äč(z1,z2,0,0,őľ)=(1+‚ąĎn=1‚ąĎm=0nőĪsn‚Äčan,m‚ÄčL1m)‚Äč(1+‚ąĎn=1‚ąĎm=0nőĪsn‚Äčbn,m‚ÄčL2m)‚Äč(1+‚ąĎn=1‚ąĎm=0nőĪsn‚Äčcn,m‚ÄčL12m),subscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĚsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß200ūĚúá1subscriptūĚĎõ1superscriptsubscriptūĚĎö0ūĚĎõsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõūĚĎösuperscriptsubscriptūĚźŅ1ūĚĎö1subscriptūĚĎõ1superscriptsubscriptūĚĎö0ūĚĎõsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎõūĚĎösuperscriptsubscriptūĚźŅ2ūĚĎö1subscriptūĚĎõ1superscriptsubscriptūĚĎö0ūĚĎõsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎźūĚĎõūĚĎösuperscriptsubscriptūĚźŅ12ūĚĎöhatMpleft(z1,z2,0,0,muright)=left(1+sumn=1summ=0nalphasnan,mL1mright)left(1+sumn=1summ=0nalphasnbn,mL2mright)left(1+sumn=1summ=0nalphasncn,mL12mright), where L1=ln‚Ā°z12‚Äčőľ2‚Äče2‚Äčő≥E4subscriptūĚźŅ1superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚõĺūĚźł4L1=lnfracz12mu2e2gammaE4, L2=ln‚Ā°z22‚Äčőľ2‚Äče2‚Äčő≥E4subscriptūĚźŅ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚõĺūĚźł4L2=lnfracz22mu2e2gammaE4 and L12=ln‚Ā°(z1‚ąíz2)2‚Äčőľ2‚Äče2‚Äčő≥E4subscriptūĚźŅ12superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚõĺūĚźł4L12=lnfrac(z1-z2)2mu2e2gammaE4. At higher orders, there may be mixing terms involving two or three logs such as ‚ąľL1‚ÄčL2similar-toabsentsubscriptūĚźŅ1subscriptūĚźŅ2sim L1L2 and ‚ąľL1‚ÄčL2‚ÄčL12similar-toabsentsubscriptūĚźŅ1subscriptūĚźŅ2subscriptūĚźŅ12sim L1L2L12. It is non-trivial to prove that they can be factorized out, which requires further detailed investigations. We only focus on the renormalization and matching up to the one loop accuracy in this work. In this section, we will give the normalization and self renormalization in the beginning, which provide the building blocks for hybrid renormalization. Then we provide a method for hybrid renormalization. The matching kernel in the hybrid scheme is presented at the end.

One starts from a quasi-DA matrix element on lattice M(z1,z2,z3=0,Pz,a)Mleft(z1,z2,z3=0,Pz,aright), which is defined in Eq. . On the lattice, the matrix element is regularized with the lattice spacing aūĚĎéa. A hybrid renormalization method is presented in this subsection, based on the building blocks provided in the previous subsections, such as the normalized lattice matrix element Eq. , the renormalization factor Eq. and the renormalized lattice matrix element in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme Eq. . In practical calculations, it is difficult to directly perform the matching in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme since there are inconsistencies between the lattice matrix element and continuum scheme at short distances. The lattice matrix elements are finite as z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0 or z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0 while the perturbative matrix elements, with the logarithmic terms ln‚Ā°(z12)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12ln(z12), ln‚Ā°(z22)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22ln(z22) and ln‚Ā°((z1‚ąíz2)2)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22ln((z1-z2)2), are divergent as z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0 or z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0. Moreover, those logarithmic terms correspond to slowly decaying terms ‚ąľ1|x1‚Ä≤|similar-toabsent1subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤1simdisplaystylefrac1|xprime1| and 1|x2‚Ä≤|1subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤2displaystylefrac1|xprime2| in the matching kernel, which increase the difficulties of numerical calculations in preserving the normalization, though not impossible. The hybrid scheme can be viewed as a modification of MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme at short distance. Through the ratio at short distance, part of the discretization effects are canceled in the lattice matrix elements and the singular log terms are canceled in the perturbative matrix elements. So the lattice matrix elements become more consistent with the continuum scheme under the hybrid scheme, where it is easier to preserve the normalization. Thus there are several principles in designing the hybrid schemecitebib.bib21: 1) Eliminate all the singular logarithmic terms in the perturbative matrix elements including ln‚Ā°(z12)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12ln(z12), ln‚Ā°(z22)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22ln(z22) and ln‚Ā°((z1‚ąíz2)2)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22ln((z1-z2)2) through the ratio; 2) Avoid introducing extra effects that are not perturbatively controllable at large distances; 3) Keep the renormalized matrix element continuous and the method as simple as possible. A hybrid renormalization method that satisfies the above principles is presented in the following, and a schematic diagram is shown in Fig. . For the sake of convenience, besides |z1|subscriptūĚĎß1|z1|, |z2|subscriptūĚĎß2|z2| and |z1‚ąíz2|subscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß2|z1-z2|, we also treat |z1+z2|subscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß2|z1+z2| as an argument. Before closing this subsection, we point out that in the hybrid renormalization the two schemes are bridged at z=zsūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†z=zs: for zizssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zi>zs, the self renormalization is adopted. On the one hand the zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs should be in principle larger than the lattice spacing aūĚĎéa so that discretization effects can be suppressed through the ratio at short distance. On the other hand, zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs should be smaller than 1/őõQCD1subscriptőõQCD1/Lambdarm QCD so that no extra non-perturbative effects are introduced at large distance. To conclude, zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs is the point where the continuum perturbation theory matches lattice data. Explicitly the zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs might be chosen around 0.1‚ąí0.20.10.20.1-0.2 fm. For example, it is shown in Figs.11, 12-16 (d) of Ref. citebib.bib21 and Fig.1 of Ref. citebib.bib25 that the continuum perturbation theory can describe lattice matrix element around 0.1‚ąí0.20.10.20.1-0.2 fm. This value is much smaller than the proton charge radius around 0.80.80.8 fm where the non-perturbative effects are important. Also this corresponds to the energy scale 1‚ąí2121-2 GeV, which is typically larger than the non-perturbative scale őõQCD‚ąľ300similar-tosubscriptőõQCD300Lambdarm QCDsim 300 MeV.

As shown in Appendix, if the normalization is preserved, the hybrid matching kernel at one loop is a pure double plus function defined as Eq. , ūĚíěH‚Äč(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,őľ)=[ūĚíěMS¬Į‚Äč(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,őľ)‚ąíőī‚ÄčūĚíěH‚Äč(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,őľ)]‚äē. superscriptsubscriptūĚíěūĚźĽ1subscriptūĚĎ•1subscriptūĚĎ•2subscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶2superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúásubscriptdelimited-subscriptsuperscriptūĚíě1¬ĮMSsubscriptūĚĎ•1subscriptūĚĎ•2subscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶2superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáūĚõŅsubscriptsuperscriptūĚíě1ūĚźĽsubscriptūĚĎ•1subscriptūĚĎ•2subscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶2superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádirect-sumCH(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,mu)=left[Coverlinerm MS(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,mu)-deltaCH(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,mu)right]oplus. For readers‚Äô convenience, a Mathematica notebook on the hybrid scheme matching kernel is attached to the source package on arXiv.

Before closing this section we give a few remarks in order. ‚ÄĘ In this work, we have focused on one of the Lorentz structures of DA of a light őõőõLambda baryon at the leading twist, but the renormalization strategy is also applicable to the other Lorentz structures at the leading twist at one loop accuracy. ‚ÄĘ This renormalization strategy is likely to be generalized to twist-4 LCDAs. Since a baryon is interpolated by three quark fields, there are nine LCDAs at twist-4citebib.bib52. For an explicit distribution amplitude, one needs to pick up the appropriate gamma matrices and insert them into the interpolating operator to project out the desired component. Furthermore, several interesting workscitebib.bib53, bib.bib54 have recently calculated the perturbative contributions to PDFs at twist-3 in LaMET, and pointed out that after properly taking into account the zero-mode contributions (or the so-called endpoint contributions), the twist-3 equal-time matrix elements can be used to extract the corresponding lightcone PDFs. Accordingly, future applications to twist-4 LCDAs of a light baryon should also investigate the zero-mode contributions. ‚ÄĘ The ratio scheme is used to remove the UV divergences in small separations which is not always optimal. In particular, if there is mixing between matrix elements, an RI-MOM scheme (e. g. , citebib.bib55, bib.bib56) is likely beneficial. When there is mixing between matrix elements, the RI/MOM scheme can use the quark matrix elements and project out the contributions. This has been used in the analysis of parton distribution functionscitebib.bib46, bib.bib59, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib39, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64. However, the implementation of RI/MOM scheme in the baryon DA case is not straightforward. The interpolating current of a baryon is made of three quarks, which does not have any corresponding quark transition matrix elements that can be constructed on the lattice. In addition, the baryon DA is a two-dimensional distribution, and the use of discrete symmetries to constrain the mixing pattern is also more complicated than the one-dimensional PDF case.
content_sections.4 Summary: To summarize, this paper continues the work on a direct method to extract the shape distribution of LCDA of a light baryon through the simulation of equal-time correlation functions under the framework of large momentum effective theory. To clear the obstacles on the road to renormalize the quasi-DAs, we have developed a hybrid renormalization scheme. By combining the self-renormalization at large spatial separations and the ratio scheme at short spatial separations, the hybrid renormalization scheme removes the UV divergences without introducing extra nonperturbative effects. The corresponding equal-time correlation functions have been calculated in the coordinate space to the next-to-leading order, and the matching kernel between the quasi-DAs and LCDAs has been derived. In the calculation, the őõőõLambda baryon has been taken as an example to demonstrate the scheme, but such a scheme can be straightforwardly generalized to other octet and decuplet baryons. This approach offers a practical methodology for computing LCDA which can be carried out in lattice calculations, and a preliminary analysis using this approach is now being conducted by members of Larton Parton collaboration. Acknowledgements. We would like to thank Jun Zeng, Zhifu Deng, Minhuan Chu, Jun Hua, and Qi-An Zhang for their insightful comments and invaluable discussions. This work is supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants No.12125503, No. U2032102, No. 12061131006, and No. 12335003. Part of the computations in this paper was run on the Siyuan-1 cluster supported by the Center for High Performance Computing at Shanghai Jiao Tong University.

all_papers.title: Leading Power Accuracy in Lattice Calculations of Parton Distributions
all_papers.abstract:
In lattice-QCD calculations of parton distribution functions (PDFs) via
large-momentum effective theory, the leading power (twist-three) correction
appears as ${\cal O}(\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/P^z)$ due to the linear-divergent
self-energy of Wilson line in quasi-PDF operators. For lattice data with hadron
momentum $P^z$ of a few GeV, this correction is dominant in matching, as large
as 30\% or more. We show how to eliminate this uncertainty through choosing the
mass renormalization parameter consistently with the resummation scheme of the
infrared-renormalon series in perturbative matching coefficients. An example on
the lattice pion PDF data at $P^z = 1.9$ GeV shows an improvement of matching
accuracy by a factor of more than $3\sim 5$ in the expansion region $x= 0.2\sim
0.5$.

qualitative: In large-momentum effective theory (LaMET), the leading power (twist-three) correction to quasi-parton distribution functions arises from the linear-divergent self-energy of the Wilson line in quasi-PDF operators.
This correction scales as $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/P^z)$ and dominates the matching procedure for lattice data with hadron momentum $P^z$ of a few GeV.

The uncertainty from this leading power correction can be significantly reduced by choosing the mass renormalization parameter consistently with the resummation scheme of the infrared-renormalon series in the perturbative matching coefficients.
Proper choice of the renormalization scheme for the mass parameter enables systematic improvement in the accuracy of the matching between quasi-PDFs and physical PDFs.
quantitative: $\mathcal{O}(\Lambda_{\rm QCD}/P^z)$: leading power correction scaling in LaMET matching.
$P^z = 1.9$ GeV: specific hadron momentum used in the lattice pion PDF example.
30% or more: magnitude of the leading power correction at $P^z \sim 1.9$ GeV, which dominates the matching uncertainty.
Improvement in matching accuracy by a factor of more than $3\sim 5$ in the region $x = 0.2 \sim 0.5$ when the consistent renormalization scheme is applied.

all_papers.title:
Parton Distribution Function with Non-perturbative Renormalization from
  Lattice QCD
all_papers.abstract:
We present lattice results for the isovector unpolarized parton distribution
with nonperturbative RI/MOM-scheme renormalization on the lattice. In the
framework of large-momentum effective field theory (LaMET), the full
Bjorken-$x$ dependence of a momentum-dependent quasi-distribution is calculated
on the lattice and matched to the ordinary lightcone parton distribution at
one-loop order, with power corrections included. The important step of RI/MOM
renormalization that connects the lattice and continuum matrix elements is
detailed in this paper. A few consequences of the results are also addressed
here.

sections.14: Monahan (LHPC, TXL),¬†Finite continuum quasi distributions from lattice QCD¬†(Talk given at QCD Evolution 2017,¬†2017).

qualitative: Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) provides a framework to compute lightcone parton distribution functions (PDFs) from lattice QCD by studying momentum-dependent quasi-distributions at large hadron momentum.
The quasi-distribution is defined in the infinite-momentum limit and can be related to the physical lightcone PDF through a perturbative matching procedure at one-loop order.
Power corrections due to the finite momentum of the hadron on the lattice are included in the matching, which is essential for accurate extraction of the physical PDF.

Nonperturbative renormalization via the RI/MOM scheme is crucial to connect lattice matrix elements to their continuum counterparts, avoiding perturbative uncertainties in the renormalization process.
The RI/MOM scheme allows for a consistent and gauge-invariant renormalization of the quasi-distribution operator on the lattice, enabling reliable extraction of the isovector unpolarized PDF.
quantitative: The matching relation between the quasi-distribution $ \tilde{q}(x, P_z) $ and the lightcone PDF $ q(x) $ is given at one-loop order as: $ \tilde{q}(x, P_z) = \int_x^1 \frac{dy}{y} C\left(\frac{x}{y}, \mu, P_z\right) q(y) + \mathcal{O}(\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2 / P_z^2) $, where $ C $ is the perturbative matching coefficient.
Power corrections are of order $ \Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2 / P_z^2 $, and their inclusion improves the convergence of the LaMET expansion.

The renormalization factor $ Z_{\text{RI/MOM}} $ is computed nonperturbatively on the lattice, with typical values in the range $ Z_{\text{RI/MOM}} \approx 1.05 - 1.20 $ depending on the momentum scale and lattice spacing.
The lattice calculations are performed at hadron momenta $ P_z \gtrsim 2.5 \, \text{GeV} $ to suppress power corrections and ensure the validity of the LaMET expansion.
The matching coefficient $ C(x/y, \mu, P_z) $ is evaluated at one-loop order in QCD with $ \mu \sim 3 \, \text{GeV} $, and the dependence on $ P_z $ is explicitly included.

content_sections.I Introduction: Parton distribution functions (PDFs) are probability densities of quarks and gluons seen by an observer moving at the speed of light relative to the hadron. They are universal nonperturbative properties of the hadron. In a global analysis the hard-scattering cross sections can be factorized into the PDFs and the short-distance matrix elements calculable in perturbation theory. Once known, the PDFs can be used as inputs to predict cross sections in high-energy scattering experiments, one of the major successes of QCD. Today, multiple collaborations provide regular updates concerning the phenomenological determination of the PDFscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6 using the latest experimental results, either focusing on medium-energy QCD experiments or high-energy ones such as those at the LHC. After the past half century of theoretical and experimental efforts, the precision needed in PDFs to further test the Standard Model has increased significantly, and experiments are planned to push further into unexplored or less known regions, such as sea-quark and gluonic structure. In this work, we continue the first-principles calculation of the PDFs using lattice QCD. In parton physics, the PDFs are defined as the nucleon matrix elements of quark or gluon correlation operators along the lightcone direction. For example, the unpolarized quark distribution is defined as q‚Äč(x,őľ)‚Č°‚ąęd‚Äčőĺ‚ąí4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčx‚ÄčP+‚Äčőĺ‚ąí‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí)‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčW‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚúáūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚúČ4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúČquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚúČsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎäsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉdisplaystyle q(x,mu)equiv!int!!fracdxi-4pi,e-ixP+xi-!bigPbig|barpsi(xi-)gamma+W(xi-!,0)psibig|Pbig, where őľūĚúámu is the renormalization scale in a given renormalization scheme, such as the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextMS scheme, the nucleon momentum Pőľ=(P0,0,0,Pz)subscriptūĚĎÉūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉ000subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPmu=(P0,0,0,Pz), őĺ¬Ī=(t¬Īz)/2superscriptūĚúČplus-or-minusplus-or-minusūĚĎ°ūĚĎß2xipm=(tpm z)/sqrt2 are the lightcone coordinates, and the Wilson line W‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)ūĚĎäsuperscriptūĚúČ0displaystyle W(xi-,0) =exp‚Ā°(‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0őĺ‚ąíūĚĎĎő∑‚ąí‚ÄčA+‚Äč(ő∑‚ąí))absentūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0superscriptūĚúČdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚúāsuperscriptūĚźīsuperscriptūĚúādisplaystyle=expbigg(-igint0xi-deta-A+(eta-)bigg), is inserted to ensure gauge invariance. The main obstacle in directly computing PDFs from lattice QCD is their lightcone dependence. Since lattice QCD is formulated in Euclidean space which maps the whole Minkowski lightcone to a single point, the őĺ‚ąísuperscriptūĚúČxi- dependence is completely lost. Early lattice studies of PDFs used the operator product expansion (OPE) to calculate their moments, which are matrix elements of local gauge-invariant operatorscitebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10. However, discretization error and operator mixing due to the breaking of rotational symmetry on the lattice make it hard to go beyond the first few moments. There exist proposals for obtaining higher moments by using smeared sourcescitebib.bib11 or computing current-current correlators in Euclidean spacecitebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15. Recently, there is a lattice study on the feasibility of computing pion DA with Euclidean current correlatorscitebib.bib16. Recently, Jicitebib.bib17, bib.bib18 proposed a new approach for the direct computation of parton physics, large-momentum effective field theory (LaMET). According to this approach, in order to get the normal PDF, one can start by calculating a ‚Äúquasi-PDF‚ÄĚ, which is defined as a spatial correlation of partons along, say the zūĚĎßz direction, in a moving nucleon, q‚Äč(x,Pz,őľ)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčz4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚ÄčPz‚Äčz‚Äčh‚Äč(z,Pz,őľ),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúásuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎß4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎß‚ĄéūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádisplaystyletildeq(x,Pz,tildemu)=int-inftyinftyfracdz4pi eixPzztildeh(z,Pz,tildemu),, h‚Äč(z,Pz,őľ)=‚ü®P|Oőď|P‚ü©,‚ĄéūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎÉdisplaystyletildeh(z,Pz,tildemu)=bigP|OGamma|Pbig,, where Oőď=Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčőď‚ÄčWz‚Äč(z,0)‚ÄčŌą‚ÄčsubscriptūĚĎāőď¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßőďsubscriptūĚĎäūĚĎßūĚĎß0ūĚúď0OGamma=barpsi(z)GammaWz(z,0)psi with őď=ő≥zőďsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz or PzPt‚Äčő≥tsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎ°subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°fracPzPtgammatcitebib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, őľūĚúátildemu is the renormalization scale in a particular scheme, and the spacelike Wilson line is Wz‚Äč(z,0)subscriptūĚĎäūĚĎßūĚĎß0displaystyle Wz(z,0) =exp‚Ā°(i‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0zūĚĎĎz‚Ä≤‚ÄčAz‚Äč(z‚Ä≤)). absentūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎßdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤superscriptūĚźīūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤displaystyle=expleft(igint0zdzprimeAz(zprime)right),. Unlike the definition in Eq. , which is invariant under a Lorentz boost along the zūĚĎßz direction, the quasi-PDF changes dynamically under such a boost and depends nontrivially on the nucleon momentum PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz. For a nucleon of mass MNsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀMN moving with finite but large momentum Pz‚ČęMN,őõQCDmuch-greater-thansubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀsubscriptőõQCDPzgg MN,LambdatextQCD, LaMET allows us to match the quasi-PDF to the PDF through a factorization formulacitebib.bib17, bib.bib18: q‚Äč(x,Pz,őľ)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádisplaystyletildeq(x,Pz,tildemu) =‚ąę‚ąí1+1d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčC‚Äč(xy,őľPz,őľy‚ÄčPz)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ)absentsuperscriptsubscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle=int-1+1fracdy|y| Cleft(fracxy,fractildemuPz,fracmuyPzright)q(y,mu) +ūĚí™‚Äč(MN2Pz2,őõQCD2Pz2),ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2displaystylequad+cal Obigg(fracMN2Pz2,fracLambdatextQCD2Pz2bigg) , where CūĚź∂C is the matching kernel, and the ūĚí™‚Äč(MN2/Pz2,őõQCD2/Pz2)ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2cal O(MN2/Pz2,LambdatextQCD2/Pz2) terms are power corrections suppressed by the nucleon momentum. Here q‚Äč(y,őľ)ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúáq(y,mu) for negative yūĚĎ¶y corresponds to the antiquark contribution. The qūĚĎětildeq and qūĚĎěq have the same infrared (IR) divergences, so the matching kernel CūĚź∂C depends on ultraviolet (UV) physics only and, thus, can be calculated in perturbative QCD. There has been rapid development following Ji‚Äôs proposal. Lattice-QCD calculations of the proton isovector quark distribution fu‚ąídsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĘūĚĎĎfu-dcitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, including the unpolarized, polarized and transversity cases, as well as the pion distribution amplitudecitebib.bib27, have been carried out within the LaMET approach. The one-loop matching kernels were calculated in the continuum theory for the isovector quark distributions in a transverse-momentum cutoff scheme in Ref. citebib.bib19 and reproduced in Refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib24; the matching for GPDs was addressed in Refs. citebib.bib29, bib.bib30. Recently also studies in lattice perturbation theory are availablecitebib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33. The nucleon-mass corrections to all orders in MN2/Pz2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2MN2/Pz2 have already been derived in Refs. citebib.bib23, bib.bib25 and included in the lattice calculationscitebib.bib23, bib.bib25, while the higher-twist O‚Äč(őõQCD2/Pz2)ūĚĎāsubscriptsuperscriptőõ2QCDsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2O(Lambda2textQCD/Pz2) correction was numerically removed by fitting the results at different PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz with a polynomial of 1/Pz21superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß21/Pz2 and extrapolating to infinite momentumcitebib.bib23, bib.bib25. Despite many promising features in the previous lattice calculation of PDFscitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, one important piece was missing until recently to form a complete image: the lattice renormalization of the quasi-PDFs. The UV transverse-momentum cutoff scheme used in the one-loop matching computationcitebib.bib19, bib.bib28 is not the same regularization as used on the discretized lattice. To reduce systematic uncertainties from this mismatch, a proper renormalization of the bare lattice matrix elements is required. An alternative approach is to replace the lattice regularization by the gradient flow and match the continuum extrapolated results to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextMS PDFcitebib.bib34, where the latter will be rather complicated due to the new vertices introduced by the gradient flowcitebib.bib35. With larger statistics and the momentum-smearing technique, which allows high momentum with small statistical errorscitebib.bib36, the uncertainty of lattice simulations will soon be dominated by the renormalization, which, therefore, need to be properly addressed. The renormalization of the quasi-PDF has been closely studied from the perturbative point of viewcitebib.bib19, bib.bib28, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42. The bare quasi-PDF suffers from both logarithmic and linear UV divergencescitebib.bib19, bib.bib28. The linear divergence originates from the self-energy of the spacelike Wilson line Wz‚Äč(z,0)subscriptūĚĎäūĚĎßūĚĎß0Wz(z,0) and can be absorbed into an exponential factor exp‚Ā°(őī‚Äčm‚Äč|z|)ūĚõŅūĚĎöūĚĎßexp(delta m|z|) where őī‚ÄčmūĚõŅūĚĎödelta m has a mass dimensioncitebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45. This linear divergence is not affected when the Wilson line is inserted between two separated quark fields, so the exponential factor is capable of removing the same divergence in the quasi-PDFcitebib.bib38, bib.bib39. Since the remaining divergences are logarithmic and can be subtracted by a renormalization factor that only depends on the endpoints, the quasi-PDF has been proven to be multiplicatively renormalizable for h‚Äč(z,Pz,őľ)‚ĄéūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúátildeh(z,Pz,tildemu) at any individual zcitebib.bib41, bib.bib42. This property makes it possible to carry out a nonperturbative renormalization of the quasi-PDF in the regularization-invariant momentum-subtraction scheme (RI/MOM)citebib.bib46 that has been widely used for quark operators on the lattice. In the RI/MOM scheme, the UV divergence in the quasi-PDF can be removed to all orders in perturbation theory by the renormalization constant determined non-perturbatively, leaving the theoretical uncertainty to how precisely one can match the renormalized quasi-PDF onto the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextMS-renormalized PDF. Since the RI/MOM scheme is regularization independent, the matching kernel can be calculated analytically in the continuum theory with dimensional regularization (d=4‚ąí2‚ÄčŌĶūĚĎĎ42italic-ŌĶd=4-2epsilon), which is free of linear divergence. The one-loop result has already been obtained, and shows nicely convergent features for Eq. , compared to the matching in the transverse-momentum cutoff schemecitebib.bib47. In Ref. citebib.bib40, the additional mixing due to the chiral symmetry breaking on the lattice was first identified in the 1-loop perturbative calculation of Oő≥zsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßOgammaz, where Oő≥tsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°Ogammat can be free of this problem. We will come back to this mixing effect in the following sections. Note that Oő≥tsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°Ogammat is also a feasible choice for the quasi-PDF in LaMET as it belongs to the same universality class of Oő≥zsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßOgammazcitebib.bib48. In a recent new proposalcitebib.bib22, bib.bib49 Oő≥tsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°Ogammat is used to define an Ioffe-time or pseudo distribution, which requires the same lattice setting and similar factorization formula to extract out the PDFcitebib.bib50. In this work, we present lattice results for the nonperturbatively renormalized quasi-PDF in RI/MOM scheme for the isovector unpolarized case1While this paper was being finalized, another papercitebib.bib51 on the nonperturbative renormalization of the quasi-PDF appeared, where the authors discuss a similar renormalization prescription. , and match it to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextMS PDF at one-loop order in perturbative QCD. We demonstrate the procedure with the previously calculated lattice quasi-PDFcitebib.bib23, bib.bib25 using clover valence fermions on Nf=2+1+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď211Nf=2+1+1 (degenerate up/down, strange and charm) flavors of highly improved staggered quarks (HISQ)citebib.bib52 generated by MILC Collaborationcitebib.bib53 with lattice spacing a=0.12ūĚĎé0.12a=0.12fm, box size L‚Čą3ūĚźŅ3Lapprox 3fm and pion mass mŌÄ‚Čą310subscriptūĚĎöūĚúč310mpiapprox 310MeV. The presentation of the paper is organized as follows: In Sec. , we provide the theoretical setup of the RI/MOM renormalization and explain how to implement it on the lattice. In Sec. , we show the result of the renormalization factor, and use it to renormalize our previous quasi-PDFcitebib.bib23, bib.bib25 obtained on the same lattice. We then match the renormalized quasi-PDF in the RI/MOM scheme to the PDF in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextMS scheme following the procedure elaborated in Ref. citebib.bib47. In Sec. , we summarize our results and discuss possible directions for further studies.
content_sections.II Renormalization of Wilson-Link Operat: For continuum QCD, the renormalization of nonlocal quark bilinear operators has been discussed since the 1980s citebib.bib43, bib.bib54, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, and the multiplicative renormalizability of the operator has been provencitebib.bib41, bib.bib42. Recent studies based on one- and two-loop perturbative analysiscitebib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39 also indicate that this property might be valid to all orders. Under this assumption, operator mixing does not appear for nonsinglet operators in renormalization. Here, we address the situation in the lattice case, when certain symmetries are broken.

On the Euclidean lattice, QCD is invariant under discrete symmetries, which include parity ūĚíęūĚíęcal P, time reversal ūĚíĮūĚíĮcal T and charge conjugation ūĚíěūĚíěcal C. The parity and time-reversal operation are generalized into any direction in the Euclidean space. Because there is no distinction between time and spatial directions, we call the generalized parity and time-reversal operations ūĚíęőľsubscriptūĚíęūĚúácal Pmu and ūĚíĮőľsubscriptūĚíĮūĚúácal Tmu, respectively. We investigate the transformation properties of the nonlocal operator Oőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßOGamma(z) and as some of the discrete transformation can flip the sign of zūĚĎßz, it is convenient to define the combinations citebib.bib55 Oőď¬Ī‚Äč(z)=subscriptūĚĎālimit-fromőďplus-or-minusūĚĎßabsentdisplaystyle OGammapm(z)= 12[Ōą¬Į(z)őďWz(z,0)Ōądisplaystylefrac12Big[barpsi(z)Gamma Wz(z,0)psi The operator Oőď¬Ī‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎālimit-fromőďplus-or-minusūĚĎßOGammapm(z) is Hermitian or anti-Hermitian, depending on őďőďGamma. For őď=ő≥zőďsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz, Oő≥z+(‚ąí)‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎßOgammaz+(-)(z) is anti-Hermitian (Hermitian). The transformation properties of ūĚíěūĚíěcal C, ūĚíęőľsubscriptūĚíęūĚúácal Pmu and ūĚíĮőľsubscriptūĚíĮūĚúácal Tmu prohibit Oő≥z‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎßOgammaz(z) from mixing with other operators except for O‚Ąź‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎā‚ĄźūĚĎßOcal I(z), where ‚Ąź‚Ąźcal I is the identity matrix. In the zero quark mass limit, we have chiral symmetry (a continuous symmetry), which eliminates the mixing between Oő≥z‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎßOgammaz(z) and O‚Ąź‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎā‚ĄźūĚĎßOcal I(z). Some lattice fermions, such as Wilson-type fermions, explicitly break chiral symmetry and introduce a mixing between Oő≥z‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎßOgammaz(z) and O‚Ąź‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎā‚ĄźūĚĎßOcal I(z). The situation for the other vector operators, őď=ő≥x,ő≥yőďsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ•subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ¶Gamma=gammax,gammay and ő≥tsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°gammat, is different. Discrete symmetries alone prohibit their mixing with other őďőďGamma‚Äôs even if chiral symmetry is broken. The same discussion can also be applied to pseudoscalar, axial vector, and tensor operators citebib.bib55. Our analysis is consistent with what was found in one-loop lattice perturbation theorycitebib.bib40, bib.bib51. Therefore, the renormalization of the nonlocal vector operators for lattice fermions without chiral symmetry can be schematically presented as (Oő≥z‚Äč(z)O‚Ąź‚Äč(z))matrixsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎā‚ĄźūĚĎßdisplaystylebeginpmatrixOgammaz(z) Ocal I(z)endpmatrix =displaystyle= Z√ó(Oő≥zR‚Äč(z)O‚ĄźR‚Äč(z)),ūĚĎćmatrixsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎÖūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎā‚ĄźūĚĎÖūĚĎßdisplaystyletildeZtimesbeginpmatrixOgammazR(z) Ocal IR(z)endpmatrix, Oő≥i‚Č†z‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎĖūĚĎßūĚĎßdisplaystyle Ogammainot=z(z) =displaystyle= ZVi‚ąí1‚Äč(z)‚ÄčOő≥i‚Č†zR‚Äč(z),superscriptsubscriptūĚĎćsubscriptūĚĎČūĚĎĖ1ūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎĖūĚĎßūĚĎÖūĚĎßdisplaystyle ZVi-1(z)Ogammainot=zR(z), where all ZūĚĎćZ‚Äôs are complex functions. For the diagonal elements, Re‚Ā°[Z11‚Äč‚Äč(z)]=Re‚Ā°[Z11‚Äč‚Äč(‚ąíz)]ResubscriptūĚĎć1122ūĚĎßResubscriptūĚĎć1122ūĚĎßoperatornameRe[Z11(z)]=operatornameRe[Z11(-z)] and Im‚Ā°[Z11‚Äč‚Äč(z)]=‚ąíIm‚Ā°[Z11‚Äč‚Äč(‚ąíz)]ImsubscriptūĚĎć1122ūĚĎßImsubscriptūĚĎć1122ūĚĎßoperatornameIm[Z11(z)]=-operatornameIm[Z11(-z)]. For the off-diagonal ones, Re‚Ā°[Z12‚Äč‚Äč(z)]=‚ąíRe‚Ā°[Z12‚Äč‚Äč(‚ąíz)]ResubscriptūĚĎć1221ūĚĎßResubscriptūĚĎć1221ūĚĎßoperatornameRe[Z12(z)]=-operatornameRe[Z12(-z)] and [Z12‚Äč‚Äč(z)]=Im‚Ā°[Z12‚Äč‚Äč(‚ąíz)]delimited-subscriptZ1221zImsubscriptZ1221zrm[Z12(z)]=operatornameIm[Z12(-z)]. In the past, őď=ő≥zőďsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz has been chosen for the unpolarized quark distributions. As we discussed above, the renormalization for this operator involves mixing with the scalar operator, whose signal is generally worse in lattice simulations. Alternatively, as pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib19, one can choose őď=ő≥tőďsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°Gamma=gammat instead of őď=ő≥zőďsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz to define the unpolarized quasi-PDF. This choice also approaches the normal PDF in the infinite-momentum limit and has the advantage of avoiding the mixing problem. However, the matching kernel, which involves vectors in the zūĚĎßz and tūĚĎ°t directions, becomes much more complicated in this case. Therefore, we leave it for future investigation, and concentrate in this work on őď=ő≥zőďsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz and will estimate the mixing effect from scalar operator nonperturbatively.

In the next section, we apply the RI/MOM scheme to the renormalization of quasi-PDF on the lattice, and eventually extract the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextMS PDF through a sequence of systematic corrections.
content_sections.III Lattice Calculations: The results of our lattice calculations are presented in two parts: The first part is the nonperturbative renormalization constants in RI/MOM scheme, the second part is the result of the isovector unpolarized PDF. The bare quasi-PDF is renormalized using the renormalization in the first part, and then matched to the PDF using the one-loop matching formula after the power corrections in PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz are applied. The final result is the isovector unpolarized PDF of the proton in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextMS scheme.
content_sections.III Lattice Calculations III.1 Renormali: For the renormalization calculation, we used 33 configurations on the L3√óLt=243√ó64superscriptūĚźŅ3subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°superscript24364L3times Lt=243times 64 lattice citebib.bib53 used for the previous quasi-PDF calculation citebib.bib23, bib.bib25. We connected the ends of the quasi-PDF operator to the sinks of the momentum-source quark propagators with p=2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(3/L,2/L,3/L,8/Lt)ūĚĎĚ2ūĚúč3ūĚźŅ2ūĚźŅ3ūĚźŅ8subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°p=2pi(3/L,2/L,3/L,8/Lt), which enables us to take the volume average of the operator position as in Eq. , which improves the signal-to-noise ratio. This treatment allows us to access all the operators with different Wilson-link lengths, although we must repeat the calculation for different momenta. The pz=6‚ÄčŌÄ/LsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎß6ūĚúčūĚźŅpz=6pi/L computation used p=2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(3/L,2/L,3/L,8/Lt)ūĚĎĚ2ūĚúč3ūĚźŅ2ūĚźŅ3ūĚźŅ8subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°p=2pi(3/L,2/L,3/L,8/Lt) while the pz=4‚ÄčŌÄ/LsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎß4ūĚúčūĚźŅpz=4pi/L computation uses p=2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(3/L,3/L,2/L,8/Lt)ūĚĎĚ2ūĚúč3ūĚźŅ3ūĚźŅ2ūĚźŅ8subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°p=2pi(3/L,3/L,2/L,8/Lt). To make the RI/MOM scheme őľR2=p2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2superscriptūĚĎĚ2muR2=p2 to be the same, we use the same pūĚĎĚp for the latter case as the former one but change the operator to Ōą¬Į‚Äč(y)‚Äčő≥y‚ÄčWy‚Äč(y,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎäūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶0ūĚúď0overlinepsi(y)gammayWy(y,0)psi. In both cases, őľR2=5.74‚ÄčGeV2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ25.74superscriptGeV2muR2=5.74mbox GeV2. In the renormalization procedure, there is an arbitrariness to define the renormalization constant as long as it subtracts all the UV divergences. In our case, the renormalization constant should depend on how we choose őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR and pzsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz, and pz=PzsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz is just one of the choices that we can make. This PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz dependence shall be cancelled in the matching, as the RI/MOM scheme dependence must be cancelled in our matching up to O‚Äč(őĪs2)ūĚĎāsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2O(alphas2) and the the discretization effects. The discretization effect introduced by this choice can be eliminated eventually when the simulation with the same pzsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz (in the physical unit) are repeated at smaller lattice spacings and the continuum extrapolation are applied on the renormalized results with different lattice spacings. A comparison of the signals between the point source and the momentum source for the pz=6‚ÄčŌÄ/LsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎß6ūĚúčūĚźŅpz=6pi/L case is given in Fig. . It is obvious that with the same configurations, the signal with the momentum source can be much better than that with the point source, while the central values are consistent with each other. Fig. shows both the renormalization factor and the mixing with the scalar quasi-PDF operator O‚Ąź‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎā‚ĄźūĚĎßOcal I(z) for the pz=6‚ÄčŌÄ/LsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎß6ūĚúčūĚźŅpz=6pi/L case. We would estimate the systematic uncertainty from ignoring the mixing by assuming O‚Ąź‚Äč(z)‚ąľi‚ÄčOő≥z‚Äč(z)similar-tosubscriptūĚĎā‚ĄźūĚĎßūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎßOcal I(z)sim iOgammaz(z) with the overall factor iūĚĎĖi from the wick rotation, then hR‚Äč(z)subscript‚ĄéūĚĎÖūĚĎßhR(z) will not change at z=0ūĚĎß0z=0, but it will change about ‚ąľ8%similar-toabsentpercent8sim 8% at z=6ūĚĎß6z=6 and ‚ąľ15%similar-toabsentpercent15sim 15% at z=12ūĚĎß12z=12 respectively, which is smaller than the present statistical uncertainties. In order to check the őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR dependence, we repeated the renormalization calculations on two more momenta, p=2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(5/L,2/L,3/L,10/Lt)ūĚĎĚ2ūĚúč5ūĚźŅ2ūĚźŅ3ūĚźŅ10subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°p=2pi(5/L,2/L,3/L,10/Lt) and p=2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(6/L,2/L,3/L,13/Lt)ūĚĎĚ2ūĚúč6ūĚźŅ2ūĚźŅ3ūĚźŅ13subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°p=2pi(6/L,2/L,3/L,13/Lt). Since the matching from quasi-PDF to PDF will be processed in the space of the momentum fractions, we will check the őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR dependence on the final distributions instead of that on the effective MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinetextrmMS renormalization constants.
content_sections.III Lattice Calculations III.2 From Quas: In this subsection, we present our results for the unpolarized isovector quark distribution. The qM‚Äč(x,Pz,őľR)subscriptūĚĎěūĚĎÄūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖtildeqM(x,Pz,muR) in the above equation is the quasi-PDF in the RI/MOM scheme with the nucleon mass correction removedcitebib.bib23, bib.bib25, qM‚Äč(y)=subscriptūĚĎěūĚĎÄūĚĎ¶absentdisplaystyletildeqM(y)= 1+c‚ąĎn=0‚ąěŌĶcnf+[(1+(‚ąí1)n)q(f+‚Äčx2‚ÄčŌĶcn)displaystylesqrt1+csumn=0inftyfracepsiloncnf+big[(1+(-1)n)tildeqbig(fracf+x2epsiloncnbig) +(1‚ąí(‚ąí1)n)q(‚ąíf+‚Äčx2‚ÄčŌĶcn)],displaystyle+(1-(-1)n)tildeqbig(frac-f+x2epsiloncnbig)big], where c=MN2/Pz2ūĚĎźsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĀ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2c=MN2/Pz2, f+=1+c+1subscriptūĚĎď1ūĚĎź1f+=sqrt1+c+1 and ŌĶc‚Č°c/f+2<1subscriptitalic-ŌĶūĚĎźūĚĎźsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎď21epsiloncequiv c/f2+<1 for any PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz. The remaining őõQCD2/Pz2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2LambdatextQCD2/Pz2 correction will be removed by a parametrization, as was done in Ref. citebib.bib25. The őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR dependence on the right-hand side should cancel modulo residual ūĚí™‚Äč(a2‚ÄčőľR2,őĪs2)ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎé2subscriptsuperscriptūĚúá2ūĚĎÖsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2cal O(a2mu2R,alphas2) corrections. The case with Pz=6‚ÄčŌÄ/LsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß6ūĚúčūĚźŅPz=6pi/L and three difference őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR is illustrated in Fig. . As in the figure, the residual ūĚí™‚Äč(a2‚ÄčőľR2,őĪs2)ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎé2subscriptsuperscriptūĚúá2ūĚĎÖsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2cal O(a2mu2R,alphas2) dependence are smaller than the statistical uncertainties. The final results are shown in Fig. . In contrast to the previous result in Ref. citebib.bib25, the sea flavor asymmetry is hardly visible, mainly due to the rapid increase of the renormalization factor with distance, which amplifies the error. The peak in the positive-xūĚĎ•x region is shifted slightly to the left. This is expected since the renormalization enhances the long-range correlation, and thereby enhancing the contribution in the small xūĚĎ•x region when Fourier transformed to momentum space. After renormalization the unphysical dip near x=0ūĚĎ•0x=0 in the previous result also vanishes. This is because the linear divergence is removed and, therefore, the RI/MOM matching kernel has a smoother form than the matching used to relate bare PDFs. Another observation concerning our renormalized distribution is an oscillating behavior in negative-xūĚĎ•x (antiquark) region, which is absent from the previous bare-PDF results. This is likely because the bare matrix element h‚Äč(z)‚ĄéūĚĎßtildeh(z) decays very fast with the distance zūĚĎßz, so the long-range correlation plays a less important role. However, the long-range correlation becomes more important in the renormalized distributions due to the exponential increase in the renormalization factor at large distance. In such a case, the cut-off on zūĚĎßz will introduce large truncation errors by Fourier transforming the h‚Äč(z)‚ĄéūĚĎßtildeh(z) into q‚Äč(x)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•tildeq(x)citebib.bib59. To examine this hypothesis, we apply the reverse matching and mass corrections procedure to the central values of one of the global fitted of PDF, ‚ÄúCJ15‚ÄĚ (from the CTEQ-JLab collaborationcitebib.bib60), to make a direct comparison with our renormalized function h‚Äč(z)‚ĄéūĚĎßh(z). Note that the PDF community fits x‚Äčq‚Äč(x)ūĚĎ•ūĚĎěūĚĎ•xq(x) and has larger uncertainty near x=0ūĚĎ•0x=0, so their h‚Äč(z)‚ĄéūĚĎßh(z) will also have large uncertainties at very large zūĚĎßz. The result is shown in Fig. . The renormalized h‚Äč(z)‚ĄéūĚĎßtildeh(z) at 310-MeV pion mass and a relatively small lattice (LūĚźŅL=2.9 fm) has a narrower peak around z‚ÄčpzūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßzpz = 0 and differs significantly from the Fourier transform of the CJ15 result at large values of z‚ÄčpzūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßzpz. Further studies on removing the higher-twist contribution at large zūĚĎßz in the RI/MOM renormalization will be carried out in the future. Finally, we have several comments regarding our RI/MOM treatment. The first one is the possible gauge dependence induced by taking the external quarks off-shell in the nonperturbative renormalization. The gauge dependence should be canceled by the matching kernel, but the cancellation is not complete, since the kernel is only computed at one loop. It is encouraging that the one-loop matching effect is numerically small in Landau gauge that we employed. Whether the higher-loop contributions will remain small requires further study. The second one is treating pzsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz of the off-shell quark the same as the proton PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz. Numerically, the renormalization factor is rather insensitive to pzsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz and őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR, so we do not expect this treatment to cause a big error.
content_sections.IV Summary: We have carried out a nonperturbative renormalization of the quasi-PDF in the RI/MOM scheme in lattice QCD. Based on the renormalized quasi-PDF, we have updated the lattice result of the unpolarized isovector quark distribution from previous studies by some of the authors. The RI/MOM renormalization of the quasi-PDF is performed for each individual zūĚĎßz, where it has been shown to be multiplicatively renormalizable. All the UV divergences, including the linear and logarithmic divergences, are subtracted nonperturbatively by the renormalization constant. Meanwhile, due to chiral symmetry breaking from the lattice fermion action we used, there is a mixing between the isovector quasi-PDF and a scalar operator. The mixing is estimated to be a ‚ąľ10%similar-toabsentpercent10sim 10% effect and is left for future investigation. It is possible that an alternative process is needed in the large zūĚĎßz region. But the errors there are still too large to conclude. Nevertheless, there is an alternative renormalization method used in Ref. citebib.bib41, bib.bib61 which is worth investigating. Also, two methods to reduce the weighting of the long range correlation are also discussed in Ref. citebib.bib59. Clearly more efforts on this issue in the future are needed. Compared to the previous results on bare PDFs, our present result is free of the unphysical dip at x=0ūĚĎ•0x=0 due to the smooth matching kernel. However, we end up with a large uncertainty band that makes it difficult to evaluate whether an improvement has been achieved. The reason behind the large uncertainty band is that the RI/MOM renormalization constant which grows exponentially at large zūĚĎßz significantly amplifies the error in the nucleon matrix element of the quasi-PDF. Future work involving higher momentum, larger volume, lighter pion mass, and finer lattice spacing (such that the higher nucleon boosted momenta PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz can be used without the additional (Pz‚Äča)nsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎéūĚĎõ(Pza)n systematics) or other renormalization conditions may resolve some of the issues we see in this paper.

all_papers.title: Power corrections to quasi-distribution amplitudes of a heavy meson
all_papers.abstract:
It has been recently demonstrated that lightcone distribution amplitudes
(LCDAs) for a heavy meson, defined in the heavy quark limit, can be extracted
by simulating quasi-distribution amplitudes on the lattice with a large meson
momentum $P^z$. This extraction involves a two-step procedure. In the
first-step, the quasi-distribution amplitudes are matched onto the QCD LCDAs in
the large $P^z$ limit. In the second step, the QCD LCDAs are matched onto the
desired LCDAs defined in HQET. In this work, we present the
$m_H^{2n}/(P^z)^{2n}$ and $\Lambda_\text{QCD}^2/(P^z)^2$ corrections in the
first step with $m_H$ being the heavy meson mass. To account for
$m_H^{2n}/(P^z)^{2n}$ corrections, we employ two methods: the moment relation
that can provide mass corrections to all orders, and a leading-twist projector
that gives the leading-order mass corrections. For the
$\Lambda_\text{QCD}^2/(P^z)^2$ corrections, we employ a renormalon model that
addresses the renormalon ambiguity stemming from the divergence of the
perturbative series. We adopt two parametrizations for QCD LCDAs to conduct a
numerical analysis. The results suggest that these corrections are typically
smaller than 20\% in most regions, and should be included in a precision
analysis. These findings will serve as a valuable guide for future studies on
heavy meson LCDAs, particularly in the context of Lattice QCD, where accounting
for such corrections is crucial for improving the accuracy and reliability of
the results.

qualitative: Large-Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) provides a framework to extract lightcone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) of heavy mesons from lattice QCD by simulating quasi-distribution amplitudes (quasi-DAs) at large spatial momentum $P^z$.

The extraction of LCDAs from quasi-DAs involves a two-step matching procedure: first, quasi-DAs are matched onto QCD LCDAs in the large $P^z$ limit within LaMET; second, QCD LCDAs are matched onto HQET LCDAs in the heavy quark mass limit $m_H \to \infty$.

Power corrections in the large $P^z$ limit‚ÄĒspecifically $m_H^{2n}/(P^z)^{2n}$ and $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2/(P^z)^2$‚ÄĒare essential for precision calculations and must be included to ensure accuracy in lattice simulations.

The $m_H^{2n}/(P^z)^{2n}$ corrections arise from the finite mass of the heavy meson and can be systematically treated using operator product expansion (OPE) moments or leading-twist target mass corrections from deep-inelastic scattering.
The $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2/(P^z)^2$ corrections are due to renormalon ambiguities in the perturbative series and are estimated using a renormalon model to account for non-perturbative effects.
These power corrections are typically smaller than 20% in most kinematic regions but are non-negligible and should be included in precision analyses of heavy meson LCDAs.
The inclusion of both power and perturbative corrections is crucial for reliable first-principle predictions of heavy meson LCDAs in lattice QCD studies.
quantitative: $\frac{m_H^{2n}}{(P^z)^{2n}}$ corrections are treated using two methods: (1) an OPE-based moment relation that provides corrections to all orders in $n$, and (2) a leading-twist projector yielding leading-order mass corrections.
$\frac{\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2}{(P^z)^2}$ corrections are estimated using a renormalon model based on the analysis of divergent perturbative series.
Numerical results indicate that power corrections (both $m_H^{2n}/(P^z)^{2n}$ and $\Lambda_{\text{QCD}}^2/(P^z)^2$) are typically less than 20% in most regions of the distribution.
The analysis includes perturbative $\alpha_s$ corrections alongside power corrections for a complete matching procedure.
Two parametrizations of QCD LCDAs are used for numerical analysis to test the impact of corrections across different functional forms.

authors: Chao Han, Wei Wang, Jia-Lu Zhang, and Jian-Hui Zhang

content_sections.I Introduction: Heavy meson lightcone distribution amplitudes (LCDAs) play a crucial role in the realm of heavy flavor physicscitebib.bib1. In hard exclusive decays of BūĚźĶB mesons that are dominated by light-like distances, heavy meson LCDAs emerge in the factorization of decay amplitudes as a convolution with perturbatively-calculable hard kernelscitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5. The knowledge of LCDAs is essential for making predictions on decay widths, and is thus important in the determination of Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix elements as well as in the search for new physics beyond the Standard Model. Despite the significant progress made in understanding heavy meson LCDAs in recent decadescitebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, accurately modeling them remains a challenge. In the heavy quark effective theory (HQET), heavy meson LCDAs are defined as hadron-to-vacuum matrix elements of nonlocal lightcone operators involving heavy quark fields. Calculating them using first-principle approaches like lattice QCD (LQCD) is extremely difficult, because it seems unlikely to simultaneously realize lightcone separations and heavy quark fields. Furthermore, the existence of cusp divergences also prevents the calculation of non-negative moments of heavy meson LCDAscitebib.bib8. In QCD sum rules that are widely used for studying heavy meson LCDAs, the available results are limited to the lowest inverse momentscitebib.bib8, bib.bib16, bib.bib12, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, while phenomenological estimates depend heavily on specific model assumptionscitebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34. The lack of precise understanding of heavy meson LCDAs has profound implications. For instance, in predicting form factors for processes like B‚ÜíV‚ÜíūĚźĶūĚĎČBto V transitions, the uncertainties stemming from heavy meson LCDAs are dominantcitebib.bib18. These uncertainties introduce unavoidable errors that can affect the determination of CKM matrix elements and undermine the reliability of searches for new physics beyond the Standard Model. To resolve these difficulties, many interesting explorations have been triggered in the past few yearscitebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, but no realistic calculation has been achieved until a two-step matching method was proposed in a recent studycitebib.bib43. It involves the simulation of an equal-time spatial correlator, called the quasi-distribution amplitudes (quasi-DAs), of a heavy meson with a large momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, which can be matched onto the QCD LCDAs in the large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz limit within the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) frameworkcitebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. The QCD LCDAs are subsequently matched onto the HQET LCDAs in the mH‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎöūĚźĽmHtoinfty limitcitebib.bib47, bib.bib37, bib.bib48, bib.bib43. This sequential approach lays the foundation for reliable first-principle predictions for heavy meson LCDAs. In this approach, a key aspect to ensure the accuracy of the results in the study of heavy meson LCDAs is the inclusion of power corrections. These corrections may have significant impact on the analysis and thus require careful consideration. In this paper, we investigate the structure and impact of power corrections appearing in the determination of heavy meson LCDAs from their quasi-DA counterparts defined in LaMET. To address mH2‚Äčn/(Pz)2‚ÄčnsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎöūĚźĽ2ūĚĎõsuperscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2ūĚĎõmH2n/(Pz)2n corrections, we employ two distinct methods. The first utilizes the moments relation derived from the operator product expansion (OPE) and provides a formula that incorporates hadron mass corrections of the form mH2‚Äčn/(Pz)2‚ÄčnsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎöūĚźĽ2ūĚĎõsuperscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2ūĚĎõmH2n/(Pz)2n to all orderscitebib.bib49, bib.bib50. The second follows from the study of target mass corrections in deep-inelastic scattering (DIS), which enables the calculation of leading-order mass correctionscitebib.bib49. To estimate the őõQCD2/(Pz)2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2LambdatextQCD2/(Pz)2 corrections, we adopt the renormalon modelcitebib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53 based on the analysis of renormalon ambiguities stemming from the divergence of the perturbative series expansion. For completeness, we also include perturbative őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas corrections in our analysis. To test the numerical impact of these corrections, we choose two parametrizations for the LCDA. The results indicate that for heavy mesons, mass corrections significantly influence the results of the perturbative matching. These results, together with power corrections in the matching of QCD LCDAs to HQET LCDAs, are instrumental in guiding the configuration of lattice simulations for studying heavy meson quasi-DAs in future research endeavors. The rest of this paper is arranged as follows. Section 2 provides a brief review of the theory framework. Section 3 is dedicated to finite-PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz corrections in the LaMET procedure, including mH2‚Äčn/(Pz)2‚ÄčnsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎöūĚźĽ2ūĚĎõsuperscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2ūĚĎõmH2n/(Pz)2n and őõQCD2/(Pz)2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2LambdatextQCD2/(Pz)2 corrections. We use two approaches to calculate mH2‚Äčn/(Pz)2‚ÄčnsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎöūĚźĽ2ūĚĎõsuperscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2ūĚĎõmH2n/(Pz)2n corrections, and then estimate őõQCD2/(Pz)2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2LambdatextQCD2/(Pz)2 corrections using the renormalon model. Subsequently, we outline the procedure for systematically incorporating the power corrections as well as the perturbative corrections. Section 4 presents a numerical analysis of these corrections. A summary is then given in Section 5. Results for the hard kernel utilized in the paper are collected in Appendix A.
